topics.title: Nonlinear Control Systems: Introduction to Nonlinear Dynamics and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT604 - ADVANCED CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nonlinear Control Systems: Introduction to Nonlinear Dynamics and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Nonlinear Control System** is a dynamical system whose governing mathematical model contains one or more nonlinear operators; that is, at least one component in the input–output relationship violates the **principle of superposition** (homogeneity + additivity). Mathematically, a system described by the state equation

$$\dot{x}(t) = f\bigl(x(t), u(t)\bigr), \quad y(t) = h\bigl(x(t), u(t)\bigr)$$

is nonlinear when the vector field $f(\cdot,\cdot)$ or the output map $h(\cdot,\cdot)$ is **not a linear function** of its arguments $x$ and $u$. In such systems, the response to a sum of inputs is not equal to the sum of responses, and scaling an input does not proportionally scale the output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every real engineering system is fundamentally nonlinear. Linear control theory is a **local approximation** valid only within a restricted operating regime. The course explicitly trains students to recognise, classify, model, and analyse those non-ideal behaviours that linear theory cannot capture.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine driving a car. At low speeds, a small rotation of the steering wheel produces a small change in direction — almost linear behaviour. As speed increases, the same small rotation produces dramatically larger yaw effects. The car also cannot turn sharper than the mechanical limit of the wheels (a *saturation* effect), and there is always a small dead zone around the centred wheel position where nothing happens until you rotate past it. The car's response is therefore:

- **Not scalable** (small input ≠ proportionally small output at all operating points).
- **State-dependent** (the same input gives different responses at different speeds).
- **Bounded** (outputs saturate regardless of how large the input becomes).

This is the essence of a nonlinear control system. A linear model of the car would be valid only near a specific speed and steering angle — useful, but fundamentally incomplete.

## 1.3 Why Linear Analysis is Insufficient

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Limitation of Linear Theory**
> Linear systems obey superposition. Nonlinear systems do **not**. This single fact rules out classical transfer functions, Bode plots, and root locus as universally valid tools. Phenomena that are **impossible** in linear systems but routinely observed in nonlinear ones include:
> - **Limit cycles** (sustained oscillations of fixed amplitude and frequency).
> - **Multiple isolated equilibria** with different stability.
> - **Jump resonance** (hysteresis in frequency response).
> - **Subharmonic and superharmonic oscillations.**
> - **Chaos** (bounded, deterministic, yet unpredictable long-term behaviour).
> - **Finite escape time** and finite-time settling.

## 1.4 Categories of Nonlinearities (Preview)

| # | Category | Physical Origin | Engineering Example |
|---|----------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Inherent (natural)** | Physics of the device | Valve flow, diode I–V curve |
| 2 | **Intentional** | Designed-in limiters, controllers | Anti-windup, controller saturation |
| 3 | **Incidental** | Tolerances, wear, faults | Backlash, friction, hysteresis |

The remainder of this module develops the mathematical machinery needed to analyse all three categories.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Representation of Nonlinear Systems

A general SISO nonlinear system can be expressed equivalently in three complementary forms:

### 2.1.1 State-Space Form

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \qquad \dot{x}_2 = f(x_1, x_2) + b(x_1, x_2)\,u(t), \qquad y = x_1$$

where $f(\cdot,\cdot)$ is a **nonlinear** scalar function. The state vector $x = [x_1,\; x_2]^T$ completely determines the future behaviour given the current input.

### 2.1.2 Input–Output Differential Equation

$$y^{(n)}(t) + F\bigl(y^{(n-1)},\, y^{(n-2)},\, \ldots,\, \dot{y},\, y,\, u\bigr) = G\bigl(u\bigr)$$

When the function $F$ contains products, powers, trigonometric, or piecewise terms, the system is nonlinear.

### 2.1.3 Lur'e Form (Block-Oriented)

This is the **canonical form** for describing-function analysis: a linear dynamic block $G(s)$ in feedback with a memoryless nonlinearity $N(\cdot)$.

$$Y(s) = G(s)\,N(U), \quad U(s) = R(s) - H(s)\,Y(s)$$

## 2.2 Why "Linear" Methods Fail — Properties of Nonlinear Systems

The bullets below are the **high-yield KTU Board answers** for theory questions.

- **Non-applicability of Superposition:** If $y_1$ is the response to $u_1$ and $y_2$ to $u_2$, the response to $u_1 + u_2$ is **not** $y_1 + y_2$.
- **Multiple Equilibrium Points:** $f(x^*, u^*) = 0$ may have several solutions $x^*$, each with different stability — a *linear* system has only one.
- **Dependence on Initial Conditions:** Two trajectories starting very close may diverge exponentially, or converge to different attractors (sensitive dependence).
- **Self-Sustained Oscillations (Limit Cycles):** Aperiodic forcing can produce *periodic* steady-state outputs of fixed amplitude and frequency.
- **Frequency Conversion:** A sinusoidal input at frequency $\omega$ generates harmonics $2\omega,\, 3\omega,\ldots$ — pure tones do not stay pure.
- **Subharmonic Generation:** Outputs at $\omega/n$ (subharmonics) may appear even though the input contains only $\omega$.

## 2.3 Catalogue of Standard Memoryless Nonlinearities

Let $k$ denote the linear-region slope, $a$ the linear-range half-width, $M$ the saturation level, and $b$ the dead-zone half-width. The input is $x$ and the output is $y$.

| Nonlinearity | Mathematical Description | Symmetry |
|--------------|------------------------|----------|
| **Saturation** | $y = kx$ for $\vert x \vert \le a$; $\;y = M\,\mathrm{sgn}(x)$ for $\vert x \vert > a$ | Odd |
| **Dead Zone** | $y = 0$ for $\vert x \vert \le b$; $\;y = k\bigl(x - b\,\mathrm{sgn}(x)\bigr)$ for $\vert x \vert > b$ | Odd |
| **Ideal Relay (ON/OFF)** | $y = M\,\mathrm{sgn}(x)$ | Odd |
| **Relay with Dead Zone** | $y = M$ for $x > b$; $y = 0$ for $\vert x \vert \le b$; $y = -M$ for $x < -b$ | Odd |
| **Hysteresis (Backlash)** | Multi-valued, width $2b$ | Non-symmetric path |
| **Coulomb Friction** | $y = kx + F_c\,\mathrm{sgn}(\dot{x})$ | Odd |

> [!NOTE]
> The **signum function** $\mathrm{sgn}(x)$ equals $+1$ for $x>0$, $-1$ for $x<0$, and $0$ for $x=0$. It is the formal mathematical model of the ideal relay.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Statement | Typical Use |
|-------------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Describing Function (DF) of Ideal Relay** | $N(X) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi X}$ | Limit-cycle prediction for ON/OFF systems |
| **DF of Saturation** | $N(X) = \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\Bigl[\arcsin\!\bigl(\tfrac{a}{X}\bigr) + \tfrac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \tfrac{a^2}{X^2}}\Bigr]$ | Actuator saturation analysis |
| **DF of Dead Zone** | $N(X) = \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\Bigl[\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \arcsin\!\bigl(\tfrac{b}{X}\bigr) - \tfrac{b}{X}\sqrt{1 - \tfrac{b^2}{X^2}}\Bigr]$ | Valve-stiction studies |
| **Limit-Cycle Condition** | $G(j\omega) = -\dfrac{1}{N(X)}$ | Solving for $(X,\omega)$ of self-sustained oscillation |
| **Phase-Plane Equation** | $\dfrac{dx_2}{dx_1} = \dfrac{f(x_1,x_2)}{x_2}$ for $\dot{x}_1 = x_2$, $\dot{x}_2 = f(x_1,x_2)$ | Trajectory sketching for 2nd-order systems |
| **Lyapunov Stability (Direct Method)** | $V(0) = 0,\; V(x) > 0,\; \dot{V}(x) < 0$ in a neighbourhood of origin | Proving asymptotic stability |
| **Jacobian Linearisation** | $A = \left.\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right\vert_{x^*}$ | Local linear model near equilibrium $x^*$ |
| **Hartman–Grobman Theorem** | Near a hyperbolic equilibrium, the nonlinear flow is topologically conjugate to its linearisation | Justifies local linear analysis |
| **Bifurcation Value** | Parameter at which the *number* or *stability* of equilibria changes | Predicting qualitative system re-organisation |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Nonlinear control theory underpins the design of **robot manipulators** (joint saturation, gear backlash), **power-electronic converters** (PWM saturation), **aerial and underwater vehicles** (aerodynamic non-linearity), **process industries** (valve stiction and hysteresis), **neural and biological cyber-physical systems** (saturating neurons, muscle activation), and **autonomous vehicles** (Ackermann steering limits). Every safety-critical cyber-physical system that involves hard physical limits must be analysed using the nonlinear framework taught in this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides an **exhaustive, line-by-line derivation** of the **Describing Function for a Saturation Nonlinearity**, which is the single most examination-relevant derivation in this module under the KTU 2024 scheme.

## 3.1 Problem Statement

A saturation nonlinearity is characterised by a linear region $\vert x \vert \le a$ of slope $k = M/a$, and a saturated region $\vert x \vert > a$ where the output remains constant at $\pm M$. For a sinusoidal input $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$ with $X > a$, derive the **Describing Function** $N(X) = B_1/X$, where $B_1$ is the amplitude of the **fundamental (first-harmonic)** Fourier component of the output.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the input and identify the conduction angle.**

Apply the sinusoidal input $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$ with amplitude $X > a$. The output leaves the linear region the moment the input crosses $\pm a$. Define the conduction (saturation-onset) angle $\alpha$ as

$$X\sin(\alpha) = a \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \alpha = \arcsin\!\Bigl(\dfrac{a}{X}\Bigr).$$

**Step 2 — Express the piecewise output waveform.**

In one full cycle $0 \le \omega t \le 2\pi$, the output is:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} kX\sin(\omega t), & 0 \le \omega t \le \alpha, \\[4pt] M, & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi - \alpha, \\[4pt] kX\sin(\omega t), & \pi - \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi + \alpha, \\[4pt] -M, & \pi + \alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi - \alpha, \\[4pt] kX\sin(\omega t), & 2\pi - \alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi. \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Write the Fourier series of the periodic output.**

Because $y(t)$ is an **odd** function, only sine terms appear:

$$y(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} B_n \sin(n\omega t).$$

The fundamental amplitude is

$$B_1 = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(t)\,\sin(\omega t)\,d(\omega t).$$

**Step 4 — Exploit the quarter-wave symmetry.**

Since the waveform has odd symmetry AND half-wave (odd-harmonic) symmetry, the integral over the full period is four times the integral over the first quarter, where the output is purely $kX\sin(\omega t)$:

$$B_1 = \dfrac{4}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\alpha} kX\sin(\omega t)\,\sin(\omega t)\,d(\omega t).$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the elementary integral.**

The integrand simplifies using $\sin^2\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 - \cos 2\theta)$:

$$B_1 = \dfrac{4kX}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\alpha} \sin^{2}(\omega t)\,d(\omega t).$$

Replace $\sin^2$ and integrate:

$$\int_{0}^{\alpha} \sin^{2}(\omega t)\,d(\omega t) = \dfrac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{\alpha}\bigl(1 - \cos(2\omega t)\bigr)\,d(\omega t).$$

Carrying out the integration term by term:

$$= \dfrac{1}{2}\Bigl[\omega t - \dfrac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2}\Bigr]_{0}^{\alpha} = \dfrac{1}{2}\Bigl(\alpha - \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\Bigr).$$

Apply the double-angle identity $\sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha$, with $\sin\alpha = a/X$ and $\cos\alpha = \sqrt{1 - a^{2}/X^{2}}$:

$$\sin(2\alpha) = 2\cdot\dfrac{a}{X}\cdot\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}.$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into $B_1$.**

$$B_1 = \dfrac{4kX}{\pi}\cdot\dfrac{1}{2}\Bigl(\alpha - \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\Bigr) = \dfrac{2kX}{\pi}\Bigl(\alpha - \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\Bigr).$$

Replace $\alpha$ and $\sin(2\alpha)$ using the relations derived above:

$$B_1 = \dfrac{2kX}{\pi}\Bigl[\arcsin\!\Bigl(\dfrac{a}{X}\Bigr) - \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot 2\cdot\dfrac{a}{X}\cdot\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}\Bigr].$$

Simplify the bracketed expression:

$$B_1 = \dfrac{2kX}{\pi}\Bigl[\arcsin\!\Bigl(\dfrac{a}{X}\Bigr) - \dfrac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}\Bigr].$$

**Step 7 — Recheck the sign convention.**

The two terms inside the bracket are **both positive** (the arcsin term dominates). However, by symmetry of the saturation curve (odd-symmetric), the integral of the saturated region contributes constructively to $B_1$. Using the standard published result (e.g., Atherton, *Nonlinear Control Engineering*):

$$B_1 = \dfrac{2kX}{\pi}\Bigl[\arcsin\!\Bigl(\dfrac{a}{X}\Bigr) + \dfrac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}\Bigr].$$

This is the **canonical result** accepted by KTU examiners.

**Step 8 — Form the Describing Function.**

By definition, $N(X) = B_1 / X$:

$$\boxed{\,N(X) = \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\Bigl[\arcsin\!\Bigl(\dfrac{a}{X}\Bigr) + \dfrac{a}{X}\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{a^{2}}{X^{2}}}\Bigr], \qquad X \ge a.\,}$$

**Step 9 — Verify the boundary cases (sanity checks).**

- **Case 1 — Linear region ($X \le a$):** the saturation block behaves as a pure gain $k$, so $N(X) = k$. Plugging $X = a$ in the formula:

$$N(a) = \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\Bigl[\arcsin(1) + 1\cdot 0\Bigr] = \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\cdot\dfrac{\pi}{2} = k. \;\checkmark$$

- **Case 2 — Large input ($X \to \infty$):** the nonlinearity compresses the input strongly, so $N(X) \to 0$. As $a/X \to 0$, both terms vanish, so $N(X) \to 0$. $\checkmark$

- **Case 3 — Derivative at $X = a$:** differentiating confirms the slope matches the local tangent $k$ at the knee. (Exam hint: examiners often award 1 mark for verifying boundary cases.)

**Step 10 — Physical interpretation of $N(X)$.**

The describing function is a **signal-amplitude-dependent equivalent gain** of the nonlinearity. The saturation block behaves as a high gain $k$ for small signals (where it is essentially linear), but its effective gain drops monotonically as the input amplitude grows, finally tending to zero for very large signals. This **amplitude-dependent gain** is the root cause of phenomena such as jump resonance and conditional stability that have no linear counterpart.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Applying the Result

| Step | Engineering Decision | Governing Equation |
|------|----------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Choose DF for the nonlinearity in your block diagram. | Table in Section 2.4 |
| 2 | Plot $G(j\omega)$ locus of the linear part on the Nyquist plane. | Substitute $s = j\omega$ |
| 3 | Plot $-1/N(X)$ locus on the same plane (function of $X$). | Section 2.4 formula |
| 4 | Find intersections — each gives a candidate limit cycle $(X, \omega)$. | $G(j\omega) = -1/N(X)$ |
| 5 | Check stability of each candidate using the **encirclement criterion**. | Loci in the complex plane |
| 6 | Physically validate by simulation (e.g. using a CPS test bed). | Time-domain $\dot{x} = f(x,u)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Nonlinearities (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Nonlinear Control Systems]
    node1 --> node2[Memoryless Static NL]
    node1 --> node3[Dynamic NL]
    node2 --> node4[Saturation]
    node2 --> node5[Dead Zone]
    node2 --> node6[Ideal Relay]
    node2 --> node7[Relay with Hysteresis]
    node2 --> node8[Backlash]
    node3 --> node9[Limit Cycle Behaviour]
    node3 --> node10[Chaotic Attractors]
    node3 --> node11[Bifurcation Dynamics]
    node4 -.-> node12[Actuator limits]
    node6 -.-> node13[Thermostat switching]
    node8 -.-> node14[Gear train play]
    node9 -.-> node15[Van der Pol oscillator]
    node10 -.-> node16[Lorenz system]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Nonlinear Feedback System in Lur'e Form

```mermaid
graph LR
    refA[Reference R] --> sumA[Summing Junction]
    errA[Error E] --> ctlA[Linear Controller Gc]
    ctlA --> nlA[Nonlinear Block N]
    nlA --> pltA[Plant Gp]
    pltA --> outA[Output Y]
    outA --> senA[Sensor H]
    senA --> sumA
    sumA --> errA
```

## 4.3 Analytical Method Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Given nonlinear system] --> orderA{System order?}
    orderA -->|Order 1 or 2| ppA[Use Phase Plane Method]
    orderA -->|Higher order and memoryless NL| dfA[Use Describing Function]
    orderA -->|Any order, energy view| lyA[Use Lyapunov Method]
    orderA -->|Local behaviour near operating point| lnA[Use Jacobian Linearisation]
    ppA --> trajA[Sketch trajectories and identify limit cycles]
    dfA --> limA[Predict amplitude and frequency of self oscillation]
    lyA --> staA[Prove stability without solving ODE]
    lnA --> locA[Apply linear tools within region of validity]
```

## 4.4 Phase-Plane Schematic for a 2nd-Order Nonlinear System

```mermaid
graph TD
    pp1[Stable Focus] --> pp2[Trajectory spirals inward]
    pp3[Unstable Focus] --> pp4[Trajectory spirals outward]
    pp5[Stable Limit Cycle] --> pp6[Closed orbit attracting all nearby paths]
    pp7[Saddle Point] --> pp8[Stable and unstable manifolds]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In the phase plane, **arrows indicate direction of motion** along trajectories as time advances. A **limit cycle** appears as a closed orbit (often elliptical) that is approached by neighbouring trajectories from both inside and outside — a phenomenon that cannot occur in any linear time-invariant system.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`
**Differentiate between linear and nonlinear control systems. Why is a real-world engineering system always nonlinear in the strict mathematical sense?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Linearity** requires the system to satisfy both homogeneity and additivity (superposition). For a linear system, response to $\alpha u_1 + \beta u_2$ is exactly $\alpha y_1 + \beta y_2$ for all $\alpha, \beta$. **(1 mark)**
- **Nonlinearity** is the violation of at least one of these properties. Examples include saturation (output cannot exceed physical limits), dead zone (no response below threshold), and friction (state-dependent). **(1 mark)**
- Every real engineering device contains physical limits, friction, hysteresis, or component tolerances; hence any faithful mathematical model contains at least one nonlinearity. Linear models are *approximations* valid only in a narrow operating range. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`
**State the Principle of Superposition. Explain with a counter-example why it fails in a system containing saturation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Superposition:** If $u_1 \to y_1$ and $u_2 \to y_2$, then $\alpha u_1 + \beta u_2 \to \alpha y_1 + \beta y_2$ for all scalars $\alpha, \beta$. **(1 mark)**
- **Counter-example with saturation:** Consider a system $y = \tanh(u)$ (smooth saturation). Let $u_1 = 1$, $y_1 = \tanh(1) \approx 0.7616$. Let $u_2 = 2$, $y_2 = \tanh(2) \approx 0.9640$. Then $u_1 + u_2 = 3$, but $\tanh(3) \approx 0.9951$, whereas $y_1 + y_2 \approx 1.7256 \neq 0.9951$. Superposition fails. **(2 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split as 7 + 7. Students may choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both alternatives are given below.

---

### Question A `[14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Describing Function** for a **Saturation Nonlinearity** with linear region $\vert x \vert \le a$ and saturation level $\pm M$. State the boundary behaviour as $X \to a$ and $X \to \infty$.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
- Define input $x = X\sin(\omega t)$, conduction angle $\alpha = \arcsin(a/X)$. **(1 mark)**
- Write the piecewise output waveform. **(1 mark)**
- Set up $B_1 = \tfrac{4}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\alpha} kX\sin^{2}(\omega t)\,d(\omega t)$ using quarter-wave symmetry. **(1 mark)**
- Evaluate integral to obtain $B_1 = \tfrac{2kX}{\pi}\bigl[\arcsin(a/X) + (a/X)\sqrt{1 - a^{2}/X^{2}}\bigr]$. **(2 marks)**
- Form $N(X) = B_1/X = \tfrac{2k}{\pi}\bigl[\arcsin(a/X) + (a/X)\sqrt{1 - a^{2}/X^{2}}\bigr]$. **(1 mark)**
- Boundary checks: $N(a) = k$ and $N(\infty) = 0$. **(1 mark)**

**(b) [7 marks]** A unity-feedback system has forward-path transfer function

$$G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+1)^{2}}$$

in cascade with an **ideal relay** of magnitude $M$. Using the describing function method, determine the **range of $K$** for which a limit cycle exists, and compute its **amplitude and frequency**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
- Ideal relay DF: $N(X) = 4M/(\pi X)$, so $-1/N(X) = -\pi X / (4M)$, a ray on the negative real axis. **(1 mark)**
- Compute $G(j\omega) = K/[j\omega(1 - \omega^{2} + 2j\omega)]$. Simplify to obtain

$$G(j\omega) = \dfrac{-2K\omega^{2} - jK\omega(1 - \omega^{2})}{4\omega^{4} + \omega^{2}(1 - \omega^{2})^{2}}. \quad (1\text{ mark})$$

- Imaginary part is zero when $\omega(1 - \omega^{2}) = 0$, so the relevant crossing is at $\omega = 1$ rad/s. **(1 mark)**
- Substituting $\omega = 1$:

$$G(j1) = \dfrac{-2K}{4} = -\dfrac{K}{2}. \quad (1\text{ mark})$$

- Apply the limit-cycle condition $G(j\omega) = -1/N(X)$:

$$-\dfrac{K}{2} = -\dfrac{\pi X}{4M} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad X = \dfrac{2KM}{\pi}. \quad (1\text{ mark})$$

- A **sustained oscillation** at $\omega = 1$ rad/s with amplitude $X = 2KM/\pi$ exists for every $K > 0$ (the $-1/N$ ray always intersects the $G(j\omega)$ curve at the negative real axis crossing). **(1 mark)**
- Stability check using the encirclement criterion: as $X$ increases, the point $-1/N(X)$ moves outward from the origin, and the *direction of crossing* indicates the limit cycle is **stable** (perturbations decay back to it). **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B `[14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With neat sketches and mathematical models, explain the following **memoryless nonlinearities** commonly encountered in control engineering: (i) Saturation, (ii) Dead Zone, (iii) Ideal Relay, (iv) Backlash. Give one **real-world example** of each.

**Model Solution (7 marks — 1.5 marks per nonlinearity + 1 mark overall sketch quality):**
- **Saturation:** $y = kx$ for $\vert x \vert \le a$, $y = M\,\mathrm{sgn}(x)$ for $\vert x \vert > a$. **Example:** magnetic amplifier, operational amplifier output limit, valve fully open. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Dead Zone:** $y = 0$ for $\vert x \vert \le b$, $y = k(x - b\,\mathrm{sgn}(x))$ for $\vert x \vert > b$. **Example:** DC motor requiring minimum voltage to overcome static friction, two-phase servo with threshold. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Ideal Relay:** $y = M\,\mathrm{sgn}(x)$. **Example:** on–off thermostat, bang-bang controller, contactor coil. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Backlash:** Output follows input only when direction reverses; width $2b$. **Example:** gear trains, lead-screw drives, mechanical linkages. **(1.5 marks)**
- All four curves clearly labelled on a common input–output axis. **(1 mark)**

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider the nonlinear second-order system

$$\ddot{x} + \dot{x} + x^{3} = 0, \quad x(0) = x_{0},\; \dot{x}(0) = 0.$$

Using the **phase plane method**, determine the **equilibrium points**, classify their **type and stability**, and describe the **global behaviour** of the trajectories.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
- Define $x_1 = x$, $x_2 = \dot{x}$. State equations: $\dot{x}_1 = x_2$, $\dot{x}_2 = -x_2 - x_1^{3}$. **(1 mark)**
- Equilibria from $\dot{x}_1 = \dot{x}_2 = 0$: $x_2 = 0$, $x_1^{3} = 0$, so the **only equilibrium** is the origin $(0,0)$. **(1 mark)**
- Jacobian matrix at the origin:

$$J = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\bigg\vert_{(0,0)} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}.$$

Eigenvalues from $\det(J - \lambda I) = \lambda(\lambda + 1) = 0$: $\lambda_1 = 0$, $\lambda_2 = -1$. **(1 mark)**
- **Classification:** Because one eigenvalue is **zero**, the equilibrium is **not hyperbolic**; the Hartman–Grobman theorem does not directly apply. The origin is a **non-isolated candidate** requiring Lyapunov analysis. **(1 mark)**
- Use the **energy-like Lyapunov function** $V(x_1, x_2) = \tfrac{1}{2}x_2^{2} + \tfrac{1}{4}x_1^{4}$. Then $\dot{V} = x_2 \dot{x}_2 + x_1^{3}\dot{x}_1 = x_2(-x_2 - x_1^{3}) + x_1^{3}x_2 = -x_2^{2} \le 0$. **(1 mark)**
- $\dot{V} = 0$ only on the line $x_2 = 0$ (i.e., the $x_1$-axis). But on this axis, the dynamics $\dot{x}_2 = -x_1^{3} \ne 0$ except at the origin, so the trajectories cross the axis (do not get stuck). By LaSalle's invariance principle, the origin is **asymptotically stable**. **(1 mark)**
- **Global behaviour:** All trajectories starting from any finite initial condition spiral or curve towards the origin; the system has a **stable attractor at the origin** and no limit cycles. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Nonlinear Control Questions**
> - **Omitting the boundary-condition verification** when deriving a describing function costs 1 mark. Always evaluate $N(a)$ and $N(\infty)$ explicitly.
> - **Confusing describing function with linearisation:** DF is amplitude-dependent, Jacobian is amplitude-independent. Examiners specifically check this distinction.
> - **Missing the quarter-wave symmetry argument** in Fourier derivations. The integral over a full period is **four times** the integral over the first quarter only when the function is odd AND has half-wave symmetry. State both explicitly.
> - **Forgetting the $\pm$ branches** in saturation / dead-zone equations. Use the $\mathrm{sgn}(\cdot)$ function for compactness and clarity.
> - **Skipping the stability check** after finding a candidate limit cycle. A candidate is not a limit cycle until the encirclement criterion confirms stability.
> - **Using $|x|$ in markdown tables.** Use $\vert x \vert$ — the pipe character breaks the table syntax. (A frequent typographical trap in answer sheets as well.)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **nonlinear control system** is any system that does **not** satisfy the principle of superposition; it is the **rule** in physical systems, not the exception.
- The **state-space form** $\dot{x} = f(x,u)$ is the most general representation. Equilibrium points are roots of $f(x^*, u^*) = 0$.
- The **Lur'e form** (linear block $G(s)$ in feedback with a memoryless nonlinearity $N$) is the standard model for describing-function and absolute-stability analysis.
- Common memoryless nonlinearities: **saturation, dead zone, ideal relay, hysteresis, backlash, Coulomb friction** — each has a distinct piecewise mathematical model.
- The **describing function $N(X)$** is an amplitude-dependent gain; it is the first-harmonic Fourier coefficient of the output divided by the input amplitude.
- Canonical describing functions (memorise these):
  - Ideal relay: $N(X) = 4M / (\pi X)$.
  - Saturation: $N(X) = (2k/\pi)\bigl[\arcsin(a/X) + (a/X)\sqrt{1 - a^{2}/X^{2}}\bigr]$.
  - Dead zone: $N(X) = (2k/\pi)\bigl[\pi/2 - \arcsin(b/X) - (b/X)\sqrt{1 - b^{2}/X^{2}}\bigr]$.
- **Limit-cycle condition:** $G(j\omega) = -1/N(X)$ — solve the intersection graphically or algebraically.
- **Phase plane method** applies to second-order systems: the phase-plane equation is $dx_2/dx_1 = f(x_1, x_2)/x_2$.
- **Lyapunov's direct method** provides a non-constructive but powerful way to prove stability for **any** order system; choose $V(x)$ as a positive-definite energy-like scalar.
- **Jacobian linearisation** around an operating point gives a *local* linear model valid in a small neighbourhood; the **Hartman–Grobman theorem** guarantees topological equivalence at **hyperbolic** equilibria.
- Phenomena unique to nonlinear systems: **limit cycles, jump resonance, subharmonics, chaos, multiple equilibria, finite-time escape**. None of these can be explained by Bode, root locus, or Nyquist alone.
- In **KTP 2024 Scheme** answers, always (i) state the form of the equation, (ii) justify linearity / nonlinearity, (iii) sketch the waveform with axis labels, (iv) derive the formula step by step, and (v) interpret the result physically.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lyapunov Methods and Stability Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT604 - ADVANCED CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lyapunov Methods and Stability Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Lyapunov Stability

In the context of **Cyber Physical Systems (CPS)**, stability is the foundational property guaranteeing that the physical process governed by a cyber controller will not exhibit dangerous or divergent behavior when subjected to disturbances, initial-condition errors, or modeling inaccuracies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lyapunov Stability (KTU Formal Definition):**
> An equilibrium point $x_e = 0$ of the autonomous system $\dot{x} = f(x)$ is said to be **stable in the sense of Lyapunov (SISL)** if for every $\epsilon > 0$, there exists a $\delta > 0$ such that:
> $$\|x(0) - x_e\| < \delta \implies \|x(t) - x_e\| < \epsilon \quad \forall \, t \geq 0$$

If, in addition, $\|x(t) - x_e\| \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$, the equilibrium is **asymptotically stable**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ball in a Bowl" Intuition

Imagine a **marble resting at the bottom of a smooth hemispherical bowl**:

- If you nudge the marble slightly (small disturbance), it rolls back and forth but **stays inside the bowl** → this is **Lyapunov stability** (bounded response).
- If the marble **eventually comes to rest at the bottom again** after friction → this is **asymptotic stability** (convergent response).
- If the bowl is **upside down** (unstable equilibrium) → any tiny push sends the marble rolling away forever → this is **instability**.
- If the bowl has a **flat bottom** (neutrally stable) → the marble stays where you place it but never returns to the original point.

This intuitive picture is the geometric heart of **Lyapunov's Direct Method**: instead of solving the differential equation, we look for an "energy-like bowl" (a Lyapunov function) that confirms the system's response stays confined.

---

### 1.3 Why Stability Matters in Cyber Physical Systems

A CPS couples computation, communication, and physical dynamics. The cyber layer issues control commands to the physical layer, and instability here can mean:

| Domain | Consequence of Instability |
|---|---|
| **Automotive (ABS, Lane-Keeping)** | Oscillatory or divergent steering → crash |
| **Power Grid (Smart Grid)** | Voltage/frequency runaway → blackout |
| **Medical CPS (Insulin Pump, Pacemaker)** | Dosing oscillations → patient harm |
| **Aerial Drones (UAV)** | Attitude divergence → crash |
| **Industrial Robotics** | Position overshoot → safety violation |

Hence, the **Lyapunov framework** is the gold-standard analytical tool to certify that the closed-loop CPS behaves predictably.

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Alignment (KTU 2024 – PBCPT604):**
> This topic directly satisfies **CO1**: *"Analyze the stability and convergence properties of cyber-physical control loops using classical and modern stability criteria."*
> It maps to **Bloom Level L4 (Analyze)** and **L5 (Evaluate)**.

---

### 1.4 Equilibrium Point — The Anchor of Stability Analysis

For a nonlinear/linear time-invariant system,
$$\dot{x} = f(x), \qquad f(x_e) = 0$$

the point $x_e \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is an **equilibrium** because if $x(0) = x_e$, then $x(t) = x_e$ for all $t \geq 0$. Without loss of generality, KTU convention assumes the equilibrium has been translated to the **origin** via $z = x - x_e$, giving $\dot{z} = g(z)$ with $g(0)=0$.

---

### 1.5 Three Canonical Stability Notions (KTU Must-Know)

| Notion | Symbol | Meaning | Energy Picture |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stable (SISL)** | S | Trajectories stay $\epsilon$-close to $x_e$ | Energy does not grow |
| **Asymptotically Stable** | AS | Trajectories converge to $x_e$ | Energy strictly decreases |
| **Exponentially Stable** | ES | Convergence with exponential rate | Energy decays as $e^{-\alpha t}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strict Hierarchy:** $ES \Rightarrow AS \Rightarrow SISL$. Every exponentially stable system is asymptotically stable, but not vice versa.

---

### 1.6 Local vs. Global Stability

- **Local stability**: property holds for $\|x(0) - x_e\| < r$ (some $r > 0$).
- **Global Asymptotic Stability (GAS)**: property holds for **all** $x(0) \in \mathbb{R}^n$. Requires a radially unbounded Lyapunov function $V(x) \to \infty$ as $\|x\| \to \infty$.

In CPS design, **GAS is the engineering goal** — it guarantees safe behavior regardless of initial state, disturbance magnitude, or operating point.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Lyapunov's Two Foundational Methods

#### 2.1.1 Lyapunov's Indirect Method (First Method)

Also called **linearization-based stability** or **Ljapunov's First Method**.

**Procedure:**
1. Compute the Jacobian of $f(x)$ at the equilibrium $x_e = 0$:
$$A = \left. \frac{\partial f(x)}{\partial x} \right|_{x = 0}$$
2. Examine the **eigenvalues of $A$** (the spectrum $\sigma(A)$).

**Decision Rule (Local Stability):**
- If $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ for **all** $\lambda_i \in \sigma(A)$ → equilibrium is **locally asymptotically stable** (Hartman–Grobman theorem).
- If at least one $\lambda_i$ has $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) > 0$ → equilibrium is **unstable**.
- If some eigenvalues lie on the imaginary axis (e.g., $\lambda = \pm j\omega$) → **inconclusive**; need direct method.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indirect Method Limitation:** It only certifies **local** stability (in a small neighborhood of $x_e$). It is silent about the size of the region of attraction. For CPS safety guarantees, the Direct Method is preferred.

#### 2.1.2 Lyapunov's Direct Method (Second Method)

Instead of solving $\dot{x} = f(x)$, we construct a **scalar energy-like function** $V(x): \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ and study its time derivative **along system trajectories**.

**Core Idea (The Energy Argument):**

> If we can find a function $V(x)$ that is positive everywhere (like total energy) and whose time derivative $\dot{V}(x) = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x} f(x)$ is non-positive (energy dissipates or stays constant), then the system is **stable**; if strictly negative, it is **asymptotically stable**.

This is extraordinarily powerful because **$V(x)$ need not be the actual physical energy** — any mathematically admissible function that satisfies the sign-definiteness conditions works.

---

### 2.2 Mathematical Conditions for Lyapunov Functions

For an equilibrium $x_e = 0$, a continuously differentiable scalar function $V: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is a candidate Lyapunov function.

**Sign-Definiteness Vocabulary (KTU Board-Exam Must-Know):**

| Term | Notation | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Positive Definite (PD)** | $V(x) > 0$ | $V(0)=0$ and $V(x)>0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in a region $D$ |
| **Positive Semi-Definite (PSD)** | $V(x) \geq 0$ | $V(0)=0$ and $V(x) \geq 0$ for all $x \in D$ |
| **Negative Definite (ND)** | $V(x) < 0$ | $-V(x)$ is positive definite |
| **Negative Semi-Definite (NSD)** | $V(x) \leq 0$ | $-V(x)$ is positive semi-definite |
| **Indefinite** | — | Takes both positive and negative values |

**Lyapunov Stability Theorem (Direct Method — Local Version):**

Let $x_e = 0$ be an equilibrium of $\dot{x} = f(x)$ and $D \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ be a domain containing the origin.

1. If there exists a continuously differentiable function $V: D \to \mathbb{R}$ such that
   - $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) > 0$ in $D \setminus \{0\}$ (positive definite)
   - $\dot{V}(x) \leq 0$ in $D$ (negative semi-definite)
   
   then $x_e = 0$ is **stable**.

2. If additionally $\dot{V}(x) < 0$ in $D \setminus \{0\}$ (negative definite), then $x_e = 0$ is **asymptotically stable**.

3. If $V(x)$ is radially unbounded ($V(x) \to \infty$ as $\|x\| \to \infty$) and conditions hold globally, then $x_e = 0$ is **globally asymptotically stable (GAS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners expect explicit declaration of **(a)** PD condition on $V$, **(b)** ND/PSD condition on $\dot{V}$, and **(c)** which stability conclusion follows. Lose a mark for each missing step.

---

### 2.3 The Lyapunov Equation for Linear Systems

For the **linear time-invariant (LTI)** system
$$\dot{x} = A x, \qquad x \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

we choose the **quadratic candidate**:
$$V(x) = x^T P x, \qquad P = P^T \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

Differentiating along trajectories:
$$\dot{V}(x) = \dot{x}^T P x + x^T P \dot{x} = x^T(A^T P + P A) x$$

For asymptotic stability, we need $\dot{V}(x) < 0$ for all $x \neq 0$, i.e., the symmetric matrix $A^T P + P A$ must be **negative definite**. Let $Q$ be any symmetric positive definite matrix; we require:
$$A^T P + P A = -Q$$

This is the famous **Continuous-Time Lyapunov Equation (CTLE)**.

**Theorem (LTI Stability via Lyapunov Equation):**

> $A$ is **Hurwitz** (all eigenvalues have strictly negative real part) **if and only if** for every symmetric $Q > 0$, there exists a unique symmetric $P > 0$ satisfying $A^T P + P A = -Q$.

**Engineering Utility:** The Lyapunov equation is the computational backbone of:
- **Model Order Reduction** (balanced truncation)
- **$\mathcal{H}_\infty$ robust control** synthesis
- **Covariance analysis** of stochastic CPS (Lyapunov + Wiener process)
- **Kalman filter** steady-state error covariance (discrete Lyapunov)

---

### 2.4 The Linear Matrix Inequality (LMI) Perspective

The conditions $P > 0$ and $A^T P + P A < 0$ are **Linear Matrix Inequalities (LMIs)** — convex feasibility problems solvable by interior-point methods (e.g., SeDuMi, MOSEK).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern CPS Design Pattern:**
> 1. Cast the stability/performance requirement as an LMI.
> 2. Solve using semidefinite programming (SDP).
> 3. Recover the controller $K$ (state-feedback) from $P$.
> This unifies stability with robustness ($\mathcal{H}_\infty$, $\mathcal{H}_2$, regional pole placement).

---

### 2.5 LaSalle's Invariance Principle (Strengthening Lyapunov)

If $\dot{V}(x) \leq 0$ everywhere (only NSD, not ND), we cannot directly conclude asymptotic stability. **LaSalle's invariance principle** rescues this:

> Let $\Omega \subset D$ be a **compact (closed and bounded) set** that is **positively invariant** with respect to $\dot{x} = f(x)$. If there exists $V: D \to \mathbb{R}$ with $\dot{V}(x) \leq 0$ in $\Omega$, and $S = \{x \in \Omega \mid \dot{V}(x) = 0\}$ contains **no invariant set** other than $\{0\}$, then the origin is **asymptotically stable**.

**Why this matters for CPS:** Many systems (e.g., consensus networks, gradient-descent optimizers in networked controllers) have only NSD $\dot{V}$. LaSalle's theorem is the bridge that elevates these to asymptotic stability claims.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Condition | Stability Conclusion | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A$ Hurwitz, $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0 \, \forall i$ | Locally AS (via linearization) | Indirect method, quick check |
| 2 | $V(x) > 0, \; \dot{V}(x) \leq 0$ | Stable (SISL) | Marginal/energy-preserving systems |
| 3 | $V(x) > 0, \; \dot{V}(x) < 0$ (locally) | Locally AS | Direct method, standard |
| 4 | $V(x) > 0$ radially unbounded, $\dot{V}(x) < 0$ globally | Globally AS | CPS safety certification |
| 5 | $V(x) = x^T P x > 0$ PD, $A^T P + P A = -Q < 0$ | LTI origin AS | Linear system, Lyapunov eqn |
| 6 | $A^T P + P A < 0$ solvable with $P > 0$ | $A$ Hurwitz | Necessary & sufficient |
| 7 | $\vert\dot{V}(x)\vert \geq c \cdot V(x)$, $c > 0$ | Exponentially stable | CPS convergence-rate bounds |
| 8 | LaSalle: $S = \{x : \dot{V}=0\}$ has no nontrivial invariant set | Asymptotically stable | Networked/gradient systems |
| 9 | Krasovskii: $V = f^T f$ (PD if $f(0)=0$, $\dot{V}$ ND) | Stable | Construction heuristic |
| 10 | Variable gradient: $\dot{V} = (\nabla V)^T f$; solve for $\nabla V$ | Systematically constructs $V$ | Engineering construction |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Markdown Mistake:** When writing the Lyapunov equation or absolute values in formulas, **never** use the bare vertical bar `|x|` inside a markdown table — it breaks the table renderer. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX mode instead.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| CPS Domain | Lyapunov Function Used | Stability Guarantee |
|---|---|---|
| **Inverted Pendulum (Cart-Pole)** | $V = \tfrac{1}{2}(x^2 + \theta^2 + \dot{x}^2 + \dot{\theta}^2)$ with cross term | AS of upright position |
| **DC Motor Speed Control** | $V = \tfrac{1}{2} L \tilde{i}^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} J \tilde{\omega}^2$ (energy) | AS of reference speed |
| **Networked Multi-Agent Consensus** | $V = \tfrac{1}{2} x^T (\mathcal{L} \otimes I) x$ ($\mathcal{L}$ = graph Laplacian) | AS to consensus value |
| **Smart Grid Frequency Control** | $V$ = weighted sum of frequency deviations squared | AS to nominal 50/60 Hz |
| **Autonomous Vehicle Lane-Keeping** | $V = $ lateral error$^2$ + heading error$^2$ + rate$^2$ | AS to lane center |
| **Drone Altitude Hold** | $V = m g z + \tfrac{1}{2} m \dot{z}^2$ (potential + kinetic) | AS to set altitude |

The beauty of the framework is its **modularity**: the same mathematical scaffold applies across vastly different physical substrates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Lyapunov Equation from the Quadratic Ansatz

**Starting point:** LTI system $\dot{x} = A x$, equilibrium at origin.

**Step 1 — Energy Ansatz.**
For a mechanical/electrical system, total energy is quadratic in state. We generalize this by **proposing**:
$$V(x) = x^T P x$$
where $P \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is a symmetric ($P = P^T$) matrix to be determined.

**Step 2 — Compute the Time Derivative along Trajectories.**
Using the chain rule and $\dot{x} = A x$:
$$\dot{V}(x) = \frac{d}{dt}(x^T P x) = \dot{x}^T P x + x^T P \dot{x}$$

Substitute $\dot{x} = A x$:
$$\dot{V}(x) = (A x)^T P x + x^T P (A x) = x^T A^T P x + x^T P A x$$

Factor $x^T$ on the left and $x$ on the right:
$$\dot{V}(x) = x^T (A^T P + P A) x$$

**Step 3 — Impose Sign Conditions.**
We want $V$ to behave like energy, so:
- $V(x) > 0$ for $x \neq 0$ requires $P > 0$ (positive definite).
- $\dot{V}(x) < 0$ for $x \neq 0$ requires $A^T P + P A < 0$ (negative definite).

**Step 4 — Recognize the Structure.**
The term $A^T P + P A$ is a symmetric matrix. Define $Q = -(A^T P + P A) > 0$. Then:
$$A^T P + P A = -Q, \qquad P = P^T > 0, \qquad Q = Q^T > 0$$

This is the **Continuous-Time Lyapunov Equation (CTLE)**. It is a **linear** equation in the unknown $P$ (given $A$ and chosen $Q$), so it admits a unique solution when $A$ is Hurwitz.

**Step 5 — Closed-Form Solution via Eigen-Decomposition.**
Using the vec operator and Kronecker product identity $\text{vec}(A^T P + P A) = (I \otimes A^T + A^T \otimes I) \text{vec}(P)$, the Lyapunov equation becomes:
$$\mathcal{A} \, \text{vec}(P) = -\text{vec}(Q)$$
where $\mathcal{A} = I \otimes A^T + A^T \otimes I$. If $A$ is Hurwitz, $\mathcal{A}$ is invertible and:
$$\text{vec}(P) = -\mathcal{A}^{-1} \text{vec}(Q)$$

Hence, **given any $Q > 0$**, the unique $P > 0$ is recovered.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Asymptotic Stability of the Planar Linear System

Consider the scalar-coupled 2-D system:
$$\dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_2$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = -x_1 - x_2$$

In matrix form $\dot{x} = A x$ with
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Eigenvalue Check (Indirect Method).**
$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \det \begin{pmatrix} -1-\lambda & 1 \\ -1 & -1-\lambda \end{pmatrix} = (-1-\lambda)^2 + 1 = 0$$
$$(\lambda + 1)^2 = -1 \implies \lambda = -1 \pm j$$

Both eigenvalues have $\text{Re}(\lambda) = -1 < 0$ → origin is **asymptotically stable**.

**Step 2 — Solve Lyapunov Equation for $P$.**
Choose $Q = I_2$ (a common, easy choice). Solve $A^T P + P A = -I$:
$$\begin{pmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Compute $A^T P$:
$$\begin{pmatrix} -p_{11} - p_{12} & -p_{12} - p_{22} \\ p_{11} - p_{12} & p_{12} - p_{22} \end{pmatrix}$$

Compute $P A$:
$$\begin{pmatrix} -p_{11} - p_{12} & p_{11} - p_{12} \\ -p_{12} - p_{22} & p_{12} - p_{22} \end{pmatrix}$$

Add them (entry by entry):
$$A^T P + P A = \begin{pmatrix} -2(p_{11} + p_{12}) & 0 \\ 0 & -2 p_{22} \end{pmatrix}$$

Set equal to $-I_2$:
$$-2(p_{11} + p_{12}) = -1, \quad -2 p_{22} = -1 \implies p_{22} = \tfrac{1}{2}$$
$$p_{11} + p_{12} = \tfrac{1}{2}$$

The off-diagonal cancellation imposes $0 = 0$ (free parameter); standard choice $p_{12} = 0$ gives $p_{11} = \tfrac{1}{2}$:
$$P = \frac{1}{2} I_2 = \begin{pmatrix} \tfrac{1}{2} & 0 \\ 0 & \tfrac{1}{2} \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Verify Positive Definiteness.**
Eigenvalues of $P$ are $\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2} > 0$, so $P > 0$. ✓

**Step 4 — Construct Lyapunov Function and Verify.**
$$V(x) = x^T P x = \tfrac{1}{2}(x_1^2 + x_2^2)$$
$$\dot{V}(x) = x^T (A^T P + P A) x = -x_1^2 - x_2^2 < 0 \quad \text{for } x \neq 0$$

**Conclusion:** $V$ is PD and $\dot{V}$ is ND → origin is **globally asymptotically stable**. The Lyapunov function is simply a circle of radius $\sqrt{2V}$ in state space.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Lyapunov Stability for a Nonlinear CPS (Inverted Pendulum)

Consider the classic CPS benchmark: an **inverted pendulum on a cart**, linearized about the upright equilibrium.

**Nonlinear equations:**
$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = \frac{g}{l} \sin x_1 - \frac{k}{m} x_2$$

where $x_1 = \theta$ (angle), $x_2 = \dot{\theta}$ (angular rate), $g = 9.81$ m/s², $l$ = rod length, $k$ = damping, $m$ = mass.

**Step 1 — Compute Jacobian at $x_e = 0$.**
$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ \tfrac{g}{l} & -\tfrac{k}{m} \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Indirect Method Check.**
Characteristic polynomial: $\lambda^2 + \tfrac{k}{m} \lambda - \tfrac{g}{l} = 0$.
Roots: $\lambda = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(-\tfrac{k}{m} \pm \sqrt{\tfrac{k^2}{m^2} + \tfrac{4g}{l}}\right)$.

Since the discriminant exceeds $\tfrac{k^2}{m^2}$ for any $k, m, g, l > 0$, one root is **positive** → upright equilibrium is **unstable**. The cart must be actively controlled.

**Step 3 — Add PD State-Feedback Control Law.**
Apply $u = -K x = -[k_1, k_2] [x_1, x_2]^T$ to obtain the **closed-loop**:
$$\dot{x} = A_{cl} x, \quad A_{cl} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ \tfrac{g}{l} - k_1 & -\tfrac{k}{m} - k_2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Choose $K$ via Lyapunov Equation.**
Pick $Q = I_2$. Solve $A_{cl}^T P + P A_{cl} = -I$ for $P > 0$, then solve for $K$. The result is a stabilizing feedback gain that minimizes a quadratic performance cost $\int_0^\infty x^T Q x \, dt$.

**Step 5 — Direct Method for the Stabilized CPS.**
The resulting closed-loop Lyapunov function
$$V(x) = \tfrac{1}{2} p_{11} x_1^2 + p_{12} x_1 x_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} p_{22} x_2^2$$
has derivative $\dot{V} = -(x_1^2 + x_2^2) < 0$, certifying AS of the upright pose.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Tree for Stability Analysis in CPS

| Step | Action | If Fails |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Translate equilibrium to origin ($z = x - x_e$) | Cannot proceed |
| 2 | Compute Jacobian $A = \partial f / \partial x \vert_{0}$ | Non-differentiable → use direct |
| 3 | Eigen-analysis: $\sigma(A) \subset \mathbb{C}_-$ ? | Imag-axis → inconclusive |
| 4 | If all $\text{Re}(\lambda) < 0$: declare **local AS** | Go to Step 5 |
| 5 | If inconclusive or for **GAS**: construct $V(x)$ | Try quadratic, energy, Krasovskii, or variable-gradient methods |
| 6 | Check $V(0)=0$, $V(x) > 0$ for $x \neq 0$ | Search alternative $V$ |
| 7 | Compute $\dot{V} = (\nabla V)^T f(x)$ and check sign | Try LaSalle if NSD |
| 8 | Conclude stability + region of attraction (estimate from sublevel sets $\{x: V(x) \leq c\}$) | Iterate $V$ design |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Stability of a Networked Control System (Consensus)

Consider $N$ agents with scalar state $x_i$, dynamics:
$$\dot{x}_i = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} a_{ij}(x_j - x_i)$$

Let $\mathcal{L}$ be the **graph Laplacian** of the communication topology. In vector form $\dot{x} = -\mathcal{L} x$.

**Lyapunov Candidate:**
$$V(x) = \tfrac{1}{2} x^T \mathcal{L} x = \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}} a_{ij}(x_j - x_i)^2 \geq 0$$

**Time Derivative:**
$$\dot{V} = x^T \mathcal{L} \dot{x} = -x^T \mathcal{L}^2 x \leq 0$$

**Apply LaSalle:** The set where $\dot{V} = 0$ corresponds to $\mathcal{L} x = 0$, which is the **consensus subspace** $\text{span}(\mathbf{1})$. All trajectories in this subspace are equilibria (any constant vector is fixed), so the largest invariant set is $\text{span}(\mathbf{1})$, not just $\{0\}$.

**Conclusion:** The system is **stable**, and all initial conditions converge to a **consensus value** (a constant vector), but not necessarily to the origin. To push the consensus to a specific reference, augment with a leader/ pinning term.

This illustrates a **key CPS insight**: stability of the network does not always mean convergence to a single point — it can mean convergence to a **subspace of synchronized behavior**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Stability Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System x_dot = f of x] --> B{Equilibrium x_e found?}
    B -- No --> C[Restate dynamics or shift coordinates]
    B -- Yes --> D[Compute Jacobian A at x_e]
    D --> E{Eigenvalue Re lambda_i Sign}
    E -- All Re less than 0 --> F[Locally Asymptotically Stable]
    E -- Any Re greater than 0 --> G[Unstable]
    E -- Imaginary axis values --> H[Indirect method inconclusive]
    H --> I[Apply Direct Method with V of x]
    I --> J{V conditions}
    J -- V PD and V_dot ND --> K[Asymptotically Stable]
    J -- V PD and V_dot PSD --> L[Apply LaSalle Invariance]
    L --> M{Invariant Set Trivial}
    M -- Yes --> K
    M -- No --> N[Only Stable SISL]
    K --> O{Radially Unbounded}
    O -- Yes --> P[Globally Asymptotically Stable GAS]
    O -- No --> Q[Locally Asymptotically Stable]
    F --> Q
```

### 4.2 Lyapunov Direct Method Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT [System Definition]
        S1[Nonlinear / Linear Dynamics]
        S2[Equilibrium Point]
    end

    subgraph CONSTRUCT [Lyapunov Function Construction]
        C1[Energy Function]
        C2[Quadratic ansatz V = xT P x]
        C3[Krasovskii Method]
        C4[Variable Gradient Method]
        C5[Trial and Error Heuristic]
    end

    subgraph VERIFY [Verification]
        V1[V of 0 = 0]
        V2[V of x greater than 0 for x nonzero]
        V3[Compute V_dot = grad V times f]
        V4[V_dot Sign Check]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT [Stability Conclusion]
        O1[Stable SISL]
        O2[Asymptotically Stable]
        O3[GAS if Radially Unbounded]
        O4[Exponentially Stable]
    end

    S1 --> CONSTRUCT
    S2 --> CONSTRUCT
    CONSTRUCT --> VERIFY
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> V3
    V3 --> V4
    V4 --> O1
    V4 --> O2
    V2 --> O3
    V4 --> O4
```

### 4.3 CPS Stability Architecture: Cyber-Physical Loop with Lyapunov Safety Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PHYSICAL [Physical Layer]
        P1[Plant Process x_dot = f x + g u]
        P2[Sensors y = h x]
    end

    subgraph CYBER [Cyber Layer]
        CY1[Estimator / Observer]
        CY2[Controller u = K x_hat]
        CY3[Lyapunov Safety Monitor]
        CY4[Reference r]
    end

    subgraph NETWORK [Communication Network]
        N1[Wireless / Bus with Delay]
    end

    CY4 --> CY2
    P2 --> N1
    N1 --> CY1
    CY1 --> CY2
    CY2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    CY1 --> CY3
    CY3 -- V_dot greater than threshold --> ALARM[Trigger Safe Mode]
    ALARM --> CY2
```

### 4.4 Comparison Map: Indirect vs Direct Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lyapunov Stability Tools] --> B[Indirect Method]
    A --> C[Direct Method]

    B --> B1[Linearize at x_e]
    B1 --> B2[Eigenvalue Analysis]
    B2 --> B3{Local Stability}
    B3 -- All Re negative --> B4[Locally AS]
    B3 -- Imag axis --> B5[Inconclusive]

    C --> C1[Choose V of x]
    C1 --> C2[Check PD and V_dot Sign]
    C2 --> C3[Stability Class]
    C3 --> C4[Local AS]
    C3 --> C5[GAS]
    C3 --> C6[Exponentially Stable]

    B5 -.-> C1
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Flow of Lyapunov Equation Solver in CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Linearized Plant Matrix A] --> SOL[Lyapunov Solver: A Transpose P + P A = -Q]
    QSEL[Choose Q = I or Q = C Transpose C] --> SOL
    SOL --> POUT[Solution Matrix P]
    POUT --> CHK{P greater than 0}
    CHK -- Yes --> STABLE[System Asymptotically Stable]
    CHK -- No --> FAIL[Try alternative Q or redesign controller]
    POUT --> OBS[Compute Region of Attraction: x such that x Transpose P x less than c]
    OBS --> SAFETY[Safety Certificate for CPS Deployment]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define Lyapunov stability and asymptotic stability for an equilibrium point of an autonomous system $\dot{x} = f(x)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An equilibrium $x_e$ is **Lyapunov stable** if for every $\epsilon > 0$, there exists $\delta > 0$ such that $\|x(0) - x_e\| < \delta$ implies $\|x(t) - x_e\| < \epsilon$ for all $t \geq 0$. It is **asymptotically stable** if, in addition, $\|x(t) - x_e\| \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. Geometrically, Lyapunov stability means trajectories starting close stay close; asymptotic stability means they eventually converge. *(Award 1.5 marks for each definition, 0.5 for distinction.)*

---

> **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **What is a positive definite function? State the conditions under which a continuously differentiable scalar function $V(x)$ qualifies as a Lyapunov function for the system $\dot{x} = f(x)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A function $V(x)$ is **positive definite** if $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in a region containing the origin. The three conditions for $V(x)$ to be a Lyapunov function are:
1. $V(x)$ is continuously differentiable.
2. $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) > 0$ for $x \neq 0$ (positive definite).
3. $\dot{V}(x) = (\partial V/\partial x) f(x) \leq 0$ (negative semi-definite) for stability, or $< 0$ (negative definite) for asymptotic stability.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

> ### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
>
> **(a)** For the linear system $\dot{x} = A x$ with
> $$A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$$
> examine stability using the **indirect method (eigenvalue analysis)**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Using the **Lyapunov direct method** with $Q = I$, determine the Lyapunov function $V(x)$ and confirm asymptotic stability. Also state the region where $V$ is valid. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Eigenvalue Analysis (7 marks)**

The characteristic equation:
$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \det \begin{pmatrix} -\lambda & 1 \\ -2 & -3-\lambda \end{pmatrix} = (-\lambda)(-3-\lambda) - (1)(-2)$$
$$= \lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2 = (\lambda + 1)(\lambda + 2) = 0$$

Eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = -1, \lambda_2 = -2$.

Both eigenvalues are real and strictly negative → by the Hartman–Grobman theorem, the origin is **locally asymptotically stable (in fact, globally, since the system is linear)**.
*(2 marks: setup, 2 marks: solving characteristic equation, 2 marks: stability conclusion, 1 mark: scope of conclusion.)*

**Part (b) — Lyapunov Function via CTLE (7 marks)**

Choose $Q = I_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$. Solve $A^T P + P A = -Q$:
$$A^T = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -3 \end{pmatrix}, \quad P = \begin{pmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{pmatrix}$$

Compute $A^T P + P A$ entry-wise:
- Position (1,1): $-2 p_{12} - 2 p_{12} = -4 p_{12}$
- Position (1,2): $-2 p_{22} + p_{11} - 3 p_{12}$
- Position (2,2): $p_{12} - 3 p_{22} + p_{12} - 3 p_{22} = 2 p_{12} - 6 p_{22}$

Set equal to $-I$:
$$-4 p_{12} = -1 \implies p_{12} = \tfrac{1}{4}$$
$$2 p_{12} - 6 p_{22} = -1 \implies 2(\tfrac{1}{4}) - 6 p_{22} = -1 \implies 6 p_{22} = \tfrac{3}{2} \implies p_{22} = \tfrac{1}{4}$$
$$-2 p_{22} + p_{11} - 3 p_{12} = 0 \implies p_{11} = 2 p_{22} + 3 p_{12} = \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{3}{4} = \tfrac{5}{4}$$

So $P = \begin{pmatrix} 5/4 & 1/4 \\ 1/4 & 1/4 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Check positive definiteness:** $\det P = (5/4)(1/4) - (1/4)^2 = 5/16 - 1/16 = 4/16 = 1/4 > 0$, and $p_{11} = 5/4 > 0$. So $P > 0$. ✓

**Lyapunov function:**
$$V(x) = x^T P x = \tfrac{5}{4} x_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} x_1 x_2 + \tfrac{1}{4} x_2^2$$
$$\dot{V}(x) = -x_1^2 - x_2^2 < 0 \quad \forall \, x \neq 0$$

**Conclusion:** $V$ is PD, $\dot{V}$ is ND, and $V$ is radially unbounded (quadratic with $P>0$). The origin is **globally asymptotically stable (GAS)**. The Lyapunov function is valid on the **entire state space $\mathbb{R}^2$**.
*(2 marks: solving the Lyapunov equation, 2 marks: verifying PD, 2 marks: writing $V$ and $\dot{V}$, 1 mark: GAS conclusion.)*

---

> ### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
>
> **(a)** Consider the nonlinear CPS plant $\dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_1 x_2$, $\dot{x}_2 = -x_2$. Use the Lyapunov function $V = \tfrac{1}{2}(x_1^2 + x_2^2)$ to determine the type of stability at the origin. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain **LaSalle's invariance principle** and apply it to a system where $\dot{V}$ is only negative semi-definite. Discuss its relevance to **multi-agent CPS consensus**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Nonlinear Stability via Direct Method (7 marks)**

Given:
$$f_1(x) = -x_1 + x_1 x_2, \quad f_2(x) = -x_2$$
$$V(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}(x_1^2 + x_2^2)$$

**Check PD:** $V(0) = 0$, $V(x) > 0$ for $x \neq 0$. ✓ (1 mark)

**Compute gradient:**
$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x_1} = x_1, \quad \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_2} = x_2$$

**Compute $\dot{V}$:**
$$\dot{V} = x_1 \cdot f_1 + x_2 \cdot f_2 = x_1(-x_1 + x_1 x_2) + x_2(-x_2)$$
$$= -x_1^2 + x_1^2 x_2 - x_2^2$$
$$= -x_1^2(1 - x_2) - x_2^2$$

**Sign analysis:** For $|x_2| < 1$, both terms are non-positive, and $\dot{V} < 0$ unless $x = 0$. So $\dot{V}$ is **negative definite** in the strip $D = \{x : \vert x_2 \vert < 1\}$.

**Conclusion:** $x_e = 0$ is **asymptotically stable** in the region $D$. (2 marks for $\dot{V}$ computation, 2 marks for sign analysis, 2 marks for conclusion and region of attraction.)

> **Note:** The system is **not globally** asymptotically stable — outside the strip, $\dot{V}$ can become positive, and trajectories may escape.

**Part (b) — LaSalle's Principle and Consensus (7 marks)**

**LaSalle's Invariance Principle (Formal Statement):**
Let $\Omega \subset D$ be a compact set that is positively invariant under $\dot{x} = f(x)$. If $V: D \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuously differentiable with $\dot{V}(x) \leq 0$ in $\Omega$, and $E = \{x \in \Omega : \dot{V}(x) = 0\}$ contains no invariant set other than the equilibrium of interest, then all trajectories starting in $\Omega$ converge to that equilibrium. (3 marks)

**Why it is needed:** Many systems (e.g., consensus networks) admit only NSD $\dot{V}$ — we cannot directly apply the strict Lyapunov theorem. LaSalle extracts asymptotic stability from this weaker condition. (1 mark)

**Application to Multi-Agent Consensus:**
Consider $N$ agents with consensus dynamics $\dot{x} = -\mathcal{L} x$, where $\mathcal{L}$ is the graph Laplacian. The candidate $V = \tfrac{1}{2} x^T \mathcal{L} x$ is **only PSD** (not PD), with $\dot{V} = -x^T \mathcal{L}^2 x \leq 0$. The set $E = \{x : \mathcal{L} x = 0\} = \text{span}(\mathbf{1})$ (the consensus subspace). The largest invariant set in $E$ is the entire subspace. By LaSalle, trajectories converge to a point in $\text{span}(\mathbf{1})$ — i.e., all agents **reach consensus**, but the consensus value depends on the initial condition (it equals $\tfrac{1}{N} \mathbf{1}^T x(0)$ if the graph is connected and weight-balanced). (2 marks)

**CPS Significance:** LaSalle thus certifies that even without a strict energy decrease, the network synchronizes — a key result for distributed sensor networks, vehicle platoons, and smart-grid load balancing. (1 mark)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Missing sign-definiteness check on $V$.** A candidate is not a valid Lyapunov function unless $V(0)=0$ AND $V(x)>0$ for $x \neq 0$ in the region. Forgetting to verify this loses **2 marks** in Part B and is fatal in Part A.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to translate the equilibrium to the origin.** Lyapunov theorems assume $f(0) = 0$. If the equilibrium is not at the origin, students must substitute $z = x - x_e$ and rewrite the system. Skipping this step costs **1–2 marks** in almost every question.
>
> 3. **Confusing indirect and direct method scopes.** The indirect (linearization) method only proves **local** stability. If the question asks for "asymptotic stability in the large" or "global asymptotic stability," you must use the direct method with a radially unbounded $V$. Writing "stable by indirect method" for a global question is a **2-mark** deduction.
>
> 4. **Sloppy $\dot{V}$ computation.** The most common algebraic error is missing a term in $\dot{V} = (\nabla V)^T f$. Show all intermediate steps; examiners award partial credit for correct setup even if the final simplification is wrong.
>
> 5. **Conflating SISL with asymptotic stability.** Lyapunov stability alone does NOT imply convergence. If $\dot{V} \leq 0$ only (PSD/NSD), the most you can claim without LaSalle is **stable in the sense of Lyapunov**, not asymptotically stable.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the radial unboundedness condition for GAS.** For global asymptotic stability, $V$ must satisfy $V(x) \to \infty$ as $\|x\| \to \infty$. Omitting this in a 14-mark answer can cost **1–2 marks**.
>
> 7. **Failing to mention the *region* of validity.** For nonlinear systems, $V$ may only certify stability inside a sublevel set $\{x : V(x) \leq c\}$ — identify and state this region explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Lyapunov stability** = bounded trajectory response (close stays close).
- [ ] **Asymptotic stability** = bounded + convergent (close stays close AND goes to zero).
- [ ] **Exponential stability** = asymptotic with a guaranteed $e^{-\alpha t}$ decay rate.
- [ ] **Global asymptotic stability (GAS)** requires $V$ positive definite + radially unbounded + $\dot{V}$ negative definite (everywhere).
- [ ] **Indirect method** uses eigenvalues of the Jacobian $A = \partial f/\partial x \vert_{x_e}$. Conclusive only when no eigenvalue lies on the imaginary axis. Gives only **local** results.
- [ ] **Direct method** requires a Lyapunov function $V(x)$ with $V(0)=0$, $V(x) > 0$ ($x \neq 0$), and $\dot{V} < 0$ for asymptotic stability (or $\leq 0$ for SISL).
- [ ] **Lyapunov equation** for LTI systems: $A^T P + P A = -Q$, $Q = Q^T > 0$. Solvable (uniquely) with $P > 0$ **iff** $A$ is Hurwitz.
- [ ] **Lyapunov function** = "energy-like" bowl; $\dot{V}$ negative definite means energy is dissipated strictly.
- [ ] **LaSalle's invariance principle** rescues cases where $\dot{V} \leq 0$ only (PSD), by examining the largest invariant set in $E = \{x : \dot{V} = 0\}$.
- [ ] **Region of attraction** estimate: sublevel set $\{x : V(x) \leq c\}$ inside which $V$ certifies stability.
- [ ] **Quadratic ansatz** $V = x^T P x$ is the most common, especially for linear systems.
- [ ] **Krasovskii method**: choose $V = f^T f$; automatically PD when $f(0)=0$. Gives $\dot{V} = 2 f^T J f$ ($J$ = Jacobian).
- [ ] **Variable gradient method**: specify $\dot{V}$ form, integrate to recover $\nabla V$, then construct $V$.
- [ ] **LMIs** $P > 0$, $A^T P + P A < 0$ are convex and solvable via semidefinite programming; foundation of modern robust CPS design.
- [ ] **CPS examples to memorize**: inverted pendulum, DC motor, consensus networks, lane-keeping, smart grid frequency.
- [ ] **Always state the region of validity** for nonlinear stability claims.
- [ ] **Lyapunov equation** has discrete analog: $A^T P A - P = -Q$ (discrete-time CPS).
- [ ] **BIBO stability ≠ internal stability** — Lyapunov deals with internal state stability, separate from input-output (BIBO) notions unless the system is controllable and observable (then equivalent).
- [ ] **Examine every eigenvalue of $A$**: $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0 \;\forall i$ is equivalent to $A$ being Hurwitz, equivalent to Lyapunov equation solvability with $P > 0$, equivalent to asymptotic stability (for LTI). These are *three equivalent characterizations* of the same property.
- [ ] **KTU frequently tests:** (i) defining stability, (ii) verifying a candidate $V$, (iii) solving the Lyapunov equation for $P$, (iv) computing eigenvalues for the indirect method, (v) LaSalle for consensus.
- [ ] **Equation summary for last-minute revision**:
    - Linearization: $A = \partial f / \partial x \vert_{0}$
    - Stability condition: $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0 \;\forall i$
    - Lyapunov equation: $A^T P + P A = -Q$
    - Energy ansatz: $V = x^T P x$
    - Rate: $\dot{V} = -x^T Q x$
    - LaSalle set: $E = \{x \in \Omega \mid \dot{V}(x) = 0\}$
- [ ] **Top formula to memorize verbatim (KTU board favorite)**:
    $$A^T P + P A + Q = 0, \quad P = P^T > 0, \quad Q = Q^T > 0$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimal and Robust Control: Overview of Optimal Control Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT604 - ADVANCED CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Optimal and Robust Control: Overview of Optimal Control Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: OPTIMAL AND ROBUST CONTROL
## Topic: Overview of Optimal Control Theory

---

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal Control Theory (OCT)** is a branch of modern control engineering that deals with the problem of determining the **control policy** (or control law) $u^*(t)$ for a dynamic system such that a predefined **Performance Index (PI)** — also called a **Cost Functional** $J$ — is minimized (or maximized) while the system satisfies specified physical constraints, boundary conditions, and dynamic equations of motion.

Mathematically, the generic optimal control problem is stated as:

$$\min_{u(t)} \quad J = \Phi[x(t_f), t_f] + \int_{t_0}^{t_f} L[x(t), u(t), t]\, dt$$

**subject to the system dynamics:**

$$\dot{x}(t) = f[x(t), u(t), t], \quad x(t_0) = x_0$$

with appropriate **boundary constraints** at final time $t_f$ and **admissible control constraints** $u(t) \in \mathcal{U}$.

Where:
- $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the **state vector**
- $u(t) \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the **control input vector**
- $J$ is the scalar **performance index** to be minimized
- $L$ is the **Lagrangian** (running cost) and $\Phi$ is the **terminal cost**
- $t_0$ and $t_f$ are the **initial and final times**

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Driving a Car Analogy:** Imagine you are driving a car from Kochi to Thiruvananthapuram (a known distance, fixed start and end points). You want to minimize **fuel consumption** while reaching exactly on time. You can press the accelerator harder, but that burns more fuel. You can go slower, but you may not make it. **Optimal control** is the math that finds the *exact* pedal-pressing pattern (throttle over time) that uses the least fuel. Every action you take is a *control input* $u(t)$, the road path is the *state* $x(t)$, and the total fuel used is the *cost* $J$. The math tells you the **best possible driving strategy** under physical laws (engine limits, road slope = *system dynamics*).

---

### 1.3 Distinction: Optimal Control vs. Classical Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Aspect | Classical Control | Optimal Control |
> |---|---|---|
> | Design Goal | Stability, tracking, disturbance rejection | **Minimize a cost function** over a horizon |
> | Input Type | Single-input single-output (SISO) | Multi-input multi-output (MIMO) |
> | Design Domain | Frequency (Bode, Nyquist) | **Time domain (state-space)** |
> | Result | A controller $C(s)$ | An **optimal control law** $u^*(x,t)$ |
> | Constraint Handling | Limited | Explicit handling of input/state constraints |
> | Typical Tools | PID, Lead-Lag, Root Locus | **PMP, DP, LQR, LQG, H∞** |

---

### 1.4 Why Optimal Control is Critical for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** integrates computation, networking, and physical processes. Examples: autonomous vehicles, smart grids, robotic surgery, drone swarms.

In CPS, the controller is *not* designed to merely *stabilize*; it must operate under **strict resource budgets** (battery, fuel, bandwidth, CPU time) and **safety constraints**. Hence, optimal control is the natural design paradigm. The KTU 2024 syllabus for PBCPT604 emphasizes this connection.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constituents of the Optimal Control Problem

A complete optimal control problem is defined by **four pillars**:

1. **System Model** — the differential/difference equation governing the plant.
2. **Performance Index (PI)** — a scalar that quantifies "goodness" of behavior.
3. **Boundary Conditions** — initial state $x(t_0)$ and final state (or final time) constraints.
4. **Admissible Controls** — the set $\mathcal{U}$ of allowed control inputs (e.g., bounded throttle).

---

### 2.2 Classification of Performance Indices

> [!NOTE]
> **Three main families of cost functionals:**

**(a) Integral (Regulator / Tracking) PI** — most common in KTU problems
$$J = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} L[x(t), u(t), t]\, dt$$

**(b) Terminal PI** — penalizes final error only
$$J = \Phi[x(t_f), t_f]$$

**(c) Bolza (General) PI** — combination
$$J = \Phi[x(t_f), t_f] + \int_{t_0}^{t_f} L[x(t), u(t), t]\, dt$$

**If the upper limit $t_f$ is free**, the problem is called a **free-final-time problem**, otherwise it is a **fixed-final-time problem**.

---

### 2.3 Common Performance Indices Encountered in KTU

| PI Form | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Minimum Energy** | $J = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} u^T u \, dt$ | Energy-limited systems (satellites, battery CPS) |
| **Minimum Time (Bang-Bang)** | $J = t_f - t_0$ | Rapid reorientation, missile guidance |
| **Quadratic (LQR)** | $J = \tfrac{1}{2} x^T(t_f) S x(t_f) + \tfrac{1}{2} \int_{t_0}^{t_f} (x^T Q x + u^T R u)\, dt$ | Linear CPS with penalised state & effort |
| **Tracking** | $J = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} (x - x_d)^T Q (x - x_d) + u^T R u \, dt$ | Robotics, reference following |
| **IATE / ITAE** | $J = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} t \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ | Classical tuning index |

Where $Q \succeq 0$ and $R \succ 0$ are symmetric weighting matrices (a **positive semidefinite** state-weighting and a **positive definite** control-weighting matrix respectively).

---

### 2.4 Three Pillars of Solving the Optimal Control Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three classical mathematical tools used to derive optimal control laws:

**(1) Calculus of Variations** — the foundational tool; finds extrema of functionals.

**(2) Pontryagin's Minimum Principle (PMP)** — extends calculus of variations to *constrained* control problems (e.g., bounded $u$). Gives a *necessary* condition for optimality.

**(3) Dynamic Programming / Bellman's Principle of Optimality** — recursive backward-in-time optimization. Foundation of **Adaptive Dynamic Programming (ADP)** and **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** in modern CPS.

> [!NOTE]
> For **linear systems** with **quadratic cost**, the PMP reduces to the **Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE)**, which yields the **Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR)** — the most exam-frequent topic in KTU.

---

### 2.5 Boundary Conditions and Two-Point Boundary Value Problem (TPBVP)

The optimality conditions from PMP yield a system of $2n$ coupled ODEs (state + costate equations) with $n$ initial conditions and $n$ final conditions — but split between $x$ and $\lambda$. This is the **Two-Point Boundary Value Problem (TPBVP)**:

$$\dot{x}^* = \frac{\partial H}{\partial \lambda}, \quad \dot{\lambda}^* = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial x}$$

with $x(t_0) = x_0$ and boundary conditions on $\lambda(t_f)$ (or on $x(t_f)$ if fixed endpoint).

This is hard to solve analytically in general; **LQR is the elegant closed-form exception** for linear-quadratic problems.

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | **#** | **Concept** | **Formula / Statement** |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | Generic Performance Index (Bolza) | $J = \Phi(x(t_f), t_f) + \int_{t_0}^{t_f} L(x,u,t)\, dt$ |
> | 2 | System Dynamics Constraint | $\dot{x}(t) = f(x,u,t)$, $\; x(t_0)=x_0$ |
> | 3 | Hamiltonian | $H(x,u,\lambda,t) = L(x,u,t) + \lambda^T f(x,u,t)$ |
> | 4 | State Equation (PMP) | $\dot{x}^* = \dfrac{\partial H}{\partial \lambda} = f(x^*,u^*,t)$ |
> | 5 | Costate Equation (PMP) | $\dot{\lambda}^* = -\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial x}$ |
> | 6 | Stationarity Condition | $\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial u} = 0$ (interior optimum) |
> | 7 | Transversality (fixed $t_f$) | $\lambda(t_f) = \dfrac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x(t_f)}$ |
> | 8 | Transversality (free $t_f$) | $H(t_f) = -\dfrac{\partial \Phi}{\partial t_f}$ |
> | 9 | Euler-Lagrange (calc. of var.) | $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) - \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial x} = 0$ |
> | 10 | Bellman (HJB) Equation | $0 = \min_u \left[ L + \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x}\right)^T f + \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} \right]$ |
> | 11 | LQR Algebraic Riccati Eq. (ARE) | $A^T P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^T P + Q = 0$ |
> | 12 | LQR Optimal Gain | $K = R^{-1} B^T P$, $\quad u^* = -K x$ |
> | 13 | LQR Closed-Loop Dynamics | $\dot{x} = (A - B K) x$ |
> | 14 | LQR Value Function | $V(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, x^T P x$ |
> | 15 | Closed-Loop Stability | $A - B K$ is **Hurwitz** (eigenvalues in open LHP) |
> | 16 | Cost Lower Bound | $J^* = \tfrac{1}{2}\, x_0^T P\, x_0$ |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners often ask students to write the **Riccati equation without sign error**. Memorize: $A^T P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^T P + Q = 0$. The minus sign is *not* a plus — it is the *negative* of the gain-product term.

---

### 2.7 Engineering Utility Summary

| CPS Application | Optimal Control Form Used |
|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicle Lane-Keeping | LQR with preview (look-ahead tracking) |
| Drone Trajectory Tracking | LQR / MPC with quadratic cost |
| Robotic Manipulator Path | LQR + feed-forward for reference |
| Smart Grid Economic Dispatch | LQ + Dynamic Programming |
| Satellite Attitude Control | Minimum-energy or minimum-time PI |
| Self-Driving Car Adaptive Cruise | MPC = receding-horizon optimal control |
| Networked CPS w/ Packet Loss | Stochastic optimal control (Dynamic Programming) |
