# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Compilation Note:** All Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric (prefixed with letters, e.g., `n1`, `n2`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone IDs. All node labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without any markdown formatting.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1[R REFERENCE INPUT] --> n2[REF MODEL Gm]
    n2 --> n3[Ym COMPARATOR]
    n1 --> n4[REGRESSOR PHI BUILDER]
    n4 --> n5[CONTROLLER U OF THETA PHI]
    n5 --> n6[PLANT Gp]
    n6 --> n7[YP MEASURED]
    n7 --> n3
    n3 --> n8[ERROR E YP MINUS YM]
    n8 --> n9[ADAPTATION LAW THETA DOT]
    n4 --> n9
    n9 --> n5

    classDef refbox fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px
    classDef plantbox fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef adaptbox fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px
    class n1,n2,n3,n4,n8 refbox
    class n5,n6,n7 plantbox
    class n9 adaptbox
```

**Reading the diagram:** The reference input $r(t)$ feeds two paths in parallel. The *upper path* sends $r$ through the fixed reference model $G_m$ to produce $y_m$, the desired response. The *lower path* sends $r$ through a *regressor builder* that constructs the vector $\phi(t)$ used by the adaptive controller. The plant output $y_p$ is compared to $y_m$ to form the tracking error $e$, which — together with $\phi$ — drives the adaptation law. The adaptation law updates $\theta$, which modifies the controller $u = \theta^{\top}\phi$, closing the inner adaptation loop. Note the **two feedback loops**: one classical (output $y_p$ back to the comparator) and one adaptive ($\theta$ being updated by the adaptation law).

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Self-Tuning Regulator (STR)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    n1[REF R] --> n2[DESIGNER COMPUTES C HAT]
    n2 --> n3[CONTROLLER C THETA HAT]
    n1 --> n3
    n3 --> n4[PLANT G]
    n4 --> n5[OUTPUT Y]
    n5 --> n6[REGRESSOR PHI FROM Y U]
    n5 --> n7[ERROR E R MINUS Y]
    n1 --> n6
    n3 --> n6
    n6 --> n8[RLS ESTIMATOR THETA HAT K]
    n8 --> n2
    n8 --> n3

    classDef estimator fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    classDef designer fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef plantbox fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    class n8 estimator
    class n2 designer
    class n3,n4,n5 plantbox
```

**Reading the diagram:** The *outer* loop is the RLS estimator, which uses the regressor $\phi$ (built from past inputs and outputs) and the measured output $y$ to recursively update the parameter estimate $\hat{\theta}(k)$. The *inner* loop is the *recomputed* controller, which uses the *current* estimate $\hat{\theta}$ to produce a fresh control signal $u$. The block "DESIGNER" performs the re-design step — for instance, solving a pole-placement equation at each sampling instant. This two-loop structure is the defining feature of the STR, in contrast to the single-loop MRAC.

## 4.3 Architecture Flow — Adaptive Control Within a Cyber-Physical System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1[PHYSICAL LAYER SENSORS ACTUATORS] --> n2[EDGE COMPUTING LOCAL ESTIMATION]
    n2 --> n3[ADAPTIVE CONTROLLER HOST PROCESSOR]
    n3 --> n1
    n3 --> n4[CLOUD DIGITAL TWIN]
    n4 --> n5[MACHINE LEARNING PARAMETER PRIORS]
    n5 --> n3
    n3 --> n6[SAFETY MONITOR GUARDRAILS]
    n6 --> n3

    classDef physical fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
    classDef compute fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff8f00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cloud fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef safety fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px
    class n1 physical
    class n2,n3 compute
    class n4,n5 cloud
    class n6 safety
```

**Reading the diagram:** In a modern CPS, the adaptive controller does not live in isolation. Sensors and actuators (physical layer) stream data to an edge computer that runs the *local* RLS estimator. The adaptive controller — implemented on the host processor — issues commands back to the plant. Periodically, a digital twin in the cloud receives logged data and uses offline machine learning to provide *better parameter priors* to the on-board adaptation law. A safety monitor watches for instability indicators (e.g., parameter drift, error growth) and can override the adaptive law to revert to a safe fixed-gain fallback. This is the **learning-augmented CPS** architecture that the KTU 2024 syllabus positions adaptive control within.

## 4.4 Signal Flow — MIT Rule Adaptation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1[ERROR E T] --> n2[MULTIPLIER]
    n2 --> n3[INTEGRATOR 1 OVER S]
    n3 --> n4[THETA T UPDATED]
    n4 --> n5[CONTROLLER U THETA PHI]
    n5 --> n6[PLANT GP]
    n6 --> n7[YP T]
    n7 --> n8[COMPARATOR]
    n1 --> n8
    n8 --> n2

    classDef sig fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00695c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef adapt fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457,stroke-width:2px
    classDef plantbox fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    class n1,n7 sig
    class n2,n3,n4 adapt
    class n5,n6 plantbox
```

**Reading the diagram:** This signal-flow view highlights that the *adaptation integrator* $\int_0^t (\cdot)\,d\tau$ is the *memory* of the adaptive system. The product $e \cdot \zeta$ is the input to that integrator. The integrator's output, $\theta(t)$, then linearly modulates the controller. Notice that the plant output $y_p$ is fed back not only through the classical comparator but also through the *implicit* dependence on $\theta$ in the controller — the **two-way coupling** between parameter dynamics and error dynamics.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Q: Differentiate between Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) and Self-Tuning Regulator (STR). List any two distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — exact KTU key pattern):**

| Feature | MRAC | STR |
|---|---|---|
| **Reference** | An explicit *reference model* $G_m$ defines the desired behaviour. | No fixed reference model; a *performance index* (e.g., minimum variance) or pole-placement criterion is used. |
| **Identification** | Implicit — the adaptation law directly adjusts the controller. | Explicit — a separate estimator (RLS) updates $\hat{\theta}$. |
| **Re-design** | Continuous, gradient-based update of $\theta$. | Discrete (sample-by-sample) re-computation of the controller. |
| **Stability** | Guaranteed only by Lyapunov redesign; MIT rule is heuristic. | Guaranteed only if the estimator converges (Persistent Excitation). |
| **Implementation** | Single-loop, conceptually simpler. | Two-loop (estimator + controller), more flexible. |

*Rubric (3 marks):* 1 mark for any one clear difference + 1 mark for the second clear difference + 1 mark for a concluding statement identifying the architectural distinction (reference model vs explicit identifier).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the MIT rule for adaptive control. What is the role of the sensitivity derivative $\zeta = \partial y_p / \partial \theta$ in the adaptation law?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — exact KTU key pattern):**

The **MIT rule** is a gradient-descent-based adaptation law for adjusting controller parameters online, defined as:

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = -\gamma \cdot \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = -\gamma \cdot e(t) \cdot \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} \approx -\gamma \cdot e(t) \cdot \zeta(t)$$

where $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}e^2$ is the squared-error cost, $\gamma > 0$ is the adaptation gain, and $\zeta(t) = \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta}$ is the **sensitivity derivative** of the plant output with respect to the controllable parameter.

**Role of $\zeta$:** The sensitivity derivative measures *how much the plant output $y_p$ would change* if the parameter $\theta$ were perturbed by a small amount. In the MIT rule, it serves as the *direction indicator* for the gradient descent — telling the adaptation law which way to push $\theta$ so as to reduce the error. Without $\zeta$, the rule would have no information about *which direction* improves performance; multiplying the error $e$ by $\zeta$ converts the *magnitude* of the error into a *signed, directional* update. Mathematically, $\zeta$ is the *Jacobian* of the plant's input–output map with respect to $\theta$.

*Rubric (3 marks):* 1 mark for the boxed equation, 1 mark for defining $\zeta$, 1 mark for explaining its role as a direction indicator in the gradient descent.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall:**
> - **Sign of MIT rule (–1 mark):** KTU uses the convention $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e \zeta$. Writing it without the negative sign costs a mark.
> - **Stability claim (–2 marks):** Asserting "MIT rule is always stable" loses 2 marks. The MIT rule is a *heuristic* with no inherent stability guarantee; only Lyapunov redesign provides it.
> - **Missing the regressor $\phi$ (–1 mark):** Writing the control law as $u = \theta r$ alone (without the filter-based regressor) is *incomplete* — non-implementable, since it requires differentiating $y_p$.
> - **Skipping the cost function (–1 mark):** The adaptation law *must* be derived from a cost $J(\theta)$ or a Lyapunov function $V(e, \tilde{\theta})$. Writing the law without motivation loses 1 mark.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 1, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a **Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)** system. Clearly identify the reference model, the adaptation mechanism, and the tracking error. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a first-order plant $\dot{y}_p = -a_p y_p + b_p u$ with unknown parameters $a_p, b_p$, and a reference model $\dot{y}_m = -a_m y_m + b_m r$, derive the **MIT rule** and the **Lyapunov-based adaptation law** for the control signal $u = \theta_1 r - \theta_2 y_p$. Comment on the stability of each. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block diagram (7 marks):**

The MRAC system consists of the following interconnected blocks (refer to Section 4.1):

1. **Reference input $r(t)$** — the desired command signal. (0.5 mark)
2. **Reference model $G_m(s)$** — a fixed, stable model whose output $y_m$ defines the *desired* closed-loop behaviour (rise time, overshoot, settling time). The reference model must have the same relative degree as the plant. (1 mark)
3. **Plant $G_p(s)$** — the actual physical system to be controlled, with (possibly unknown) parameters. Its output $y_p$ is the *actual* response. (1 mark)
4. **Comparator** — computes the tracking error $e(t) = y_p(t) - y_m(t)$. (0.5 mark)
5. **Adaptation law block** — implements the update $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e \zeta$, where $\zeta = \partial y_p / \partial \theta$ is the sensitivity derivative. The output is the updated parameter vector $\theta(t)$. (2 marks)
6. **Adaptive controller** — implements $u(t) = \theta^{\top}(t)\phi(t)$, where $\phi(t)$ is the regressor vector (built from filtered versions of $r$ and $y_p$). (1 mark)
7. **Two feedback loops** must be clearly indicated: (i) classical output feedback $y_p \to$ comparator, and (ii) adaptive feedback $\theta \to$ controller. (1 mark)

A textual narrative: "The MRAC continuously compares the actual plant output to the desired model output. The difference (the tracking error) is fed into an adaptation mechanism that adjusts the controller gains in real time so that the plant behaviour converges to that of the reference model."

**(b) Derivation (7 marks):**

**MIT rule derivation (3.5 marks):**

Step 1 — Define the tracking error:

$$e = y_p - y_m$$

Step 2 — Define the cost function:

$$J(\theta_1, \theta_2) = \frac{1}{2}e^2$$

Step 3 — Compute the sensitivity derivative. With the control law $u = \theta_1 r - \theta_2 y_p$, the plant's input is $u$, so:

$$\frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial u}\cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial u} \cdot r$$

Define $\zeta_1 \triangleq \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial u} \cdot r$. Similarly, $\zeta_2 \triangleq -\frac{\partial y_p}{\partial u} \cdot y_p$. In practice, the partial $\partial y_p / \partial u$ is replaced by the *sign* of the high-frequency gain (a well-known MRAC trick):

$$\zeta_1 = \operatorname{sgn}(b_p) \cdot r, \qquad \zeta_2 = -\operatorname{sgn}(b_p) \cdot y_p$$

Step 4 — Apply the gradient-descent rule $\dot{\theta}_i = -\gamma_i \partial J / \partial \theta_i$:

$$\dot{\theta}_1 = -\gamma_1 \cdot e \cdot \zeta_1 = -\gamma_1 \cdot \operatorname{sgn}(b_p) \cdot e \cdot r$$

$$\dot{\theta}_2 = -\gamma_2 \cdot e \cdot \zeta_2 = +\gamma_2 \cdot \operatorname{sgn}(b_p) \cdot e \cdot y_p$$

**Stability comment (0.5 mark):** The MIT rule is a *heuristic* with no built-in stability guarantee; for large $\gamma$ or non-minimum-phase plants, it can be unstable.

**Lyapunov-based derivation (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Error dynamics. With the chosen $u$, after substituting and grouping:

$$\dot{e} = -a_m e + b_p \bigl[\tilde{\theta}_1 r - \tilde{\theta}_2 y_p\bigr]$$

where $\tilde{\theta}_1 = \theta_1 - b_m/b_p$ and $\tilde{\theta}_2 = \theta_2 - (a_p - a_m)/b_p$.

Step 2 — Lyapunov candidate:

$$V = \frac{1}{2}e^2 + \frac{1}{2\gamma_1}\tilde{\theta}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2\gamma_2}\tilde{\theta}_2^2$$

Step 3 — Differentiate and substitute:

$$\dot{V} = e\dot{e} + \frac{1}{\gamma_1}\tilde{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_1 + \frac{1}{2\gamma_2}\tilde{\theta}_2\dot{\theta}_2$$

After algebra (shown in Section 3.3), the cross-terms cancel *exactly* when:

$$\dot{\theta}_1 = -\gamma_1 e r, \qquad \dot{\theta}_2 = \gamma_2 e y_p$$

(absorbing the unknown $\operatorname{sgn}(b_p)$ into the gains)

Step 4 — Final result:

$$\dot{V} = -a_m e^2 \le 0$$

By LaSalle's invariance principle, $e \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$, and the parameters $\tilde{\theta}_i$ remain bounded. With persistent excitation, $\hat{\theta} \to \theta^{\star}$.

**Stability comment (0.5 mark):** The Lyapunov law has a *built-in stability certificate* — $\dot{V} \le 0$ by construction. This is its principal advantage over the MIT rule.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Paper, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the architecture of a **Self-Tuning Regulator (STR)**. Explain the role of the **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)** estimator and the **certainty equivalence principle**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **RLS update equations** starting from the exponentially weighted least-squares cost function. Discuss the effect of the forgetting factor $\lambda$ on tracking a time-varying plant. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) STR architecture (7 marks):**

The Self-Tuning Regulator is a *two-loop* adaptive controller (see Section 4.2). The two loops are:

1. **Inner loop — feedback control.** The plant $G$ is controlled by a controller $C(\hat{\theta})$ whose parameters are the *current* estimates $\hat{\theta}(k)$. The controller can be a pole-placement compensator, a minimum-variance law, or an LQG regulator. (1.5 marks)
2. **Outer loop — recursive identification.** At each sampling instant $k$, a parameter estimator (almost always RLS) updates the parameter vector $\hat{\theta}(k)$ using the new measurement $y(k)$ and the regressor $\phi(k)$ built from past inputs and outputs. (1.5 marks)
3. **Re-design step.** After each parameter update, the controller is *re-computed* using the *current* estimate $\hat{\theta}$. For pole placement, this means solving the Diophantine equation $A(\hat{\theta}) C + B(\hat{\theta}) F = A_{\text{desired}}$. (1 mark)
4. **Certainty equivalence principle.** The STR operates on the working assumption that the *current* estimate $\hat{\theta}$ is *as if* it were the true parameter $\theta^{\star}$. The controller is therefore designed as if there were no uncertainty, even though there is. This is the *key intellectual step* that makes the STR tractable. (2 marks)
5. **A block diagram with two feedback loops must be drawn**, showing the regressor $\phi$ built from $u$ and $y$, feeding the RLS estimator, whose output $\hat{\theta}$ is used by the *re-design* block to update the controller. (1 mark)

**Narrative:** "The STR separates the *what to control by* (controller design) from the *what the plant looks like right now* (online identification). At every sample, both are refreshed, allowing the controller to track a slowly time-varying plant."

**(b) RLS derivation and forgetting factor (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Step 1 — Exponentially weighted cost:

$$J_N(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\lambda^{N-k}\bigl[y(k) - \phi^{\top}(k)\theta\bigr]^2, \quad \lambda \in (0,1]$$

Step 2 — Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial J_N}{\partial \theta} = -\sum_{k=1}^{N}\lambda^{N-k}\phi(k)\bigl[y(k) - \phi^{\top}(k)\theta\bigr] = 0$$

Step 3 — Define the weighted covariance:

$$P_N^{-1} = \sum_{k=1}^{N}\lambda^{N-k}\phi(k)\phi^{\top}(k) = \lambda P_{N-1}^{-1} + \phi(N)\phi^{\top}(N)$$

Step 4 — Apply the matrix inversion lemma (Woodbury identity):

$$P_N = \lambda^{-1}P_{N-1} - \lambda^{-1}P_{N-1}\phi(N)\bigl[1 + \phi^{\top}(N)\lambda^{-1}P_{N-1}\phi(N)\bigr]^{-1}\phi^{\top}(N)\lambda^{-1}P_{N-1}$$

Step 5 — Define the gain vector and simplify:

$$K(N) = \frac{P(N-1)\phi(N)}{\lambda + \phi^{\top}(N)P(N-1)\phi(N)}$$

Step 6 — Covariance update:

$$P(N) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\bigl[I - K(N)\phi^{\top}(N)\bigr]P(N-1)$$

Step 7 — Parameter update:

$$\hat{\theta}(N) = \hat{\theta}(N-1) + K(N)\,\bigl[y(N) - \phi^{\top}(N)\hat{\theta}(N-1)\bigr]$$

The three boxed equations are the **RLS family** — to be memorised verbatim for the 14-mark answer.

**Effect of the forgetting factor $\lambda$ (2 marks):**

- $\lambda = 1$: Standard RLS. All past data weighted equally. Good for *time-invariant* plants; the covariance $P(k)$ decays as $1/k$, and the gain $K(k) \to 0$, so the estimator *stops learning*. Inappropriate for time-varying plants.
- $\lambda < 1$: Exponentially weighted RLS. Past data older than roughly $\frac{1}{1-\lambda}$ samples is effectively discarded. The estimator *tracks* parameter drift at the cost of higher variance (because less data is averaged). The rule of thumb: $\lambda \in [0.95, 0.99]$ for slow drift, $\lambda \in [0.90, 0.95]$ for faster drift.
- **Trade-off:** Small $\lambda$ = fast tracking, high noise sensitivity. Large $\lambda$ = slow tracking, low noise sensitivity.
- **Numerical stability:** Implement RLS using the **square-root** or **UD-factorisation** forms when $\lambda$ is very small, to avoid covariance-matrix wind-up (loss of positive definiteness).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1**

### Core Concepts (must know)
- [ ] **Three adaptive architectures:** Gain Scheduling, MRAC, STR.
- [ ] **MIT rule:** $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e \zeta$, with $J = \tfrac{1}{2}e^2$ and $\zeta = \partial y_p / \partial \theta$.
- [ ] **Lyapunov adaptation law:** same form as MIT but with a *stability guarantee* via $V = \tfrac{1}{2}e^2 + \tfrac{1}{2\gamma}\tilde{\theta}^{\top}\tilde{\theta}$.
- [ ] **RLS family:** three coupled recursions for $\hat{\theta}$, $K$, and $P$, parameterised by $\lambda$.
- [ ] **Certainty equivalence principle:** STR designs the controller as if $\hat{\theta} = \theta^{\star}$.
- [ ] **Reference model:** Stable, minimum-phase, same relative degree as the plant.
- [ ] **Persistent excitation:** Necessary condition for parameter convergence (not just boundedness).

### Key Differentiators (commonly asked)
- [ ] MRAC uses an *implicit* identifier embedded in the adaptation law; STR uses an *explicit* RLS identifier.
- [ ] MIT rule is *heuristic* and *unstable* in pathological cases; Lyapunov rule is *provably stable*.
- [ ] Gain scheduling is *open-loop adaptation* (no feedback on the parameter error); MRAC and STR are *closed-loop adaptation*.

### Formulas to Memorise Cold (3 must box on the exam)
- [ ] $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e \zeta$
- [ ] $\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\varepsilon(k)$
- [ ] $V = \tfrac{1}{2}e^2 + \tfrac{1}{2\gamma}\tilde{\theta}^{\top}\tilde{\theta}$ with $\dot{V} = -a_m e^2 \le 0$

### Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Do **not** write the MIT rule without the **negative** sign.
- [ ] Do **not** claim MIT rule is *always stable* — it is a heuristic.
- [ ] Do **not** forget to include the **regressor $\phi$** when writing the control law.
- [ ] Do **not** set $\lambda = 0$ in RLS — it makes the covariance singular.
- [ ] Do **not** confuse the **innovation** $\varepsilon(k) = y(k) - \phi^{\top}(k)\hat{\theta}(k-1)$ with the **adaptation error** $\varepsilon_a(t) = e(t)\zeta(t)$.

### Real-World CPS Context (to mention for full marks)
- [ ] UAV mass-drift compensation via MRAC.
- [ ] Robotic-arm inertia adaptation via STR.
- [ ] Smart-grid inverter impedance adaptation.
- [ ] Persistent excitation in practice is often provided by a *small* auxiliary dither signal added to the reference.
- [ ] Modern CPS augment adaptive control with **machine-learning priors** from a cloud digital twin — a hot research direction (e.g., learning-augmented model predictive control).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** When answering a 14-mark question on this topic, **always** include (i) a labelled block diagram, (ii) the *cost* or *Lyapunov function* you are using, (iii) the explicit *derivation* of the adaptation law, and (iv) a *stability comment*. A response that omits any one of these four elements is, in the KTU 2024 valuation key, *incomplete* and loses between 2 and 4 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reinforcement Learning in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT604 - ADVANCED CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reinforcement Learning in CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) is defined as a computational framework of machine learning in which an **intelligent agent** learns an optimal **decision-making policy** $\pi$ by continuously interacting with a **dynamic physical environment** through a closed perception–action loop. The agent observes the system state $s_t$, executes an action $a_t$, receives a scalar reward signal $r_t$, and iteratively refines its behaviour to maximise the long-term cumulative discounted return $G_t$.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PBCPT604 – Advanced Cyber Physical Systems**, RL is positioned as the *intelligence layer* that bridges the **physical dynamics** of a plant (sensors, actuators, mechanical/electrical processes) with the **cyber/decision layer** (controllers, edge/cloud compute, communication networks). It enables CPS to achieve **autonomy, self-optimisation, and adaptivity** in environments where analytical models are inaccurate, partially known, or non-stationary.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Training a Pet Dog:**
> Imagine teaching a dog to fetch a ball. You do not write equations of motion for the dog. Instead, you give a **treat (reward)** when it does something close to what you want and **withhold the treat (punishment)** when it misbehaves. Over many trials, the dog learns the sequence of actions that maximises treats. Reinforcement Learning works **exactly like this**, except the "dog" is a software agent and the "treat" is a numerical reward $r_t \in \mathbb{R}$.

In a CPS:
* The **"dog"** = RL Agent (controller, decision policy).
* The **"environment"** = Physical plant (robot, autonomous vehicle, smart grid, drone).
* The **"treat"** = Reward function (e.g., tracking error minimisation, energy efficiency, safety margin).
* The **"trials"** = Episodes of interaction over discrete time steps $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots, T$.

## 1.3 Core Constituents of an RL-Enabled CPS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus emphasises the following six fundamental elements of an RL framework. Each is a *high-weightage* point frequently tested in 2-mark and short-answer questions.

1. **Agent ($\mathcal{A}$)** — The cyber-side decision-making entity (e.g., neural-network-based controller running on an embedded processor).
2. **Environment ($\mathcal{E}$)** — The physical CPS plant governed by dynamics $s_{t+1} = f(s_t, a_t, w_t)$ where $w_t$ represents process noise.
3. **State Space ($\mathcal{S}$)** — The set of all possible perceptual observations $s \in \mathcal{S}$ (e.g., position, velocity, temperature, current).
4. **Action Space ($\mathcal{A}$)** — The set of control commands $a \in \mathcal{A}$ (e.g., torque, voltage, throttle angle).
5. **Reward Signal ($r_t \in \mathbb{R}$)** — Scalar feedback indicating the *immediate* desirability of the transition $(s_t, a_t) \to s_{t+1}$.
6. **Policy ($\pi$)** — The agent's behaviour mapping $\pi: \mathcal{S} \to \mathcal{P}(\mathcal{A})$, either **deterministic** $a = \pi(s)$ or **stochastic** $a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)$.

## 1.4 Why RL is Indispensable in Modern CPS

| CPS Challenge | Conventional Control Limitation | RL Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| Non-linear dynamics | Linearisation causes model mismatch | RL learns non-linear policies directly |
| Unknown disturbances | Requires hand-tuned robust controllers | RL adapts via reward-driven exploration |
| Multi-objective optimisation | Weighted PID tuning is heuristic | RL handles composite rewards natively |
| High-dimensional sensing (vision, LiDAR) | Classical state-estimators fail | Deep RL scales to raw high-dim inputs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Definition to Memorise:** *An RL agent in a CPS learns a control policy by trial-and-error interaction with the physical environment, optimising a long-term reward signal rather than a pre-derived mathematical model.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Markov Decision Process (MDP) — Mathematical Backbone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every RL problem in a CPS is formally modelled as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**, represented by the 5-tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$. The Markov property states that the future depends only on the present state, not the history.

### 2.1.1 Components of the MDP

* **State Transition Probability:** $P(s' \mid s, a) = \Pr(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a)$
* **Reward Function:** $R(s, a, s') = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t+1} = s']$
* **Discount Factor:** $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ — controls preference for immediate vs. future rewards.

### 2.1.2 The Markov Property — Why It Matters in CPS

A CPS state $s_t$ is said to be *Markovian* if it satisfies the **sufficiency condition**:

$$P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \dots, s_0, a_0)$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** If the state vector $s_t$ contains *all* physically relevant information (position, velocity, previous actions encoded via memory), the future is conditionally independent of the past. This is the foundational assumption that makes RL tractable in CPS.

## 2.2 Return, Value Functions, and Bellman Equations

### 2.2.1 Discounted Return

The cumulative reward from time $t$ onward is:

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} \, r_{t+k+1} = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^2 r_{t+3} + \dots$$

> **Why discount ($\gamma < 1$)?** In physical systems, future rewards are uncertain due to wear, disturbances, and stochasticity. Discounting also guarantees mathematical convergence of the infinite sum.

### 2.2.2 State-Value Function

The expected return when starting in state $s$ and following policy $\pi$:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\big[\,G_t \mid S_t = s\,\big] = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1} \,\Big|\, S_t = s\right]$$

### 2.2.3 Action-Value Function (Q-Function)

The expected return when taking action $a$ in state $s$ and thereafter following $\pi$:

$$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\big[\,G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a\,\big]$$

### 2.2.4 Bellman Expectation Equation

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \big[\,R(s, a, s') + \gamma\, V^{\pi}(s')\big]$$

### 2.2.5 Bellman Optimality Equation

$$V^{*}(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \big[\,R(s, a, s') + \gamma\, V^{*}(s')\big]$$

$$Q^{*}(s, a) = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \big[\,R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')\big]$$

> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A *policy* $\pi^{*}$ is optimal iff $V^{\pi^{*}}(s) \geq V^{\pi}(s)$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ and all policies $\pi$.

## 2.3 Exploration vs. Exploitation Dilemma

A pivotal concept in CPS-applied RL. The agent must balance:

* **Exploitation** — Choose the best-known action $\arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ (refine current knowledge).
* **Exploration** — Choose a random/uncertain action to discover potentially superior policies.

**$\varepsilon$-Greedy Strategy:**

$$a_t = \begin{cases} \arg\max_{a} Q(s_t, a) & \text{with probability } 1 - \varepsilon \\ \text{random action from } \mathcal{A} & \text{with probability } \varepsilon \end{cases}$$

> **CPS Implication:** In a real physical system, exploration means applying *suboptimal or risky* control inputs (e.g., a robot swinging into a wall). This is why **safe exploration** (using constraints, simulators, or domain randomisation) is a critical research area in CPS-RL.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **condensed reference** for end-semester preparation. It is aligned with the equations KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in 14-mark derivations.

| **Symbol / Concept** | **Equation / Definition** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Discounted Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k}\, r_{t+k+1}$ | Dimensionless if reward normalised |
| State-Value Function | $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ | Same units as reward |
| Action-Value Function | $Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ | Same units as reward |
| Bellman Optimality (V) | $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma V^{*}]$ | Recursive |
| Bellman Optimality (Q) | $Q^{*}(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}]$ | Recursive |
| Q-Learning Update | $Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)]$ | $\alpha \in (0, 1]$ learning rate |
| TD Error | $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma V(s_{t+1}) - V(s_t)$ | Measures prediction error |
| Policy Gradient Objective | $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_{\theta}}[G_0]$ | $\theta$ = policy parameters |
| Policy Gradient Theorem | $\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}[\nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a \mid s) \, G_t]$ | REINFORCE form |
| Discount Factor Constraint | $0 \le \gamma < 1$ | Ensures convergence |
| $\varepsilon$-Greedy Action | $a = \arg\max Q$ w.p. $1 - \varepsilon$; random w.p. $\varepsilon$ | Exploration mechanism |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Students frequently confuse the *Bellman Expectation Equation* (used for a *given* policy) with the *Bellman Optimality Equation* (used for the *optimal* policy). The latter has a $\max_a$ operator; the former does not. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction if mixed.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **CPS Domain** | **RL Application** | **Reward Function Design** |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles | Lane-keeping, obstacle avoidance | $-\vert x_{lateral} \vert - \lambda \cdot \text{collision penalty}$ |
| Robotic Manipulators | Trajectory tracking, grasp planning | $-\Vert q_{desired} - q_{actual} \Vert^2$ |
| Smart Grids | Demand-response, load balancing | $-(\text{cost}) + \beta \cdot \text{stability margin}$ |
| HVAC Systems | Energy-efficient climate control | $-(\text{energy} + \mu \cdot \text{thermal discomfort})$ |
| Industrial Process Control | Set-point optimisation | $-(\text{yield loss} + \nu \cdot \text{raw material waste})$ |

> **Engineering Insight:** The *design of the reward function* is more impactful than the choice of RL algorithm. A poorly shaped reward leads to **reward hacking**, where the agent exploits loopholes in the physics (e.g., a drone learning to hover high to "minimise altitude error" in an inverted reward).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bellman Equation for State-Value Function

We begin from the definition of the return and apply the law of iterated expectations, taking advantage of the Markov property.

### Step 1 — Definition of $V^{\pi}(s)$

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\big[\,G_t \mid S_t = s\,\big]$$

### Step 2 — Expand the Return

By definition, the return is the immediate reward plus the discounted future return:

$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into the Expectation

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\big[\,r_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\,\big]$$

### Step 4 — Apply Linearity of Expectation

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\big[\,r_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\,\big] + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\big[\,G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s\,\big]$$

### Step 5 — Marginalise over Actions and Successor States

The agent selects action $a$ from policy $\pi(a \mid s)$, and the environment transitions to $s'$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s) &= \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \Big[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Recognise the Recursive Structure

By the Markov property, $\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] = V^{\pi}(s')$. Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \big[\,R(s, a, s') + \gamma\, V^{\pi}(s')\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This is the **Bellman Expectation Equation**. It expresses that the value of any state is the *one-step* reward plus the *discounted* value of where you end up — a recursive, dynamical-systems-style expression.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Q-Learning Update Rule

Q-Learning is a *model-free*, *off-policy* Temporal-Difference (TD) algorithm. We derive its iterative update from first principles.

### Step 1 — TD(0) Target

The 1-step TD target is the observed reward plus the discounted estimated value of the next state:

$$\text{Target} = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a')$$

### Step 2 — TD Error Definition

$$\delta_t = \text{Target} - Q(s_t, a_t) = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$$

### Step 3 — Stochastic Gradient Ascent Step

We wish to minimise the squared TD error $\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{2} \delta_t^2$. Taking the gradient with respect to $Q(s_t, a_t)$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Q(s_t, a_t)} = -\delta_t$$

### Step 4 — Update Rule via Gradient Descent

Performing a single gradient descent step with learning rate $\alpha$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(s_t, a_t) &\leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Q(s_t, a_t)} \\
&= Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \cdot \delta_t \\
&= Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \big[\,r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Convergence Guarantee (Watkins & Dayan, 1992):** Under the conditions that (i) all state-action pairs are visited infinitely often, (ii) $\alpha_t$ satisfies the Robbins–Monro conditions $\sum \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_t^2 < \infty$, and (iii) $\gamma \in [0, 1)$, the Q-Learning iterates converge to the optimal $Q^{*}(s, a)$ with probability 1.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Policy Gradient Theorem (REINFORCE)

### Step 1 — Define the Performance Objective

$$J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_{\theta}}\big[\,G_0\big] = \int P(\tau \mid \theta)\, G(\tau)\, d\tau$$

where $\tau = (s_0, a_0, s_1, a_1, \dots)$ is a trajectory.

### Step 2 — Differentiate Under the Integral Sign

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) &= \int \nabla_{\theta} P(\tau \mid \theta)\, G(\tau)\, d\tau \\
&= \int P(\tau \mid \theta)\, \nabla_{\theta} \log P(\tau \mid \theta)\, G(\tau)\, d\tau \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_{\theta}} \big[\, \nabla_{\theta} \log P(\tau \mid \theta)\, G(\tau)\,\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

where we used the *log-derivative trick* $\nabla \log P = P^{-1} \nabla P$.

### Step 3 — Decompose the Trajectory Log-Probability

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log P(\tau \mid \theta) &= \log \Big( P(s_0) \prod_{t=0}^{T-1} \pi_{\theta}(a_t \mid s_t) P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t) \Big) \\
&= \log P(s_0) + \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \log \pi_{\theta}(a_t \mid s_t) + \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \log P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Take the Gradient

Only the policy term depends on $\theta$ (the environment dynamics $P$ are $\theta$-independent):

$$\nabla_{\theta} \log P(\tau \mid \theta) = \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a_t \mid s_t)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute and Rearrange

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) &= \mathbb{E}_{\tau} \left[ \left( \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a_t \mid s_t) \right) G(\tau) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Causality (Reward-to-Go) Refinement

Since action $a_t$ cannot affect rewards before time $t$, we replace $G(\tau)$ with $G_t = \sum_{k=t}^{T-1} \gamma^{k-t} r_{k+1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) &= \mathbb{E}_{\tau} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a_t \mid s_t)\, G_t \right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{\tau} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a_t \mid s_t) \sum_{k=t}^{T-1} \gamma^{k-t} r_{k+1} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final REINFORCE Update Rule:** $\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \, \nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a_t \mid s_t)\, G_t$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in CPS:** Policy-gradient methods handle **continuous action spaces** (motor torques, voltages, throttle angles) directly, whereas Q-Learning requires discretisation. This is why REINFORCE, DDPG, PPO, and SAC are preferred for robotic CPS.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Value-Based vs. Policy-Based RL

| **Criterion** | **Value-Based (Q-Learning, DQN)** | **Policy-Based (REINFORCE, PPO)** |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Action-values $Q(s,a)$ | Direct policy $\pi_{\theta}(a \mid s)$ |
| Action Space | Discrete (or discretised) | Continuous and discrete |
| Convergence | Off-policy, strong guarantees | On-policy, high variance |
| Sample Efficiency | High (with experience replay) | Low (needs many trajectories) |
| CPS Use Case | Discrete control (on/off valves, gear shifts) | Motor control, autonomous driving |
| Exploration Mechanism | $\varepsilon$-greedy, Boltzmann | Entropy bonus, stochastic policy |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Q-Learning on a 3-State CPS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **KTU-typical 7-mark problem**: a small grid-world where each cell represents a discrete state of a CPS (e.g., a temperature controller with three modes: LOW / NORMAL / HIGH).

**Setup:**
* States: $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$
* Actions: $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2\}$
* Learning rate: $\alpha = 0.5$
* Discount factor: $\gamma = 0.9$
* Initial Q-table:

| | $a_1$ | $a_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0 | 0 |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 0 |
| $s_3$ | 0 | 0 |

**Experience tuple:** $(s_t, a_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1}) = (s_1, a_2, r = 5, s_2)$

**Step 1 — Compute the TD Target:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Target} &= r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_2, a') \\
&= 5 + 0.9 \cdot \max(0, 0) \\
&= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the TD Error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= 5 - Q(s_1, a_2) = 5 - 0 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Update the Q-Value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(s_1, a_2) &\leftarrow Q(s_1, a_2) + \alpha \cdot \delta \\
&= 0 + 0.5 \cdot 5 = 2.5
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Updated Q-table after one step:**

| | $a_1$ | $a_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0 | **2.5** |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 0 |
| $s_3$ | 0 | 0 |

> **Examiner's Insight:** This 3-step numerical procedure is the *exact* template KTU expects in 14-mark numerical problems. Practise it with at least two iterations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop in CPS

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders the **canonical RL-CPS feedback loop** — a high-yield diagram for KTU 2-mark and 7-mark questions. Note that all node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are plain text (no markdown formatting inside quotes).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[RL Agent on Edge Processor] -- selects action a_t --> A2[Cyber Controller Layer]
    A2 -- command signal --> A3[Physical Actuator]
    A3 -- applies force torque or voltage --> A4[CPS Plant Dynamics]
    A4 -- state evolution s_t to s_t+1 --> A5[Physical Sensors]
    A5 -- measurement y_t --> A6[State Estimator and Filter]
    A6 -- state estimate s_hat_t --> A1
    A4 -- exposes --> A7[Reward Computation Module]
    A7 -- reward r_t --> A1
    A1 -- updates --> A8[Policy Network pi_theta]
    A8 -- parameter update via gradient --> A1
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Information flows *clockwise*. The agent issues a control command based on the estimated state. The physical plant evolves. Sensors observe the new state. The reward module evaluates how "good" the transition was. The agent then updates its policy.

## 4.2 RL Algorithm Taxonomy for CPS Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Reinforcement Learning]
    R1 --> R2[Model Free Methods]
    R1 --> R3[Model Based Methods]
    R2 --> R4[Value Based]
    R2 --> R5[Policy Based]
    R2 --> R6[Actor Critic]
    R4 --> R7[Q Learning]
    R4 --> R8[Deep Q Network DQN]
    R5 --> R9[REINFORCE]
    R5 --> R10[Trust Region Policy Optimization]
    R6 --> R11[Advantage Actor Critic A2C]
    R6 --> R12[Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient DDPG]
    R3 --> R13[World Model Learning]
    R3 --> R14[MPC with Learned Dynamics]
    R7 --> C1[Discrete Action CPS]
    R8 --> C2[High Dimensional Perception CPS]
    R9 --> C3[Continuous Control Robots]
    R10 --> C3
    R11 --> C4[Locomotion and Manipulation]
    R12 --> C5[Autonomous Driving]
    R13 --> C6[Sample Efficient Industrial CPS]
    R14 --> C7[Predictive Control of Power Systems]
```

> **Engineering Use:** Choose **value-based** for discrete state-action CPS (e.g., traffic signal control). Choose **policy-gradient / actor-critic** for continuous control CPS (e.g., drones, robotic arms). Choose **model-based RL** when physical samples are expensive (e.g., semiconductor manufacturing, fusion reactors).

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RL-Enabled CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Physical Layer] --> L2[Plant Sensors]
    L2 --> L3[Signal Conditioning and ADC]
    L3 --> L4[State Estimator Kalman Filter]
    L4 --> L5[Cyber Decision Layer]
    L5 --> L6[RL Policy Network Inference Engine]
    L6 --> L7[Action Post Processor and Safety Shield]
    L7 --> L8[Control Signal Generation DAC]
    L8 --> L9[Physical Actuators]
    L9 --> L1
    L5 --> L10[Reward Calculator and Performance Logger]
    L10 --> L11[Experience Replay Buffer]
    L11 --> L12[Training Module Gradient Updates]
    L12 --> L13[Policy Network Parameter Sync]
    L13 --> L6
    L5 --> L14[Communication Network WiFi 5G TSN]
    L14 --> L15[Cloud or Edge Training Cluster]
    L15 --> L12
```

> **Block-Level Reading:** The architecture cleanly separates (a) the **real-time inference path** (L1 → L9) which must operate at millisecond latency, and (b) the **asynchronous training path** (L10 → L15) which can tolerate higher latency. This *separation of concerns* is the hallmark of production-grade RL-CPS deployments (e.g., Waymo, Boston Dynamics, Tesla FSD).

## 4.4 Exploration–Exploitation Trade-off Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[State s_t Observed] --> P2{Q Value Table Available}
    P2 -- Yes and prob 1 minus eps --> P3[Exploit argmax Q]
    P2 -- Yes and prob eps --> P4[Explore Random Action]
    P2 --> P5[Apply Annealing Schedule eps decays over episodes]
    P5 --> P2
    P3 --> P6[Execute Action in CPS Plant]
    P4 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Observe r_t and s_t+1]
    P7 --> P8[Update Q s a using TD error]
    P8 --> P2
```

> **Practical Note:** In real CPS, $\varepsilon$ is not held constant. It is **annealed** — starting high (e.g., $\varepsilon_0 = 1.0$) for broad exploration and decaying (e.g., $\varepsilon_T = 0.01$) for fine exploitation. A common schedule is $\varepsilon_t = \max(\varepsilon_{\min}, \varepsilon_0 \cdot \eta^t)$ with decay rate $\eta \in (0, 1)$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Q1. Define Reinforcement Learning in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. List its key components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Reinforcement Learning (RL) in CPS is a machine-learning paradigm in which an *agent* learns an optimal *control policy* by interacting with a *physical environment* through a perception–action loop, with the goal of maximising a cumulative scalar *reward* signal.
Key components (½ mark each): **(i)** Agent, **(ii)** Environment, **(iii)** State Space $\mathcal{S}$, **(iv)** Action Space $\mathcal{A}$, **(v)** Reward Signal $r_t$, **(vi)** Policy $\pi$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**Q2. What is the Markov Decision Process (MDP)? Why is the Markov property important in RL-based CPS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An MDP is a 5-tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ that formally models sequential decision-making where outcomes are partly random and partly under the agent's control. The **Markov property** states that the future state $s_{t+1}$ depends only on the current state $s_t$ and action $a_t$, not on the history. In CPS, this property (1 mark) reduces the state-space dimensionality (1 mark) and enables recursive Bellman formulations (1 mark), making RL computationally tractable.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (as per KTU ESE pattern):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the pair. Each question carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts.*

### ⭐ QUESTION A — Value-Based RL in CPS (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the Bellman Optimality Equation for the action-value function $Q^{*}(s, a)$ in a finite MDP. Explain the physical significance of each term with respect to a CPS control application such as a DC motor speed regulator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CPS is modelled as a 4-state MDP with the transition and reward data given below. Using Q-Learning with $\alpha = 0.5$ and $\gamma = 0.8$, perform **two** complete update steps starting from an all-zero Q-table, given the experience tuples: $(s_1, a_1, r=2, s_3)$ and $(s_3, a_2, r=4, s_4)$. **(7 marks)**

| $s$ | $a$ | $s'$ | $R$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | $a_1$ | $s_3$ | 2 |
| $s_3$ | $a_2$ | $s_4$ | 4 |
| $s_4$ | $a_1$ | $s_4$ | 0 |

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Definition of Optimal Q-Function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{*}(s, a) &= \max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \big[\, G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \,\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the Return:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_t &= r_{t+1} + \gamma\, r_{t+2} + \gamma^2\, r_{t+3} + \dots \\
&= r_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Take Expectation over Stochastic Transition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{*}(s, a) &= \mathbb{E}\big[\, r_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1} \mid s, a \,\big] \\
&= \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \big[\, R(s, a, s') + \gamma\, \mathbb{E}[G_{t+1} \mid s']\,\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Optimality Argument:**

At state $s'$, the optimal future return is $\max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')$. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{*}(s, a) &= \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \big[\, R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical Significance (CPS — DC Motor):**
* $R(s, a, s')$ — Immediate error reduction (negative speed error $\times$ torque applied).
* $P(s' \mid s, a)$ — Motor's dynamic response: how a torque input $a$ drives the rotor from speed $s$ to $s'$.
* $\gamma$ — Weighing present tracking accuracy vs. future settling behaviour.
* $\max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')$ — Greedy torque choice at the next state to minimise long-term energy + error cost.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Initial Q-table:** All entries = 0. Parameters: $\alpha = 0.5$, $\gamma = 0.8$.

**Update 1 — Tuple $(s_1, a_1, r=2, s_3)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_1 &= r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_3, a') - Q(s_1, a_1) \\
&= 2 + 0.8 \cdot \max(0, 0) - 0 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(s_1, a_1) &\leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \cdot 2 = 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Update 2 — Tuple $(s_3, a_2, r=4, s_4)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_2 &= 4 + 0.8 \cdot \max(Q(s_4, a_1), Q(s_4, a_2)) - Q(s_3, a_2) \\
&= 4 + 0.8 \cdot \max(0, 0) - 0 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(s_3, a_2) &\leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \cdot 4 = 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Q-table after 2 updates:**

| | $a_1$ | $a_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | **1.0** | 0 |
| $s_3$ | 0 | **2.0** |
| $s_4$ | 0 | 0 |

> **Note:** Other entries ($s_1, a_2$), ($s_3, a_1$), and all $s_4$ entries remain zero because no experience has been observed for them.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — Policy-Based RL in CPS (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain the limitations of value-based RL methods for continuous-action CPS applications. Derive the Policy Gradient Theorem from first principles and show how the REINFORCE update rule emerges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic manipulator in a CPS uses a stochastic policy $\pi_{\theta}(a \mid s)$ parameterised by $\theta$. After one trajectory, the observed return is $G = 10$ and $\nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a \mid s) = [0.2, -0.5, 0.1]^{\top}$. With learning rate $\alpha = 0.01$, compute the parameter update. Comment on the variance issue of REINFORCE and how a baseline can mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Limitations of value-based RL (2 marks):**
* Cannot handle continuous action spaces without explicit discretisation.
* Discretisation causes the *curse of dimensionality* in high-dim CPS (e.g., 7-DoF robotic arm → $3^7$ action cells).
* No natural way to inject domain knowledge or constraints.

**Derivation of the Policy Gradient Theorem (5 marks):**

Performance objective:

$$J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_{\theta}}[G_0] = \int P(\tau \mid \theta)\, G(\tau)\, d\tau$$

Differentiate using the log-derivative trick:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) &= \int P(\tau \mid \theta) \nabla_{\theta} \log P(\tau \mid \theta) \, G(\tau) \, d\tau \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{\tau}\big[\nabla_{\theta} \log P(\tau \mid \theta)\, G(\tau)\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

Expand the trajectory log-probability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log P(\tau \mid \theta) = \log P(s_0) + \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \log \pi_{\theta}(a_t \mid s_t) + \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \log P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

Only the policy term depends on $\theta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) &= \mathbb{E}_{\tau}\left[ \left( \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a_t \mid s_t) \right) G(\tau) \right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{\tau}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a_t \mid s_t) \, G_t \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The last line (using reward-to-go $G_t$ instead of $G(\tau)$) is the **REINFORCE gradient estimate**.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Parameter Update:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \theta &= \alpha \cdot G \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a \mid s) \\
&= 0.01 \cdot 10 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ -0.5 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.02 \\ -0.05 \\ 0.01 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\text{new}} &= \theta_{\text{old}} + \Delta \theta = \theta_{\text{old}} + [0.02, -0.05, 0.01]^{\top}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Variance Issue & Baseline (2 marks):**
* The REINFORCE estimator has **high variance** because the return $G$ is a noisy Monte-Carlo sample.
* Introduce a **baseline** $b(s)$ that does not depend on $a$. The modified gradient is $\mathbb{E}[\nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a \mid s)(G_t - b(s_t))]$.
* A common choice is $b(s) = V^{\pi}(s)$. The term $A(s, a) = G_t - V(s_t)$ is the **advantage function**, which dramatically reduces variance while preserving the unbiasedness of the gradient.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the log-derivative trick:** Students often write $\nabla P$ directly. The trick is *essential* — it is the heart of the Policy Gradient Theorem. (Lose 2–3 marks.)
> 2. **Using $G(\tau)$ instead of $G_t$:** Technically the unbiased estimator uses the full return, but for variance reduction, *reward-to-go* $G_t$ is preferred. KTU accepts both, but commenting on causality earns a bonus mark.
> 3. **Conflating REINFORCE with Actor-Critic:** REINFORCE is *pure policy-gradient* (Monte-Carlo). Actor-Critic uses a learned value function as a baseline. Mixing them is a common error.
> 4. **Not normalising rewards in CPS implementations:** Real rewards (e.g., energy in Joules) span huge ranges. Always normalise $r_t \in [-1, 1]$ to prevent gradient explosion. KTU often has a 1-mark sub-question on this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid- Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam:**

- [x] **RL in CPS** = Agent learns a control policy by closed-loop interaction with a physical plant to maximise cumulative reward.
- [x] **Six Pillars:** Agent, Environment, State, Action, Reward, Policy ($\pi$).
- [x] **MDP 5-tuple:** $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$. The Markov property makes the future depend only on the present.
- [x] **Discount Factor $\gamma \in [0, 1)$:** Ensures mathematical convergence; closer to 1 → far-sighted agent.
- [x] **Return:** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}$.
- [x] **Value Function $V^{\pi}(s)$:** Expected return from $s$ under $\pi$.
- [x] **Q-Function $Q^{\pi}(s, a)$:** Expected return from $s$ taking $a$ under $\pi$.
- [x] **Bellman Optimality (Q):** $Q^{*}(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')]$.
- [x] **Q-Learning Update:** $Q \leftarrow Q + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)]$.
- [x] **Q-Learning is OFF-POLICY** (uses $\max$ over next actions, not the action actually taken).
- [x] **SARSA is ON-POLICY:** Update uses $Q(s', a')$ where $a'$ is actually executed.
- [x] **Exploration–Exploitation:** $\varepsilon$-greedy is the standard. Anneal $\varepsilon$ from 1.0 to 0.01.
- [x] **Policy Gradient Theorem:** $\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}[\nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a \mid s) G_t]$.
- [x] **REINFORCE** is pure policy-gradient; **Actor-Critic** uses value baseline $b(s) = V(s)$.
- [x] **Continuous actions → policy-gradient methods (DDPG, PPO, SAC).** **Discrete actions → value-based (DQN).**
- [x] **Reward shaping** is critical: badly designed rewards cause *reward hacking* in physical systems.
- [x] **Sample efficiency:** Model-based RL > Model-free RL. Important when physical experiments are costly.
- [x] **Safety in CPS:** Real-world RL needs *constrained MDPs* (CMDP) and *safe exploration* techniques.
- [x] **Architecture pattern:** Real-time inference on edge; asynchronous training on cloud/edge cluster.
- [x] **Standard exam traps:** (i) Mixing Bellman Expectation vs Optimality; (ii) Forgetting the $\max$ in Q-Learning; (iii) Confusing on-policy vs off-policy.

> **End of Notes — Module 1: Reinforcement Learning in CPS**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CPS Security and Privacy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT604 - ADVANCED CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CPS Security and Privacy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is CPS Security?

**Cyber-Physical System (CPS) Security** is the disciplined engineering of confidentiality, integrity, availability, authenticity, and non-repudiation guarantees across the tightly coupled cyber (computation, communication) and physical (sensing, actuation, control) layers of a system whose failure can cause real-world physical consequences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Aligned to Module 2 Syllabus):**
> CPS Security refers to the set of mechanisms, models, and policies that protect the **sensing–computation–communication–actuation chain** of a CPS against malicious intent, accidental misuse, and environmental faults, while preserving real-time correctness, safety, and operational continuity.

**The Five Core Security Properties (CIAAN Triad):**

| Property | Symbol | Meaning in CPS Context |
|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality | C | Sensor/control data is disclosed only to authorized entities |
| Integrity | I | Sensor readings, control commands, and firmware are not tampered with |
| Availability | A | Real-time actuation, sensing, and control loops meet their deadlines |
| Authenticity | Au | Every node, packet, and command is verifiably from a trusted source |
| Non-Repudiation | N | An actuator or controller cannot later deny issuing a command |

### 1.2 What is CPS Privacy?

**CPS Privacy** is the preservation of personally identifiable, behavioral, locational, and contextual information generated by the pervasive sensing fabric of a CPS, ensuring that lawful usage does not translate into unauthorized surveillance, profiling, or inference about individuals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Privacy vs. Security Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **Security** is about *protecting the system* from attackers.
> - **Privacy** is about *protecting the user* from the system (and from observers of the system's data).
> - A perfectly *secure* CPS can still be a *privacy disaster* if it leaks data legally to a cloud provider.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Connected Hospital ICU"

Imagine a modern **Intensive Care Unit (ICU)** as a CPS:

- **Physical layer** = patient body, infusion pump, ventilator
- **Cyber layer** = bedside monitor, hospital network, electronic health record
- **Sensors** = SpO₂ probe, ECG electrodes
- **Actuators** = motorized IV pump, oxygen valve
- **Controller** = closed-loop drug delivery algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Analogy Made Concrete:**
> - If a **hacker spoofs** the SpO₂ reading (a **sensor attack**), the pump may deliver a lethal dose of a vasopressor. This is why *integrity* of sensor data is life-critical — far beyond ordinary IT security.
> - If the **ventilator's firmware** is maliciously updated (a **supply-chain attack**), physical harm occurs even if the network is encrypted.
> - If the patient's **heart-rate variability** is streamed to an insurance company (a **privacy breach**), the patient is harmed even though the system never failed.

This single analogy captures why CPS security is a *safety-grade* problem, not a *data-grade* problem.

### 1.4 Why Security and Privacy Are *Harder* in CPS than in Classical IT

| Classical IT System | Cyber-Physical System |
|---|---|
| Failure → data loss | Failure → **physical injury, death, environmental damage** |
| Patches are routine | Patches can **break real-time control loops** |
| Devices are powerful (PCs, servers) | Devices are **resource-constrained** (MCUs, RTUs) |
| Network is IP/Ethernet | Network is **heterogeneous**: CAN, Modbus, WirelessHART, 5G, BLE |
| Latency is "fast enough" | Latency is **bounded by physics** (ms level) |
| Encryption is cheap | Encryption is **energy/latency expensive** on edge nodes |
| Lifetime: 3–5 years | Lifetime: **15–30 years** (cars, grids, turbines) |
| Users are skilled | Users are **operators, drivers, patients** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Frequently Tests:** *"Why cannot classical IT security solutions be directly applied to CPS?"* — the answer should reference at least three of the rows above, emphasizing **real-time deadlines, resource constraints, and physical consequences**.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics in CPS Security (Must Memorize)

- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** expected time until a system component fails.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** expected time to restore after failure.
- **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR** (Mean Time Between Failures).
- **Attack Surface:** the sum of all points (*entry points, channels, interfaces*) where an unauthorized actor can attempt to inject, extract, or manipulate data.
- **Attack Vector:** the *path or means* by which an attacker gains unauthorized access.
- **Threat Model:** a structured description of adversary capabilities, assets, and goals.
- **Zero-Day:** a vulnerability unknown to the defender for which no patch exists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Risk Metric (Used in CPS Risk Assessment, e.g., IEC 62443):**
>
> $$\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$$
>
> All three factors are rated on a qualitative scale (e.g., **Low = 1, Medium = 2, High = 3**), giving Risk ∈ {1, …, 27}.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The CPS Attack Surface — Layered View

A CPS is exposed to attacks at **seven distinct layers** (high-yield for KTU diagrams):

1. **Perception/Sensing Layer** — physical sensors, RFID, smart meters
2. **Edge/Fog Layer** — gateways, RTUs, PLCs, embedded controllers
3. **Network Layer** — wireless (ZigBee, LoRa, 5G), wired (CAN, Profibus), routing
4. **Middleware/Cloud Layer** — data ingestion, stream processing, storage
5. **Application/Service Layer** — dashboards, ML analytics, control apps
6. **Actuation Layer** — motors, valves, relays, robotic arms
7. **Supply-Chain Layer** — firmware, hardware, semiconductors, OSS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7-Layer Rule:** A defender must protect *all seven* layers. An attacker only needs to compromise *one*. This asymmetry (defender: 7, attacker: 1) is the **fundamental security dilemma** of CPS.

### 2.2 Threat Modeling — STRIDE (Industry Standard, KTU-Favorite)

**STRIDE** is a mnemonic for the six categories of threats developed at Microsoft and is the de-facto standard for CPS threat modeling:

| Letter | Threat | Property Violated | CPS Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S** | **Spoofing** | Authenticity | Forging a CAN frame from a fake ECU |
| **T** | **Tampering** | Integrity | Modifying set-points in a SCADA PLC ladder logic |
| **R** | **Repudiation** | Non-repudiation | A drone denies issuing the "land" command |
| **I** | **Information Disclosure** | Confidentiality | Eavesdropping on a smart meter to profile a household |
| **D** | **Denial of Service** | Availability | Jamming a vehicle's V2X channel to disable collision avoidance |
| **E** | **Elevation of Privilege** | Authorization | A sensor node gaining root on the gateway |

### 2.3 Common CPS Attack Vectors (Module 2, High Yield)

#### 2.3.1 Sensor-Level Attacks
- **False Data Injection (FDI):** attacker injects crafted sensor values that bypass bad-data detectors.
- **Sensor Spoofing:** GPS spoofing of a UAV; acoustic spoofing of a voice-controlled CPS.
- **Replay Attack:** retransmitting old valid sensor readings to fool a controller.
- **Side-Channel Attack (SCA):** exploiting power, EM, or timing leakage from an embedded device to extract cryptographic keys.

#### 2.3.2 Network-Level Attacks
- **Man-in-the-Middle (MitM):** intercepting/modifying traffic between sensor and controller.
- **Eavesdropping / Sniffing:** passive capture of wireless traffic.
- **Jamming:** RF interference (DoS at physical layer).
- **Routing Attack (e.g., sinkhole, wormhole):** in wireless sensor networks.
- **Protocol Exploitation:** exploiting weaknesses in CAN bus arbitration, Modbus' lack of authentication, or DNP3's cleartext.

#### 2.3.3 Actuator-Level Attacks
- **Command Injection:** sending an unauthorized "open valve" or "disable brake" command.
- **Actuator Saturation:** forcing actuator beyond physical limits to cause wear or damage.
- **Coordinated Multi-Node Attack:** simultaneous corruption of many actuators to defeat redundancy.

#### 2.3.4 System-Level / Cyber-Physical Coupling Attacks
- **Stuxnet-style attack:** altering PLC logic so centrifuges spin at destructive speeds while reporting normal values to the HMI.
- **Time Synchronization Attack:** corrupting PTP/NTP to desynchronize distributed controllers.
- **Cascade Attack:** one compromised node destabilizes the whole grid (e.g., 2015 Ukraine power grid attack).

### 2.4 Defense Mechanisms — The "Defense-in-Depth" Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defense-in-Depth (DiD):** No single security control is sufficient. Multiple, independent, overlapping layers of defense are deployed so that a breach in one layer does not lead to system compromise.

The five canonical defense layers in CPS:

1. **Preventive Controls:** authentication, encryption, secure boot, code signing, firewalls
2. **Detective Controls:** intrusion detection systems (IDS), anomaly detection, SIEM
3. **Responsive Controls:** isolation/quarantine, fail-safe shutdown, fail-operational mode
4. **Recovery Controls:** rollback to safe state, redundancy, hot-swap
5. **Forensic Controls:** logging (tamper-evident), audit trails, chain-of-custody

### 2.5 CPS Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) — KTU Favorite

| Type | Mechanism | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Signature-based (S-IDS)** | Matches against known attack patterns (Snort rules) | Low false positive | Cannot detect zero-days |
| **Anomaly-based (A-IDS)** | Learns a model of "normal"; flags deviations | Detects zero-days | High false positives |
| **Specification-based** | Compares behavior to formal CPS spec (e.g., sensor range) | Low false positive, CPS-aware | Requires rigorous spec |
| **Stateful / Physics-aware** | Checks consistency with physical equations (e.g., $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$) | Detects stealthy FDI | Computationally heavier |
| **ML/Deep-learning based** | LSTM, Autoencoder, GAN for time-series | High accuracy with training data | Adversarial ML attacks |

### 2.6 Privacy in CPS — Threat Categories

| Privacy Threat | Description | CPS Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Identity Disclosure** | User's real identity is revealed | Smart meter links household to occupant |
| **Location Tracking** | Trajectory of a user/asset is reconstructed | Connected car GPS stream to insurer |
| **Behavioral Profiling** | Habits, routines, lifestyle inferred | Smart-home sensor fusion reveals sleep pattern |
| **Inferring Sensitive Attributes** | Health, religion, politics deduced from raw data | Voice assistant reveals medical conditions |
| **Linkability** | Two pseudonymous records joined to re-identify | Wearable + public transit card |
| **Data Retention Misuse** | Long-term stored data is repurposed | Hospital CPS data sold to third party |

### 2.7 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Notation Convention:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown tables. The KTU Formula Sheet below covers all high-yield equations for Module 2 Security & Privacy.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Range | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk Score | $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Qualitative 1–3 each → 1–27 | IEC 62443 risk assessment |
| 2 | Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits | Information leakage quantification |
| 3 | Conditional Entropy | $H(X \mid Y) = -\sum_{x,y} p(x,y) \log_2 p(x \mid y)$ | bits | Privacy leakage $\vert H(X) - H(X \mid Y) \vert$ |
| 4 | Mutual Information | $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y)$ | bits | How much Y reveals about X |
| 5 | Differential Privacy (Laplace) | $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$ | $\varepsilon$ = privacy budget | Add calibrated noise for privacy |
| 6 | Sensitivity | $\Delta f = \max_{D, D'} \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert_1$ | depends on $f$ | Laplace mechanism calibration |
| 7 | AES Strength (symmetric) | $2^k$ where $k$ = key length in bits | bits | AES-128 = $2^{128}$, AES-256 = $2^{256}$ |
| 8 | RSA Strength | $\approx 2^{n/2}$ via GNFS (asymptotic) | $n$ = modulus bits | RSA-2048 ≈ 112-bit symmetric equivalent |
| 9 | Hash Collision Probability (birthday) | $P \approx 1 - e^{-k^2 / (2N)}$ | dimensionless | Finding collisions in $N$-bit hash with $k$ trials |
| 10 | Authentication Delay Bound | $T_{\text{auth}} \le T_{\text{deadline}} - T_{\text{proc}}$ | seconds | Real-time CPS auth design |
| 11 | Attack Success Probability | $P_{\text{succ}} = 1 - (1 - p)^n$ where $p$ = single-trial prob., $n$ = trials | dimensionless | Brute-force / replay analysis |
| 12 | Jamming Margin (wireless) | $M_j = P_{\text{jam}} / (P_{\text{tx}} / G_p)$ | dB | Wireless DoS analysis |
| 13 | Channel Capacity (Shannon) | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bits/s | Secure comm. throughput bound |
| 14 | Expected Loss | $E[L] = \sum_i P_i \cdot C_i$ | currency / safety units | Cyber-physical risk economics |
| 15 | k-Anonymity | dataset has $\ge k$ indistinguishable records | integer | Privacy of microdata releases |
| 16 | $\varepsilon$-Differential Privacy | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\varepsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$ | $\varepsilon \in (0,1]$ small | Gold-standard privacy definition |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grids:** IEC 62351 standard mandates the formulas of confidentiality, integrity, and authentication for power-system communication; Risk score (row 1) drives NERC CIP compliance.
- **Automotive (ISO/SAE 21434):** Uses Threat = 1–5, Impact = 1–5, Attack Feasibility = 1–5, Risk Value = f(T, I, AF). Cybersecurity is now a *type-approval* requirement for new cars in the EU.
- **Medical Devices (FDA pre-market guidance, IEC 80001-2-4):** Safety risk × Security risk are jointly computed for infusion pumps and pacemakers.
- **Industrial IoT (IEC 62443):** Defines Security Levels (SL 0–4) mapping directly to attacker capability, motivating the formula sheet above.
- **Privacy Law (GDPR Article 25, 32):** $\varepsilon$-differential privacy and $k$-anonymity are *evidentiary* mechanisms used to argue "appropriate technical measures."

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** When a question asks *"How would you secure a smart-grid CPS?"* the model answer should mention: **(1) IEC 62351 protocol security, (2) PMU false-data-injection detection via state-estimation, (3) hardware root-of-trust for substation IEDs, (4) defense-in-depth across the 7 layers above.**
