# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Format:** Short-answer questions testing definitions, comparisons, and direct recall. Answers should fit in **3–4 sentences** or a short table.

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Differentiate between Symmetric and Asymmetric Key Cryptography. Give one example algorithm for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Symmetric Key | Asymmetric Key |
|---|---|---|
| Keys Used | Single shared secret key K | Key pair: public key PU and private key PR |
| Speed | Fast (100–1000× faster) | Slow (modular exponentiation) |
| Key Distribution Problem | Yes — must share K securely | No — public key is openly distributed |
| Example Algorithm | **AES-256**, 3DES, ChaCha20 | **RSA-2048**, ECC, ElGamal |
| Primary Use in CPS | Bulk sensor data encryption | Digital signatures, key exchange |

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]

**What is a cryptographic hash function? List any three of its essential properties.**

**Model Answer:**
A cryptographic hash function $H$ maps an arbitrary-length input $M$ to a fixed-length output $h$ of size $n$ bits, such that $h = H(M)$. It is a **one-way function** used to verify data integrity.

**Three Essential Properties:**
1. **Pre-image Resistance:** Given $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find any $M$ such that $H(M) = h$.
2. **Second Pre-image Resistance:** Given $M_1$, it is infeasible to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.
3. **Collision Resistance:** It is infeasible to find any two distinct inputs $M_1, M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

**Example:** SHA-256 produces a 256-bit digest and satisfies all three properties.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The KTU pattern includes a **module-level internal choice** — students answer **one of two** alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the RSA Algorithm in Detail. Perform RSA Encryption and Decryption for $p = 7, q = 11, e = 13, M = 5$. Show All Steps.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the modulus:**

$$
n = p \times q = 7 \times 11 = 77
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Euler's Totient:**

$$
\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 6 \times 10 = 60
$$

**Step 3 — Verify Public Exponent:** $\gcd(13, 60) = 1$ ✓ (13 is coprime to 60)

**Step 4 — Compute Private Exponent $d$:** Solve $13d \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$.

Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$
60 = 4 \times 13 + 8
$$
$$
13 = 1 \times 8 + 5
$$
$$
8 = 1 \times 5 + 3
$$
$$
5 = 1 \times 3 + 2
$$
$$
3 = 1 \times 2 + 1
$$

Back-substituting:

$$
1 = 3 - 1 \times 2
$$
$$
1 = 3 - 1 \times (5 - 1 \times 3) = 2 \times 3 - 1 \times 5
$$
$$
1 = 2 \times (8 - 1 \times 5) - 1 \times 5 = 2 \times 8 - 3 \times 5
$$
$$
1 = 2 \times 8 - 3 \times (13 - 1 \times 8) = 5 \times 8 - 3 \times 13
$$
$$
1 = 5 \times (60 - 4 \times 13) - 3 \times 13 = 5 \times 60 - 23 \times 13
$$

Therefore:

$$
d \equiv -23 \equiv 60 - 23 = 37 \pmod{60}
$$

**Public Key:** $(n=77, e=13)$; **Private Key:** $d=37$.

**Step 5 — Encryption:** $C = M^{e} \bmod n = 5^{13} \bmod 77$.

Successive squaring:

$$
5^{1} \equiv 5 \pmod{77}
$$
$$
5^{2} = 25 \pmod{77}
$$
$$
5^{4} \equiv 25^{2} = 625 \equiv 625 - 8 \times 77 = 625 - 616 = 9 \pmod{77}
$$
$$
5^{8} \equiv 9^{2} = 81 \equiv 4 \pmod{77}
$$

Since $13 = 8 + 4 + 1$:

$$
5^{13} = 5^{8} \times 5^{4} \times 5^{1} \equiv 4 \times 9 \times 5 = 180 \equiv 180 - 2 \times 77 = 26 \pmod{77}
$$

**Ciphertext: $C = 26$**.

**Step 6 — Decryption:** $M = C^{d} \bmod n = 26^{37} \bmod 77$.

(Applying successive squaring modulo 77 yields $M = 5$, confirming correctness.)

**Verification:**

$$
26^{37} \bmod 77 = 5
$$

Thus, decryption correctly recovers the original plaintext $M = 5$. ✓

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange Algorithm. Identify Its Main Vulnerability and Propose a Countermeasure.

**Model Solution:**

The **Diffie–Hellman (DH) Key Exchange** allows two parties, Alice and Bob, to derive a shared secret key $K$ over an insecure channel without prior key sharing.

**Public Parameters (Known to All):** A large prime $p$ and a generator $g$ (a primitive root modulo $p$).

**Protocol Steps:**

1. **Alice** chooses a private random integer $a$, computes $A = g^{a} \bmod p$, and sends $A$ to Bob.
2. **Bob** chooses a private random integer $b$, computes $B = g^{b} \bmod p$, and sends $B$ to Alice.
3. **Alice** computes the shared secret: $K = B^{a} \bmod p$.
4. **Bob** computes the shared secret: $K = A^{b} \bmod p$.
5. Both arrive at the same value $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$.

**Security Basis:** The hardness of the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** — given $g, p, A = g^{a} \bmod p$, computing $a$ is infeasible for large $p$.

**Main Vulnerability — Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack:**

A malicious entity Mallory can intercept $A$ and $B$, replace them with her own values $A' = g^{a'} \bmod p$ and $B' = g^{b'} \bmod p$, and establish two independent shared secrets — one with Alice and one with Bob — thereby reading and modifying all traffic transparently.

**Countermeasure — Authenticated DH (e.g., Station-to-Station Protocol):**

- Both parties **digitally sign** their DH public values using long-term private keys (e.g., **ECDSA signatures**).
- Signatures are verified using each party's **X.509 certificate** issued by a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)**.
- Alternatively, use **ECDH with mutual authentication** embedded in protocols like **TLS 1.3** where certificates are exchanged during the handshake.

This binds the DH exchange to authenticated identities, preventing impersonation by a MITM.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Describe the AES Algorithm. List the Four Main Transformations Applied in Each Round and Explain the Purpose of Each.

**Model Solution:**

**Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)** is a symmetric block cipher standardized by NIST in **FIPS PUB 197** in 2001. AES operates on **128-bit blocks** and supports key sizes of **128, 192, or 256 bits** (10, 12, or 14 rounds respectively). The 128-bit block is organized as a 4×4 matrix of bytes called the **State**.

**The Four Main Transformations in Each AES Round:**

**1. SubBytes (Substitution)**
- Each byte of the State is replaced by its corresponding value in a fixed 16×16 lookup table called the **S-Box**.
- The S-Box is constructed by taking the **multiplicative inverse** of each byte in the finite field $\text{GF}(2^{8})$ (modulo the irreducible polynomial $m(x) = x^{8} + x^{4} + x^{3} + x + 1$), followed by an **affine transformation** over $\text{GF}(2)$.
- **Purpose:** Provides **non-linearity**, which is essential to resist **linear** and **differential cryptanalysis**.

**2. ShiftRows (Permutation)**
- Row 0 of the State is unchanged; Row 1 is cyclically shifted left by 1 byte; Row 2 by 2 bytes; Row 3 by 3 bytes.
- **Purpose:** Provides **diffusion** by spreading the influence of individual bytes across the entire State, ensuring ciphertext bits depend on many plaintext bits.

**3. MixColumns (Mixing)**
- Each column of the State is treated as a 4-byte vector and multiplied by a fixed $4 \times 4$ matrix over $\text{GF}(2^{8})$.
- The matrix multiplication mixes the four bytes in each column.
- **Purpose:** Provides strong **diffusion** within columns; guarantees that a change in one byte affects all four bytes of the column after one round.

**4. AddRoundKey (Key Mixing)**
- The State is XORed with a 128-bit **round key** $K^{r}$, which is derived from the original key via a deterministic **Key Expansion** schedule.
- **Purpose:** Introduces **confusion** by incorporating the secret key into the ciphertext; without the key, the transformation is irreversible.

**Note:** The **final round** (round 10 for AES-128) omits the MixColumns step.

**Decryption:** Each transformation has a mathematically defined inverse — InvSubBytes, InvShiftRows, InvMixColumns, InvAddRoundKey (which is identical to AddRoundKey since XOR is its own inverse).

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the Digital Signature Standard (DSS) and the RSA Signature Scheme with a Neat Diagram. Compare Their Security Properties.

**Model Solution:**

**Digital Signature Standard (DSS):**
DSS is a U.S. federal standard (FIPS 186) for digital signatures. The most widely deployed variant is the **Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA)**, with the modern preferred form being **Elliptic Curve DSA (ECDSA)**.

**RSA Signature Scheme:**

**Key Generation:** Same as RSA encryption — generate modulus $n = pq$, choose $e$, compute $d$.

**Signing a Message $M$:**
1. Compute the hash: $h = H(M)$ (using SHA-256).
2. Apply PKCS#1 v1.5 padding: form the padded encoded message $\text{EM}$.
3. Sign with private key: $S = (\text{EM})^{d} \bmod n$.

**Verification:**
1. Recover the padded hash: $\text{EM}' = S^{e} \bmod n$.
2. Compare the hash portion of $\text{EM}'$ with $H(M)$. If they match, the signature is valid.

**ECDSA Signature Scheme:**

1. **Key Generation:** Choose elliptic curve parameters; select random private key $d_{priv}$; compute public key $Q = d_{priv} \cdot G$, where $G$ is the generator point.
2. **Signing:** Compute $h = H(M)$, choose random nonce $k$, compute point $(x_1, y_1) = k \cdot G$, set $r = x_1 \bmod n$, compute $s = k^{-1}(h + r \cdot d_{priv}) \bmod n$. Signature is $(r, s)$.
3. **Verification:** Compute $u_1 = h \cdot s^{-1} \bmod n$, $u_2 = r \cdot s^{-1} \bmod n$, then $(x_1, y_1) = u_1 \cdot G + u_2 \cdot Q$. If $x_1 \equiv r \pmod{n}$, the signature is valid.

**Comparison of Security Properties:**

| Property | RSA Signatures | ECDSA Signatures |
|---|---|---|
| Underlying Hard Problem | Integer Factorization | Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm (ECDLP) |
| Key Size for 128-bit security | 3072 bits | 256 bits |
| Signature Size | 3072 bits (for 3072-bit key) | 512 bits (two 256-bit values) |
| Signing Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Verification Speed | Faster | Slower |
| Non-Repudiation | Yes | Yes |
| Quantum Vulnerability | High (Shor's algorithm) | High (Shor's algorithm) |
| Preferred CPS Use | Server-side, fixed infrastructure | Resource-constrained IoT, V2X, smart cards |

**Neat Diagram of the RSA Signing Process:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Original Message M] --> H[Hash Function SHA-256]
    H --> Hv[Hash Value h]
    Hv --> Pad[PKCS1 Padding]
    Pad --> EM[Encoded Message EM]
    EM --> SignMod[Modular Exponentiation: S = EM^d mod n]
    SignMod --> Sig[Digital Signature S]
    M --> Bundle[Bundle M and S for Transmission]
    Sig --> Bundle
    Bundle --> Recv[Receiver]
    Recv --> Hash2[Recompute h prime = SHA256 of M]
    Bundle --> Verify[Verifier computes EM prime = S^e mod n]
    Verify --> Cmp{Compare h prime with hash portion of EM prime}
    Cmp -- Match --> OK[Signature Valid]
    Cmp -- Mismatch --> Fail[Signature Rejected]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **For RSA, students often confuse $e$ and $d$ in encryption vs. signing.** Remember: encryption uses the **receiver's public key**; signing uses the **sender's private key**. Mixing them leads to a complete loss of marks.
> 2. **For Diffie–Hellman, students frequently forget to state the MITM vulnerability explicitly** and the need for authentication. KTU examiners specifically look for the word "authentication" or "digital signatures" in the answer.
> 3. **For AES, students commonly miss that the *final* round does NOT include MixColumns.** This is a 1-mark differentiator in KTU valuation.
> 4. **For hash functions, the three properties (pre-image, second pre-image, collision resistance) must be named correctly and in that order** — examiners check the precise terminology.
> 5. **Numerical errors in RSA are heavily penalized.** Always recheck $\phi(n)$ and the Extended Euclidean steps. A wrong $d$ is fatal to the rest of the answer.
> 6. **Citing outdated algorithms (MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4) without flagging them as insecure** loses marks. KTU 2024 explicitly tests awareness of deprecated primitives.
> 7. **Neglecting to mention the avalanche effect and entropy in a hash-function question** is a common 2-mark deduction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2**

- [ ] **CIA-N** model is the bedrock: Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-repudiation.
- [ ] **Symmetric** (AES, ChaCha20) is fast but has a key-distribution problem.
- [ ] **Asymmetric** (RSA, ECC) solves key distribution but is slow.
- [ ] **Hybrid encryption** (asymmetric to exchange a symmetric key) is the universal pattern in modern protocols.
- [ ] **RSA Security** rests on the difficulty of factoring $n = p \cdot q$ for large primes.
- [ ] **Diffie–Hellman** Security rests on the Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP).
- [ ] **ECDH/ECDSA** offer equivalent security to RSA with much smaller key sizes — ideal for CPS.
- [ ] **Hash functions** (SHA-256, SHA-3) provide integrity; **HMAC** adds authentication.
- [ ] **Digital signatures** provide all four CIA-N properties.
- [ ] **AES** has 10/12/14 rounds for 128/192/256-bit keys; final round omits MixColumns.
- [ ] **Avalanche effect** — flipping one input bit changes ~50% of output bits.
- [ ] **PKI with X.509 certificates** is the standard mechanism for binding identity to public keys in CPS.
- [ ] **TLS 1.3 / DTLS 1.3** is the dominant transport security layer; **IPsec** for network-layer VPNs; **MACsec** for link-layer.
- [ ] **Quantum threat:** RSA, ECC, and DH are all broken by Shor's algorithm → migrate to **post-quantum cryptography (PQC)** such as **Kyber, Dilithium** (NIST PQC standards).
- [ ] **CPS-specific considerations:** real-time deadlines, resource constraints, safety-criticality, and long device lifecycles dictate protocol choice.
- [ ] **De facto CPS standards:** OPC UA (industrial), IEC 62351 (smart grid), V2X PKI (automotive), MQTT over TLS (IoT).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Privacy in CPS: Privacy Concerns and Regulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT604 - ADVANCED CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Privacy in CPS: Privacy Concerns and Regulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Privacy in CPS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, **Privacy** is formally defined as the controlled, authorized, and context-aware management of personally identifiable information (PII), sensor data, behavioral traces, and operational telemetry generated by the seamless integration of computational algorithms and physical processes. Per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *PBCPT604 – Advanced Cyber Physical Systems*, privacy in CPS is not a singular construct but a **multi-dimensional engineering objective** encompassing *data confidentiality*, *identity unlinkability*, *location indistinguishability*, and *behavioral opacity* against adversaries operating across the cyber, physical, and human strata.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> Privacy in CPS is treated as a **first-class design constraint** alongside reliability, safety, and real-time performance. It addresses the leakage vector that emerges when sensor fusion, IoT telemetry, and actuation loops persistently emit traceable signals into the cyber domain.

Mathematically, privacy in CPS is characterized by the **indistinguishability** of a system’s observable output distributions with respect to any single individual’s data contribution. This is expressed as:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2) \in S]
$$

where $\mathcal{M}$ is the privacy-preserving mechanism, $D_1$ and $D_2$ are *adjacent datasets* differing by a single record, $S$ is any subset of the output range, and $\varepsilon$ is the **privacy budget** (a dimensionless scalar, typically $\varepsilon \in [0, 1]$ for strong guarantees).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a smart city in Kerala equipped with thousands of IoT sensors monitoring traffic, pollution, and electricity grids. A commuter, *Anjali*, drives through 47 sensors on her way to work. Each sensor logs her **license plate, speed, GPS coordinates, and timestamps**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The “Crowded Elevator” Principle**
> Anjali’s privacy is preserved only when her individual footprint is **lost in the crowd** of other commuters. If the city uses a *blinding layer* (a privacy mechanism), her traces are mixed with hundreds of others so that no observer — not the cloud server, not a hacker, not even the system administrator — can confidently say “Anjali was here.”

In CPS terms, this means **transforming raw sensor data** so that:
- **Identity** (who?) is hidden via anonymization
- **Presence** (where?) is hidden via location obfuscation
- **Behavior** (what?) is hidden via differential privacy noise
- **Linkability** (when?) is hidden via temporal decoupling

## 1.3 Engineering Metrics for CPS Privacy

The following **standard privacy metrics** must be memorized by every KTU student tackling this module:

| **Metric** | **Symbol** | **Ideal Value** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Privacy Budget | $\varepsilon$ | $\leq 1$ | Lower = stronger privacy, weaker utility |
| K-Anonymity Set | $k$ | $\geq 5$ | Minimum cohort size for indistinguishability |
| L-Diversity | $l$ | $\geq 3$ | Diversity within equivalence classes |
| T-Closeness | $t$ | $\leq 0.2$ | Distribution distance to global |
| Entropy Loss | $H(X \vert Y)$ | High | Information leaked about $X$ given $Y$ |
| Mutual Information | $I(X;Y)$ | Low | Statistical dependence between data and output |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:**
> Examiner questions frequently demand *justification* of threshold values. Remember that **$\varepsilon \leq 1$ is “strong privacy”**, while **$\varepsilon \in [1, 10]$ is “weak/utility-favoured privacy.”**

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Deconstruction — The Privacy Concern Stack in CPS

Privacy threats in CPS do not originate from a single layer; they emerge from the **convergence** of cyber and physical sensing. The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies these into **five orthogonal concern families**:

### Layer 1: Identity Concerns
- **Persistent Identifier Leakage:** MAC addresses, RFID tags, IMEI numbers.
- **Re-identification Attacks:** Linking anonymized sensor logs to public datasets.
- **Biometric Exposure:** Wearable CPS devices (smartwatches, ECG patches) emit unique physiological fingerprints.

### Layer 2: Location Concerns
- **Trajectory Reconstruction:** Aggregating GPS, cellular, and Wi-Fi probes.
- **Proximity Tracking:** Bluetooth beacons in retail, healthcare, smart buildings.
- **Geofence Inference:** Deduce home/work routines from actuation timestamps (e.g., smart thermostat cycles).

### Layer 3: Behavioral Concerns
- **Lifestyle Inference:** Energy usage patterns reveal sleep cycles, occupancy, religious practices.
- **Health Inference:** Smart toilet sensors reveal medical conditions; gait sensors reveal mobility disorders.
- **Social Graph Inference:** Who-meets-whom from autonomous vehicle coordination messages.

### Layer 4: Aggregation & Inference Concerns
- **Linkage Attacks:** Combining CPS streams with social media or open government data.
- **Mosaic Effect:** Each individual data point is benign, but their **union** exposes secrets.
- **Side-Channel Leakage:** Power consumption, electromagnetic emanations, and acoustic signatures of CPS devices.

### Layer 5: Lifecycle Concerns
- **Consent Decay:** Permissions granted at deployment rarely reflect long-term use.
- **Data Retention:** Sensors store indefinitely without explicit erasure.
- **Secondary Use:** Data collected for traffic optimization repurposed for law enforcement surveillance.

## 2.2 Global Privacy Regulation Framework (KTU 2024 Mandatory)

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates familiarity with **six landmark regulations** that govern CPS deployment worldwide. Engineers must understand not just *what* the law says but *how it constrains the technical architecture*.

| **Regulation** | **Region** | **Year** | **Core CPS-Relevant Mandate** | **Key Technical Impact** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| GDPR | European Union | 2018 | Lawful basis, right to erasure, data minimization | Mandates Privacy-by-Design in IoT/CPS |
| CCPA / CPRA | California, USA | 2020 / 2023 | Consumer right to know, delete, opt-out of sale | Requires CPS data inventory maps |
| HIPAA | United States | 1996 | Protected Health Information (PHI) in medical CPS | Encrypts medical IoT telemetry |
| PIPEDA | Canada | 2000 | Consent-based processing | Affects cross-border CPS data flows |
| IT Act (Sec 43A, 72A) | India | 2000 / 2008 | Compensation for negligence, imprisonment for breach | Drives CPS vendor accountability |
| DPDP Act | India | 2023 | Data Principal Rights, fiduciary obligations | Strict consent + cross-border transfer rules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GDPR Article 25 — Privacy by Design and by Default:**
> This is the *single most cited clause* in KTU examination answers. The Article legally obligates CPS engineers to embed privacy controls **into the system architecture itself**, not as an after-thought layer.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Privacy Mathematics

The following equations form the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for this topic. Every student should derive these from first principles before the test.

### Formula 1: Differential Privacy (ε-DP)

A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}: \mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{R}$ satisfies **ε-differential privacy** if for all datasets $D_1, D_2$ differing in at most one element, and all $S \subseteq \mathcal{R}$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2) \in S]
$$

### Formula 2: Laplace Mechanism Noise Scale

To achieve ε-DP for a query with sensitivity $\Delta f$, add **Laplace noise** scaled as:

$$
\sigma_{Laplace} = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}
$$

The Laplace PDF is:

$$
f(x \mid \mu, b) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert x - \mu \vert}{b}\right), \quad b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}
$$

### Formula 3: K-Anonymity Cohort Size

A dataset is **k-anonymous** if every quasi-identifier tuple appears at least $k$ times:

$$
\forall \, q \in \mathcal{Q}: \quad \vert \{r \in \mathcal{D} : q(r) = q\} \vert \geq k
$$

### Formula 4: L-Diversity Condition

An equivalence class is **l-diverse** if it contains at least $l$ “well-represented” values for the sensitive attribute:

$$
\forall \, E \in \mathcal{E}: \quad \vert \pi_{S}(E) \vert \geq l
$$

where $\pi_{S}(E)$ is the projection onto the sensitive attribute $S$.

### Formula 5: T-Closeness (Earth Mover’s Distance)

An equivalence class $E$ satisfies **t-closeness** if:

$$
d_{EMD}\!\left(P_{S \vert E}, \, P_{S}\right) \leq t
$$

### Formula 6: Mutual Information Leakage

$$
I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \vert Y) = \sum_{x,y} p(x,y) \log \frac{p(x,y)}{p(x)\,p(y)}
$$

Lower $I(X;Y)$ implies **stronger privacy**.

### Formula 7: Composition Theorem (Sequential Queries)

For $n$ sequential ε-DP mechanisms:

$$
\varepsilon_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \varepsilon_i \quad \text{(basic composition)}
$$

For advanced composition (Dwork et al.):

$$
\varepsilon_{total} = \sqrt{2 n \ln(1/\delta)} \cdot \varepsilon + n \varepsilon \cdot \frac{e^{\varepsilon} - 1}{e^{\varepsilon} + 1}
$$

### Formula 2.8 — Boundary Conditions & Sanity Checks

| **Parameter** | **Range / Bound** | **Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|
| $\varepsilon$ | $(0, \infty)$ | $0$ = absolute privacy (impossible), $\infty$ = no privacy |
| $k$ | $\mathbb{Z}^+, k \geq 2$ | $k = 1$ means no anonymization |
| $l$ | $\mathbb{Z}^+, l \leq \vert E \vert$ | Diversity must fit class size |
| $t$ | $[0, 1]$ | $0$ = identical distributions |
| $\Delta f$ | $\mathbb{R}^+$ | Query sensitivity (L1 / L2 norm) |
| $I(X;Y)$ | $[0, \min(H(X), H(Y))]$ | Measured in **bits** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of These Equations:**
> - *Smart grid load forecasting*: Uses Laplace mechanism with $\varepsilon = 0.5$ to mask individual household consumption.
> - *Contact tracing apps (e.g., Aarogya Setu)*: Employs k-anonymity with $k = 21$ for proximity alert aggregation.
> - *Autonomous vehicle V2X*: Applies t-closeness to obfuscate driving trajectories from roadside observers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Differential Privacy Laplace Mechanism

**Problem Setup:** A CPS smart-meter network publishes aggregate hourly energy consumption. Each query asks: *“What is the total energy used in district X in the last hour?”* The published answer must be **ε-differentially private** so that any single household’s data cannot be inferred.

**Step 1 — Define the Query Function:**

Let $f: \mathcal{D} \to \mathbb{R}$ be the aggregate query:

$$
f(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

where $x_i$ is the hourly energy consumption of household $i$ in district $X$.

**Step 2 — Compute the Global Sensitivity $\Delta f$:**

The global sensitivity of $f$ is the maximum change in output when one record is added or removed:

$$
\Delta f = \max_{D_1, D_2 : \vert D_1 \triangle D_2 \vert \leq 1} \vert f(D_1) - f(D_2) \vert
$$

Since each household contributes at most $x_{max}$ kWh (e.g., $x_{max} = 10$ kWh for residential):

$$
\Delta f = \max_i \vert x_i \vert = x_{max} = 10 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Choose the Laplace Mechanism:**

The privacy-preserving output is:

$$
\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \eta, \quad \eta \sim \text{Laplace}(0, b)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Scale Parameter $b$:**

To satisfy ε-DP, the Laplace noise scale must equal sensitivity divided by privacy budget:

$$
b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} = \frac{10}{\varepsilon} \text{ kWh}
$$

For $\varepsilon = 0.5$ (strong privacy):

$$
b = \frac{10}{0.5} = 20 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Write the Probability Ratio:**

The Laplace density is:

$$
f_{Lap}(x \mid b) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert x \vert}{b}\right)
$$

For an observed output $o \in S$, the probability under datasets $D_1$ and $D_2$ is:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) = o] = \frac{1}{2b}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert o - f(D_1) \vert}{b}\right)
$$

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2) = o] = \frac{1}{2b}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert o - f(D_2) \vert}{b}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Establish the Ratio Bound:**

By the triangle inequality:

$$
\vert o - f(D_1) \vert - \vert o - f(D_2) \vert \leq \vert f(D_1) - f(D_2) \vert \leq \Delta f = \varepsilon b
$$

Therefore:

$$
\exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert o - f(D_1) \vert}{b}\right) \leq \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert o - f(D_2) \vert}{b} + \varepsilon\right)
$$

**Step 7 — Combine the Probability Ratio:**

$$
\frac{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) = o]}{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2) = o]} = \exp\!\left(\frac{\vert o - f(D_2) \vert - \vert o - f(D_1) \vert}{b}\right) \leq e^{\varepsilon}
$$

Hence:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2) \in S]
$$

**Step 8 — Final Interpretation:**

The smart-meter network publishes:

$$
\mathcal{M}(D) = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}_{\text{true aggregate}} + \underbrace{\text{Lap}(0, 20)}_{\text{privacy noise (kWh)}}
$$

For district $X$ with true total $f(D) = 1{,}450$ kWh, the published value could be $1{,}468$ kWh or $1{,}431$ kWh — *the noise hides any single household’s exact contribution*.

## 3.2 Derivation — K-Anonymity Equivalence Class Construction

**Step 1 — Identify Quasi-Identifiers (QI):**
For a CPS smart-building dataset, QIs are: `{age_range, zip_prefix, gender, building_floor}`.

**Step 2 — Group Records by QI-Tuple:**

Each record $r \in \mathcal{D}$ has a QI-vector $q(r)$. Form equivalence classes:

$$
E_j = \{r \in \mathcal{D} : q(r) = q_j\}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the K-Anonymity Condition:**

For k-anonymity, every equivalence class must satisfy:

$$
\forall j: \quad \vert E_j \vert \geq k
$$

**Step 4 — Generalization Hierarchy:**

To enforce $k \geq 5$, replace `age_range = "25-30"` with `age_range = "20-40"` until each group has $\geq 5$ members.

**Step 5 — Information Loss Metric:**

The information loss for an equivalence class $E_j$ with $d$ QI attributes, where attribute $a$ is generalized from $V_a$ original values to $V_a^{gen}$ values:

$$
IL(E_j) = \sum_{a=1}^{d} \frac{\vert V_a \vert - \vert V_a^{gen} \vert}{\vert V_a \vert}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Dataset Information Loss:**

$$
IL_{total} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D} \vert} \sum_{j} \vert E_j \vert \cdot IL(E_j)
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Privacy Mechanisms in CPS

| **Mechanism** | **Privacy Guarantee** | **Utility Cost** | **Computational Overhead** | **Best CPS Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| K-Anonymity | Membership hiding | Moderate | Low (suppression / generalization) | Smart home occupancy logs |
| L-Diversity | Attribute hiding | Moderate-High | Low | Healthcare wearables |
| T-Closeness | Distribution hiding | High | Moderate | Smart grid consumption patterns |
| Laplace (ε-DP) | ε-bounded leakage | High (small datasets) | Low | Statistical CPS data publishing |
| Gaussian (ε, δ-DP) | Relaxed DP | Moderate | Low | ML model training in CPS |
| Homomorphic Encryption | Cryptographic | Very High | Very High | Outsourced CPS computation |
| Federated Learning | Decentralized DP | Low-Moderate | Moderate | Autonomous vehicle fleets |
| Secure Multiparty Computation | Cryptographic | High | High | Multi-utility grid coordination |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> In practice, modern CPS deployments use **hybrid mechanisms** — e.g., *k-anonymity at the edge* + *ε-DP at the cloud* — to balance latency (edge requirement) and statistical rigor (cloud requirement).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> *“Define privacy in Cyber-Physical Systems. List any four privacy concerns unique to CPS deployments.”*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Privacy in CPS is the controlled management of personally identifiable sensor telemetry, behavioral traces, and actuation metadata such that no adversary — internal or external — can infer the identity, location, activity, or social graph of any individual from observable system outputs, while preserving functional utility.

**Four CPS-Specific Concerns (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Trajectory Reconstruction:** GPS, cellular, and V2X messages allow continuous tracking of individuals through smart city infrastructure.
2. **Lifestyle Inference:** Smart meters reveal occupancy patterns, sleep cycles, and religious practices through energy usage profiles.
3. **Biometric Exposure:** Wearable CPS devices (ECG patches, fitness bands) emit unique physiological fingerprints.
4. **Mosaic Effect:** Combining seemingly benign CPS streams (e.g., thermostat + lighting + door sensors) reconstructs intimate household routines.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

> *“Explain the concept of differential privacy. Why is it preferred over traditional anonymization in CPS?”*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Differential privacy is a *mathematical framework* guaranteeing that the inclusion or exclusion of any single record in a dataset causes only a *bounded* change (controlled by privacy budget $\varepsilon$) in the output distribution of any analytical query. Formally:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2) \in S]
$$

**Why Preferred Over Traditional Anonymization (1.5 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Provable Guarantee:** DP provides *mathematically rigorous* privacy; k-anonymity can be defeated by auxiliary information.
2. **Composability:** Sequential queries can be budgeted without losing track of cumulative leakage.
3. **Robustness to Linkage:** DP prevents the mosaic effect by ensuring no single record materially influences output.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *“Privacy Concerns in CPS”*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> *“Discuss in detail the major privacy concerns arising in CPS deployments. Categorize them across cyber, physical, and human layers with suitable examples.”*

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Cyber-Physical Systems integrate sensing, computation, communication, and actuation. This convergence creates privacy concerns that transcend those in traditional IT systems because the **physical world itself becomes a data source**.

**Cyber Layer Concerns (2 Marks):**
- **Data Collection Concerns:** CPS devices continuously collect granular data (e.g., smart meters record energy usage every 15 minutes).
- **Data Transmission Concerns:** Wireless CPS communication (Zigbee, LoRa, BLE) can be intercepted, exposing unencrypted telemetry.
- **Storage Concerns:** Cloud-stored CPS data attracts breaches; the 2017 *Verkada* incident exposed 150,000 camera feeds.

**Physical Layer Concerns (2 Marks):**
- **Sensor Privacy:** Cameras, microphones, and motion detectors capture environmental data beyond user expectation.
- **Side-Channel Leakage:** Acoustic emanations of 3D printers reveal printed objects; power analysis reveals encryption keys.
- **Actuator Inference:** Smart-lock actuation logs reveal user movement schedules.

**Human Layer Concerns (2 Marks):**
- **Behavioral Inference:** Smart home data reveals daily routines, medical conditions, and personal relationships.
- **Consent Erosion:** Users grant blanket consent at device purchase without understanding long-term data flows.
- **Vulnerable Populations:** Children, elderly, and patients using medical CPS face heightened exposure.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Effective CPS privacy engineering requires a *layered defense* spanning all three planes. The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates **Privacy-by-Design** as the foundational architectural principle, ensuring privacy is enforced at each abstraction level rather than retrofitted after deployment.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> *“With neat diagrams, explain the Privacy-by-Design framework for a smart healthcare CPS. Identify the GDPR articles that govern its operation.”*

**Model Solution:**

**Smart Healthcare CPS Architecture (3 Marks):**

| **Layer** | **Component** | **Privacy Mechanism** |
|---|---|---|
| Wearable Layer | ECG, BP, SpO2 sensors | Local k-anonymization ($k=10$) |
| Edge Layer | Smartphone gateway | Differential privacy noise ($\varepsilon = 0.5$) |
| Cloud Layer | Hospital analytics server | Homomorphic encryption |
| Audit Layer | Compliance dashboard | GDPR Article 30 records |

**GDPR Articles Governing Healthcare CPS (2 Marks):**

| **Article** | **Mandate** | **Healthcare CPS Application** |
|---|---|---|
| Article 9 | Special category data (health) | Requires *explicit consent* for ECG processing |
| Article 25 | Privacy by Design | Embeds anonymization into firmware |
| Article 32 | Security of processing | Mandates encryption of PHI in transit |
| Article 17 | Right to erasure | Patient can demand deletion of all telemetry |
| Article 35 | DPIA requirement | Mandatory before deploying AI diagnostics |

**Operational Flow (1 Mark):**
1. Sensor captures raw ECG $\rightarrow$ 2. Edge applies DP noise $\rightarrow$ 3. Encrypted upload to hospital cloud $\rightarrow$ 4. Diagnostic AI runs on encrypted data $\rightarrow$ 5. Audit log records every access $\rightarrow$ 6. Patient dashboard allows erasure requests.

**Justification (1 Mark):**
This architecture ensures **data minimization, purpose limitation, and storage limitation** — the three pillars of GDPR compliance — while preserving the *real-time diagnostic capability* essential for medical CPS.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *“Privacy Regulations and Mechanisms”*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> *“Compare GDPR, CCPA, and the Indian DPDP Act 2023 in terms of their applicability to CPS deployments. Which regulation imposes the strictest cross-border data transfer rules?”*

**Model Solution:**

**Comparative Analysis Table (4 Marks):**

| **Dimension** | **GDPR (EU)** | **CCPA / CPRA (USA)** | **DPDP Act 2023 (India)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Territorial Scope | EU residents, any processor | California residents | Indian residents, global processors offering goods/services in India |
| Lawful Basis | 6 explicit bases (consent, contract, etc.) | Opt-out model | Consent + legitimate uses |
| Data Principal Rights | Access, erasure, portability, object | Know, delete, opt-out, correct | Access, correction, erasure, grievance, nomination |
| Penalty Maximum | €20M or 4% global turnover | $7,500 per intentional violation | ₹250 Crore |
| CPS-Specific Provision | Article 25 (Privacy by Design) | IoT-specific cybersecurity law (SB-327) | Digital Personal Data Protection Rules 2025 |
| Cross-Border Rules | Adequacy decisions, SCCs, BCRs | No federal cross-border limit | Blacklist of countries, government approval |

**Strictest Cross-Border Regime (2 Marks):**
The **GDPR** historically imposed the strictest cross-border data transfer regime through its *adequacy decision* mechanism. However, the **DPDP Act 2023** introduces an *even more restrictive* approach by empowering the Central Government to **publish a blacklist of countries** to which data transfer is entirely prohibited, and additionally requiring government approval for transfers even to non-blacklisted jurisdictions.

**Engineering Implication (1 Mark):**
CPS architects deploying IoT products globally must implement *geo-fencing*, *data localization*, and *consent-driven routing engines* to comply with all three regimes simultaneously. A multi-region deployment requires a **compliance-as-code** layer that dynamically enforces transfer rules based on the data subject's location.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> *“Derive the Laplace noise scale required to achieve ε-differential privacy for a CPS query with sensitivity $\Delta f = 5$ and privacy budget $\varepsilon = 0.5$. What is the noise standard deviation?”*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the Mechanism (1 Mark):**
The Laplace mechanism adds noise drawn from a zero-mean Laplace distribution with scale parameter $b$:

$$
\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \eta, \quad \eta \sim \text{Laplace}(0, b)
$$

**Step 2 — Sensitivity Definition (1 Mark):**

$$
\Delta f = \max_{D_1, D_2 : \vert D_1 \triangle D_2 \vert \leq 1} \vert f(D_1) - f(D_2) \vert = 5
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Scale Parameter (2 Marks):**

$$
b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} = \frac{5}{0.5} = 10
$$

**Step 4 — Relate to Standard Deviation (2 Marks):**
For a Laplace distribution with scale $b$, the variance and standard deviation are:

$$
\text{Var}(\text{Lap}(0, b)) = 2b^2
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{2} \cdot b = \sqrt{2} \cdot 10 = 10\sqrt{2} \approx 14.142
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer (1 Mark):**
The CPS query requires Laplace noise with scale $b = 10$ and standard deviation $\sigma \approx 14.142$. Any noise smaller than this scale would breach ε-DP; any larger would harm utility.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Privacy Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Anonymization with Encryption:** Anonymization *removes identity*; encryption *scrambles data*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for using these interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the Privacy Budget in Composition:** When multiple queries are made sequentially, students often forget $\varepsilon_{total} = \sum \varepsilon_i$. Always state the **basic composition theorem** explicitly.
> 3. **Missing Units in Numerical Answers:** The Laplace scale $b$ has the *same units as the query output*. Writing $b = 10$ without stating “kWh” or “units” loses 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Citing GDPR Article Numbers Incorrectly:** Article 25 is Privacy by Design; Article 32 is Security of Processing; Article 17 is Right to Erasure. Mixing these up is a frequent error.
> 5. **Ignoring the Utility-Privacy Trade-off:** Examiners award bonus marks to answers that *quantify* how increasing $\varepsilon$ reduces noise but weakens privacy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Privacy in CPS** is a *first-class design constraint*, not an add-on layer — enforced via Privacy-by-Design (GDPR Article 25).
- The **five concern families** are: Identity, Location, Behavioral, Aggregation/Inference, and Lifecycle.
- **Differential Privacy** provides a *mathematically provable* guarantee using the privacy budget $\varepsilon$; smaller $\varepsilon$ = stronger privacy.
- **Laplace mechanism** noise scale is $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$; standard deviation is $\sigma = b\sqrt{2}$.
- **K-Anonymity** requires every equivalence class to have $\geq k$ members based on quasi-identifiers.
- **L-Diversity** strengthens k-anonymity by ensuring diversity of sensitive attributes within each class.
- **T-Closeness** further restricts the distribution distance to global, preventing skewness attacks.
- **GDPR** imposes the broadest compliance burden, including extraterritorial scope and Privacy-by-Design mandates.
- **DPDP Act 2023** introduces government-approved cross-border data transfer rules and penalty up to ₹250 crore.
- **Federated Learning** + **Homomorphic Encryption** are emerging CPS privacy mechanisms that enable decentralized analytics without raw data exposure.
- **Sequential composition** of DP mechanisms accumulates privacy loss: $\varepsilon_{total} = \sum \varepsilon_i$.
- **Mutual information** $I(X;Y)$ quantifies leakage in *bits*; lower is better.
- Always state **units, sensitivity values, and privacy budget** in numerical answers.
- **Smart grid, healthcare CPS, and autonomous vehicles** are the three canonical use cases most often cited in KTU question papers.
- **Consent management, right to erasure, and audit logging** are the three operational pillars of GDPR-compliant CPS.
- The **mosaic effect** is the most insidious privacy threat in CPS because each individual data stream appears benign in isolation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Privacy-Preserving Techniques in Data Collection and Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT604 - ADVANCED CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Privacy-Preserving Techniques in Data Collection and Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Privacy-Preserving Techniques in Data Collection and Processing** refer to a suite of mathematical, cryptographic, and statistical methods engineered to allow meaningful computation, analysis, and learning from sensitive data while preventing the disclosure of personally identifiable information (PII), behavioural patterns, or proprietary records. In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, these techniques are critical because CPS ecosystems continuously generate high-velocity, high-dimensional sensor data (wearables, smart grids, autonomous vehicles, medical IoT) that must be processed without violating user confidentiality, regulatory mandates (GDPR, HIPAA, DPDP Act 2023), or operator secrecy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Privacy-preserving computation is the discipline of designing algorithms whose output distribution is provably independent (or weakly dependent) of any single individual's record, even when an adversary holds arbitrary auxiliary side-information.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Noisy Whisper in a Crowd"

Imagine you are in a hospital waiting room and a researcher wants to know the *average* blood pressure of patients. If you simply hand over your exact reading, anyone with the dataset can single you out. A **privacy-preserving system** works like this:

- You whisper your answer to a *translator* (the privacy mechanism).
- The translator intentionally **mumbles a little** — adding controlled, calibrated random noise — before speaking to the researcher.
- The researcher, after collecting whispers from **hundreds of patients**, still obtains a statistically accurate average (Law of Large Numbers), but **cannot reverse-engineer any single whisper** to identify your exact reading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examiners:** Privacy is *not* about hiding the data — it is about **hiding each individual's contribution** to the aggregate.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in CPS

| CPS Domain | Sensitive Data | Privacy Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Smart Healthcare (IoMT) | ECG, glucose, location | Patient re-identification, insurance fraud |
| Smart Grid | Household consumption | Lifestyle inference, burglary profiling |
| Autonomous Vehicles | Trajectories, LiDAR | Tracking, commercial espionage |
| Industrial IoT | Process parameters | Competitor reverse-engineering |
| Smart Cities | Pedestrian flow | Mass surveillance, political profiling |

## 1.4 The Four Pillars of Privacy-Preserving Computation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Privacy-Preserving Quadruple (memorise for KTU):**
> 1. **Statistical / Perturbation Methods** — Differential Privacy, k-Anonymity, l-Diversity, t-Closeness
> 2. **Cryptographic Methods** — Homomorphic Encryption, Secure Multi-Party Computation, Zero-Knowledge Proofs
> 3. **Distributed Learning Methods** — Federated Learning, Split Learning, Secure Aggregation
> 4. **Architectural / Hardware Methods** — Trusted Execution Environments (TEE), Secure Enclaves (SGX), Differential Privacy in hardware

The **privacy budget (ε)** is the *single most-tested parameter* in KTU board papers — a smaller ε means stronger privacy but lower utility.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Differential Privacy (DP)

### 2.1.1 Formal Mechanism

A randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies **ε-differential privacy** if for all adjacent datasets $D_1, D_2$ (differing by exactly one record) and all measurable output sets $S \subseteq \text{Range}(\mathcal{M})$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2) \in S]$$

**Intuition:** The multiplicative bound $e^{\varepsilon}$ constrains how much the output distribution can shift when one individual is added/removed. If ε is tiny, the output barely changes → strong privacy.

### 2.1.2 Key Properties

- **Composability (Sequential):** Applying mechanisms $\mathcal{M}_1$ (with $\varepsilon_1$) and $\mathcal{M}_2$ (with $\varepsilon_2$) on the same dataset yields $(\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2)$-DP.
- **Parallel Composition:** On *disjoint* subsets of data, the total cost is $\max(\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2)$.
- **Post-Processing Immunity:** Any function applied to a DP output remains DP — privacy cannot be eroded by downstream computation.
- **Group Privacy:** k records changing degrade privacy by factor $e^{k\varepsilon}$.

### 2.1.3 The Laplace Mechanism (workhorse of KTU problems)

For a numerical query $f : \mathcal{D} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^d$, the Laplace mechanism returns:

$$\mathcal{M}_{\text{Lap}}(D, f, \varepsilon) = f(D) + \eta, \quad \eta \sim \text{Lap}\!\left(0, \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)^d$$

where the **global sensitivity** $\Delta f$ is defined as:

$$\Delta f = \max_{D_1, D_2 \text{ adjacent}} \lVert f(D_1) - f(D_2) \rVert_1$$

### 2.1.4 The Gaussian Mechanism

For $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP (relaxed DP with a small failure probability δ):

$$\mathcal{M}_{\text{Gauss}}(D, f, \varepsilon, \delta) = f(D) + \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \sigma^2 I\right), \quad \sigma \geq \frac{\Delta f_2 \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}$$

### 2.1.5 The Exponential Mechanism

For non-numeric queries (e.g., selecting a category), score function $q(D, r)$ with sensitivity $\Delta q$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) = r] \propto \exp\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon \cdot q(D, r)}{2 \Delta q}\right)$$

## 2.2 k-Anonymity, l-Diversity, t-Closeness

### 2.2.1 k-Anonymity (Samarati & Sweeney, 1998)

A dataset $D$ is **k-anonymous** if every tuple is indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ other tuples with respect to the **quasi-identifier (QI)** attributes. Formally, every equivalence class of $D$ under QI-generalisation has size $\geq k$.

> [!NOTE]
> **QI vs SA vs NA:** Attributes are partitioned into Quasi-Identifiers (QIs), Sensitive Attributes (SAs), and Non-sensitive Attributes (NAs).

### 2.2.2 l-Diversity

A k-anonymous equivalence class is **l-diverse** if it contains at least $l$ *well-represented* values for every sensitive attribute. "Well-represented" is usually interpreted as **distinct l-diversity** (≥ l distinct SA values) or **entropy l-diversity** (entropy of SA distribution $\geq \log l$).

### 2.2.3 t-Closeness

A stricter refinement: the distribution of a sensitive attribute within any equivalence class must be at **Earth-Mover distance ≤ t** from the global distribution.

## 2.3 Homomorphic Encryption (HE)

A cryptosystem $(KeyGen, Enc, Dec, Eval)$ is **homomorphic** if:

$$\text{Dec}(sk, \text{Eval}(pk, \circ, \text{Enc}(m_1), \text{Enc}(m_2))) = m_1 \circ m_2$$

| Class | Supported Operations | Schemes | CPS Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PHE** (Partially HE) | One op (add or mul) | RSA, Paillier, ElGamal | Voting, aggregation |
| **SHE** (Somewhat HE) | Bounded add + mul | BGV, BFV | Lightweight ML |
| **FHE** (Fully HE) | Arbitrary circuits | CKKS, TFHE, FHEW | Encrypted neural nets |

## 2.4 Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)

Allows $n$ parties $P_1, \dots, P_n$ holding private inputs $x_1, \dots, x_n$ to jointly compute $f(x_1, \dots, x_n)$ such that no party learns anything beyond their own input and the output. Two foundational paradigms:

- **Yao's Garbled Circuits** — boolean circuits, constant-round.
- **Shamir's Secret Sharing** — polynomial-based $(t, n)$-threshold scheme:

$$s \;\longleftrightarrow\; p(x) = s + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \cdots + a_{t-1} x^{t-1} \pmod{p}$$

Any $t$ shares reconstruct $s$ via Lagrange interpolation; fewer than $t$ shares reveal nothing.

## 2.5 Federated Learning (FL)

The **FedAvg** algorithm (McMahan et al., 2017) operates in rounds:

$$w_{t+1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{n} w_{t+1}^{k}, \quad w_{t+1}^{k} = w_t - \eta_t \nabla \mathcal{L}_k(w_t)$$

where $w_t$ is the global model, $w_t^k$ is the local model on client $k$, and $\mathcal{L}_k$ is the local loss.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DP definition | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2) \in S]$ | ε = privacy budget (lower = stronger) |
| 2 | Laplace noise scale | $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$ | Real-valued scalar |
| 3 | Global sensitivity | $\Delta f = \max \lVert f(D_1) - f(D_2) \rVert_1$ | Count query: $\Delta f = 1$ |
| 4 | Gaussian σ | $\sigma = \Delta f_2 \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)} / \varepsilon$ | For $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP |
| 5 | Sequential composition | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum \varepsilon_i$ | Additive over same data |
| 6 | Parallel composition | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \max \varepsilon_i$ | Disjoint data subsets |
| 7 | Group privacy factor | $e^{k\varepsilon}$ | k records changed |
| 8 | k-anonymity | $\vert C \vert \geq k \;\; \forall C$ equivalence class | $k$ typically 5–20 |
| 9 | Entropy l-diversity | $-\sum p_i \log p_i \geq \log l$ | bits |
| 10 | Shamir reconstruction | $s = \sum_{j} y_j \prod_{i \neq j} \frac{x_i}{x_i - x_j}$ | Lagrange over $\mathbb{F}_p$ |
| 11 | FedAvg aggregation | $w_{t+1} = \sum_k (n_k/n) w_{t+1}^{k}$ | Weighted by dataset size |
| 12 | HE correctness | $\text{Dec}(sk, \text{Eval}(pk, \circ, c_1, c_2)) = m_1 \circ m_2$ | $c_i = \text{Enc}(pk, m_i)$ |
| 13 | Renyi DP divergence | $D_{\alpha}(P \Vert Q) \leq \varepsilon'$ | Tight composition |
| 14 | Communication of SMPC | $O(n^2 \cdot \text{depth})$ garbled | Yao's circuits |
| 15 | Central vs Local DP | Global vs per-device noise | Local weaker by $\sqrt{n}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The "count query sensitivity = 1" fact appears in **every** differential-privacy problem. Memorise it.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Proof — Laplace Mechanism Satisfies ε-Differential Privacy

**Theorem.** For any function $f : \mathcal{D} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^d$ with $\ell_1$-sensitivity $\Delta f$, the mechanism

$$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + Y, \quad Y \sim \text{Lap}(0, b)^d, \quad b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}$$

satisfies ε-differential privacy.

**Proof (line-by-line).**

The probability density of a $d$-dimensional Laplace random vector $Y \sim \text{Lap}(0, b)^d$ evaluated at point $y \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is

$$p_Y(y) = \prod_{i=1}^{d} \frac{1}{2b} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert y_i \vert}{b}\right) = \left(\frac{1}{2b}\right)^d \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lVert y \rVert_1}{b}\right).$$

For a measurable output set $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ and adjacent datasets $D_1, D_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) \in S] 
&= \int_{S} p_Y(s - f(D_1)) \, ds \\
&= \int_{S} \left(\frac{1}{2b}\right)^d \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lVert s - f(D_1) \rVert_1}{b}\right) ds.
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the analogous expression for $D_2$ and taking the ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) \in S]}{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2) \in S]} 
&= \frac{\int_S \exp\!\left(-\lVert s - f(D_1)\rVert_1 / b\right) ds}{\int_S \exp\!\left(-\lVert s - f(D_2)\rVert_1 / b\right) ds} \\
&\leq \sup_{s \in S} \frac{\exp\!\left(-\lVert s - f(D_1)\rVert_1 / b\right)}{\exp\!\left(-\lVert s - f(D_2)\rVert_1 / b\right)} \\
&= \sup_{s \in S} \exp\!\left(\frac{\lVert s - f(D_2) \rVert_1 - \lVert s - f(D_1) \rVert_1}{b}\right).
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the **reverse triangle inequality** $\lVert a \rVert - \lVert b \rVert \leq \lVert a - b \rVert$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lVert s - f(D_2)\rVert_1 - \lVert s - f(D_1)\rVert_1 
&\leq \lVert (s - f(D_2)) - (s - f(D_1)) \rVert_1 \\
&= \lVert f(D_1) - f(D_2) \rVert_1 \\
&\leq \Delta f.
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the supremum is bounded by $\exp(\Delta f / b)$. Substituting $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$:

$$
\frac{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) \in S]}{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2) \in S]} \leq \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\Delta f / \varepsilon}\right) = e^{\varepsilon}.
$$

Symmetrically, swapping $D_1 \leftrightarrow D_2$ gives the same bound, completing the proof. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Laplace Mechanism on Count Query

**Problem:** A hospital wants to publish the number of patients diagnosed with diabetes. Current true count is 142. The dataset has 10,000 records. Compute the noisy release under ε = 1.0 using the Laplace mechanism.

**Step 1 — Identify the query and sensitivity.**
The query is $f(D) = $ count of diabetic patients. Removing or adding one record changes the count by exactly 1. Therefore:

$$\Delta f = 1 \quad \text{(count queries always have sensitivity 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute noise scale.**

$$b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{1.0} = 1.0$$

**Step 3 — Draw Laplace noise.**
A realisation $Y \sim \text{Lap}(0, 1)$ could, for example, be $Y = +0.73$ (sampled from the distribution).

**Step 4 — Release noisy answer.**

$$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + Y = 142 + 0.73 = 142.73$$

The released statistic is 142.73. Note: the released value is a *real number*; in practice one rounds to the nearest integer (143) but this rounding is itself a **post-processing step** that preserves DP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Utility check:** If 1,000 such queries are answered with $\varepsilon = 0.01$ each, the total privacy budget is $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = 10$ — strongly violating reasonable privacy. This is why **budget accounting** is graded in KTU.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — k-Anonymity Check

**Problem:** Given the microdata table below with QI = {Age, ZIP}, determine $k$ and assess 4-anonymity.

| Age | ZIP | Disease |
|---|---|---|
| 25 | 11001 | Flu |
| 27 | 11001 | Cold |
| 29 | 11003 | Flu |
| 35 | 11003 | Fever |
| 36 | 11005 | Flu |
| 38 | 11005 | Cold |

**Step 1 — Build equivalence classes on raw QI.**
Each row has a unique (Age, ZIP) → equivalence class size = 1 → $k = 1$. **Fails** 4-anonymity.

**Step 2 — Generalise QI.**
Apply generalisation: Age → decade, ZIP → first 3 digits.

| Age* | ZIP* | Disease |
|---|---|---|
| 20s | 110 | Flu |
| 20s | 110 | Cold |
| 20s | 110 | Flu |
| 30s | 110 | Fever |
| 30s | 110 | Flu |
| 30s | 110 | Cold |

**Step 3 — Re-count equivalence classes.**
- Class 1: {20s, 110} → size 3
- Class 2: {30s, 110} → size 3

So $k = 3$ — still **fails** 4-anonymity. To achieve 4-anonymity, we must *suppress* (mask with *) at least one record or generalise more aggressively (e.g., Age to 20–39).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Technique | Privacy Guarantee | Utility | Computation Overhead | Communication Overhead | Best CPS Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Differential Privacy** | Mathematical, quantitative (ε) | Medium (tunable) | Low–Medium | Low | Statistical releases, census |
| **k-Anonymity** | Heuristic, syntactic | High | Low | Low | Tabular microdata |
| **l-Diversity** | Stronger than k-anon | Medium | Low | Low | Heterogeneous sensitive data |
| **t-Closeness** | Strongest of three | Lower | Medium | Low | Skewed SA distributions |
| **Homomorphic Encryption (FHE)** | Cryptographic (semantic sec.) | High | **Very High** (10⁶×) | High | Encrypted ML inference |
| **SMPC (Yao)** | Cryptographic | High | Medium | **Very High** (garbled) | Joint analytics, auctions |
| **Federated Learning** | Inferential (no raw data) | High | Medium | Medium | IoT model training |
| **TEE / SGX** | Hardware-rooted trust | High | Low | Low | Edge CPS gateways |
| **Synthetic Data (DP-GAN)** | Inherits DP guarantees | Variable | High | Medium | Sharing & benchmarking |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — End-to-End Privacy-Preserving CPS Data Pipeline

```mermaid
    E1 -->|Local Anonymization| Q1{QI Detection}

    Q1 -->|Add Laplace Noise| A2[DP-Noised Local Buffer]

    A1 -->|Encrypted Upload| SA1[Secure Aggregator]
    A2 -->|Encrypted Upload| SA1

    SA1 -->|Crypto Aggregation| SM1[Secure Multi-Party Compute]
    SM1 -->|Decrypted Aggregate| CS1[Cloud Server]

    RT1 -->|Predictions| AC1[Actuator Layer]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Differential Privacy Mechanism Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D[Dataset D] --> Q[Query Function f]
    Q --> TR[True Answer f of D]
    TR --> SENS[Sensitivity Calculator]

    CALC --> LPL[Laplace Sampler b]
    LPL --> NSE[Noise Vector eta]
    NSE --> ADD[Summation Node]
    TR --> ADD
    ADD --> OUT[Private Release M of D]
    OUT --> PP[Post-Processing]
    PP --> RL[Released Statistic]

    AD[Adversary] -.->|Side-Info Attack| OUT
    PP -.->|Cannot Reverse-Engineer| AD

    style D fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Federated Learning with Secure Aggregation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C1 as Client 1
    participant C2 as Client 2
    participant C3 as Client N
    participant SA as Secure Aggregator
    participant SV as Parameter Server

    SV->>C1: Send global model w_t
    SV->>C2: Send global model w_t
    SV->>C3: Send global model w_t

    par Local Training
        C1->>C1: Train on local data
        C2->>C2: Train on local data
        C3->>C3: Train on local data
    end

    C1->>SA: Masked gradient mask g1 of w
    C2->>SA: Masked gradient mask g2 of w
    C3->>SA: Masked gradient mask gN of w

    SA->>SA: Sum of masks equals zero
    SA->>SV: Aggregated update sum gi
    SV->>SV: w of t plus 1 equals w t plus eta sum gi

    SV-->>C1: Broadcast w of t plus 1
    SV-->>C2: Broadcast w of t plus 1
    SV-->>C3: Broadcast w of t plus 1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Decision Tree: Selecting a Privacy Technique

```mermaid

    ST -->|Distributed edge devices| A4[Federated Learning]
    ST -->|Publishing static microdata| A5[k-Anonymity or l-Diversity]

    A1 -->|Numeric statistics| A1a[Laplace or Gaussian]
    A1 -->|Non-numeric selection| A1b[Exponential Mechanism]

    A3 -->|Only addition| A3a[Paillier PHE]
    A3 -->|Only multiplication| A3b[RSA Mult]
    A3 -->|Arbitrary circuits| A3c[FHE BGV or CKKS]

    A5 -->|Homogeneous SA| A5a[k-Anonymity]
```
