# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define ε-differential privacy. Explain the role of the privacy budget ε.**

**Model Answer:**

A randomised algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies ε-differential privacy if for any two adjacent datasets $D_1, D_2$ differing by one record and any output set $S$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon}\,\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2) \in S]$$

The **privacy budget ε** is a non-negative real number controlling the *strength* of the privacy guarantee. **Smaller ε ⇒ stronger privacy but noisier output; larger ε ⇒ weaker privacy but higher utility.** Typical values used in industrial deployments (Apple, Google) lie in $\varepsilon \in [0.1, 10]$. ε is consumed additively when multiple queries are made on the same data (sequential composition).

**Keywords for 3 marks:** DP definition, adjacency, $e^{\varepsilon}$ bound, ε interpretation, composition.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between k-Anonymity and l-Diversity with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | k-Anonymity | l-Diversity |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Hide identity via indistinguishability | Hide sensitive attribute value |
| Mechanism | Each tuple identical to k−1 others on QI | Each equivalence class has ≥ l distinct SA values |
| Vulnerability | **Homogeneity attack** (all in class share same SA) | Skewness & similarity attacks |
| Guarantee | Syntactic / heuristic | Semantic / stronger |

**Example (Homogeneity attack defeats k-anonymity):**
Suppose QI = {ZIP} generalises a class of 5 patients, all of whom share Disease = "HIV". Although the dataset is 5-anonymous, an attacker who knows the patient is in that class **deduces** their disease with 100 % probability. Applying **2-diversity** would force ≥ 2 *different* diseases in any class, preventing the inference.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the **Laplace mechanism** for differential privacy with its mathematical formulation. Derive the condition under which it satisfies ε-DP. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **architecture of Federated Learning** and explain how it preserves privacy in CPS data collection. List two major challenges. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**Step 1 — Mechanism Definition.** The Laplace mechanism for a query $f : \mathcal{D} \to \mathbb{R}^d$ is

$$\mathcal{M}_{\text{Lap}}(D) = f(D) + \eta, \quad \eta \sim \text{Lap}\!\left(0, \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)^d$$

where $\Delta f$ is the $\ell_1$-sensitivity of $f$, defined as the maximum $\ell_1$-change in $f$'s output when one record is added/removed from the dataset.

**Step 2 — Derivation of the ε-DP condition.** See Section 3.1 of these notes for the exhaustive derivation. The conclusion is that with $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$ the ratio of output probabilities is bounded by $e^{\varepsilon}$, satisfying the DP definition.

**Step 3 — Worked illustration.** For a count query, $\Delta f = 1$. With $\varepsilon = 0.5$, $b = 2$, so each released count is perturbed by Laplace noise of scale 2, providing strong privacy but moderate utility.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1 — Federated Learning Architecture.** A central *parameter server* maintains a global model $w_t$. In each round:

1. Server broadcasts $w_t$ to $K$ selected clients (edge devices, vehicles, hospitals).
2. Each client $k$ trains locally: $w_{t+1}^{k} = w_t - \eta_t \nabla \mathcal{L}_k(w_t)$ using only its local data $D_k$ of size $n_k$.
3. Clients send model *updates* (not raw data) to the server.
4. Server aggregates using FedAvg: $w_{t+1} = \sum_{k} (n_k/n)\,w_{t+1}^{k}$.

**Step 2 — Privacy Preservation Mechanisms in FL.**
- **Data never leaves the device** — only model gradients/weights are transmitted.
- **Secure Aggregation** masks individual updates so the server only sees the sum.
- **Differential Privacy** can be added to the gradients to defend against membership-inference attacks.
- **Homomorphic masking** enables the server to operate on ciphertexts.

**Step 3 — Two Major Challenges in CPS context.**
1. **Statistical heterogeneity** — non-IID data across clients (e.g., hospitals in different cities) degrades convergence; addressed by FedProx, SCAFFOLD.
2. **Communication bottleneck** — gradient dimensions are large; compression (quantisation, sparsification) and client selection are mitigations. Additional challenge: **gradient leakage attacks** (e.g., Deep Leakage from Gradients, 2019) — mitigated by DP-SGD.

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**(a)** What is **Homomorphic Encryption**? Explain its types with suitable CPS use-cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **k-Anonymity and l-Diversity** with examples. Discuss one limitation of k-anonymity and how l-diversity overcomes it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**Step 1 — Definition.** A public-key cryptosystem is homomorphic if it allows meaningful computation on ciphertexts without decryption. Formally, given plaintexts $m_1, m_2$ and public operation $\circ$:

$$\text{Dec}_{sk}(\text{Eval}_{pk}(\circ, \text{Enc}_{pk}(m_1), \text{Enc}_{pk}(m_2))) = m_1 \circ m_2$$

**Step 2 — Types.**

| Type | Operations | Examples | CPS Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Partially HE (PHE)** | Only addition *or* multiplication | Paillier (add), RSA/Unpadded (mul) | Encrypted smart-meter billing (sum of consumption) |
| **Somewhat HE (SHE)** | Both, but bounded depth | BGV, BFV | Encrypted sum of squared signals |
| **Fully HE (FHE)** | Arbitrary-depth circuits | CKKS (approx. real numbers), TFHE (boolean), FHEW | Encrypted ML inference on CPS sensor data |

**Step 3 — CPS Use Cases.**
- **Healthcare CPS:** Hospitals upload encrypted patient records to cloud; cloud runs diagnostic ML on ciphertext.
- **Smart grid:** Aggregator computes total grid load from encrypted smart-meter readings (using Paillier addition).
- **Vehicular ad-hoc networks (VANETs):** Location aggregation without revealing trajectories.

**Step 4 — Trade-off.** FHE offers strongest security but is **~10⁶ times slower** than plaintext operations — limiting real-time CPS use. TFHE/CKKS accelerators and FPGA implementations are active research.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1 — k-Anonymity.** A release is k-anonymous if each record is indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ others on quasi-identifiers. Achieved via *generalisation* (e.g., Age 25 → 20–29) and *suppression* (mask with *).

**Example:** A hospital table with QI = {Age, ZIP, Sex}. After generalising, a 5-anonymous release has every equivalence class with ≥ 5 individuals.

**Step 2 — Limitation of k-Anonymity — Homogeneity Attack.** If all 5 patients in a class have Disease = "Cancer", an attacker who knows the target belongs to that class **perfectly infers** the sensitive value. k-anonymity provides no protection against sensitive-attribute disclosure.

**Step 3 — l-Diversity as the Fix.** l-diversity requires each equivalence class to contain at least $l$ *distinct* values of the sensitive attribute, with reasonable frequency. With 3-diversity, the class above would need at least 3 different diseases, breaking the homogeneity attack.

**Step 4 — Further refinement — t-Closeness.** For highly skewed SA distributions (e.g., 99 % "Healthy"), even l-diversity leaks information. t-closeness demands that the local SA distribution be within Earth-Mover distance $t$ of the global distribution.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Costing Marks in Privacy-Preserving Techniques Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting "adjacent"** — Always state datasets $D_1, D_2$ are *adjacent* (differ by 1 record) when writing the DP definition. Missing this loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing "sensitivity 1" with "global sensitivity"** — Count queries have $\Delta f = 1$, but average/median queries do not. Compute it!
> 3. **Mixing up PHE / SHE / FHE** — Students often claim Paillier is "fully" homomorphic. It is **partially** homomorphic (additive only).
> 4. **Confusing $e^{\varepsilon}$ direction** — The inequality is $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_1)] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D_2)]$, not the reverse.
> 5. **Neglecting post-processing immunity** — Always mention that *any* downstream function on a DP output remains DP.
> 6. **k-Anonymity: forgetting suppression** — A common error is generalisation-only; remember suppression is the dual technique.
> 7. **FL privacy myth** — Vanilla FedAvg is **not** DP-private; gradients leak information. Mention Secure Aggregation or DP-SGD.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Differential Privacy (DP):** A *mathematical* guarantee bounding the multiplicative shift $e^{\varepsilon}$ in output distributions for adjacent datasets. Smaller ε ⇒ stronger privacy.
- **Privacy Budget ε:** Cumulative under sequential composition ($\sum \varepsilon_i$); *max* under parallel composition. Once exhausted, no more queries allowed.
- **Sensitivity Δf:** Maximum $\ell_1$ change in $f$ over adjacent datasets. Count queries ⇒ $\Delta f = 1$.
- **Laplace Mechanism:** Add $\text{Lap}(0, \Delta f / \varepsilon)$ noise. Use for real-valued queries.
- **Gaussian Mechanism:** Add $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma \geq \Delta f_2 \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)} / \varepsilon$ for $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP. Use for vector queries with $\ell_2$ sensitivity.
- **Exponential Mechanism:** Use for non-numeric outputs (categorical selection, median).
- **k-Anonymity:** Each equivalence class on QI has size ≥ k. Defeated by *homogeneity* and *background-knowledge* attacks.
- **l-Diversity:** Strengthens k-anonymity by requiring ≥ l well-represented SA values per class.
- **t-Closeness:** Local SA distribution ≤ t EMD from global distribution. Strongest of the three syntactic models.
- **Homomorphic Encryption:** Allows $\text{Dec}(\text{Eval}(c_1, c_2)) = m_1 \circ m_2$. **PHE** (Paillier/RSA), **SHE** (BGV/BFV), **FHE** (CKKS/TFHE/FHEW). Trade-off: security ↔ computational cost.
- **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC):** Yao's garbled circuits (constant-round, boolean), Shamir's $(t, n)$-threshold secret sharing over $\mathbb{F}_p$ with Lagrange interpolation, GMW protocol.
- **Federated Learning (FL):** Local training + central aggregation via FedAvg. Vulnerable to gradient leakage; harden with Secure Aggregation and DP-SGD.
- **Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKP):** Prover convinces verifier of a statement's truth without revealing the witness (e.g., zk-SNARKs in blockchain CPS).
- **Privacy-Utility Trade-off:** Universal in all techniques. The art lies in choosing the operating point.
- **GDPR / DPDP Act 2023 compliance:** DP and FL are explicitly recognised as "appropriate safeguards" for cross-border data flows.
- **Real-world adopters:** Apple (Local DP for emojis), Google (RAPPOR), US Census 2020 (DP), Meta (FL for ads), Hospitals (FL for medical imaging).
- **Three attacks to memorise:** Membership-inference, Re-identification, Model-inversion.
- **Five CPS verticals to recall:** Healthcare IoT, Smart Grid, Autonomous Vehicles, Industrial IoT, Smart Cities.
- **Composition theorems:** Sequential (additive), Parallel (max), Advanced (Rényi DP-based) — required for high-scoring answers.
- **Final KTU Mantra:** *Privacy is a budget — spend it wisely.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intrusion Detection and Response: Techniques for Detecting and Mitigating Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT604 - ADVANCED CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Intrusion Detection and Response: Techniques for Detecting and Mitigating Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Intrusion Detection and Response: Techniques for Detecting and Mitigating Attacks

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intrusion Detection and Response (IDR)** in a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) is the continuous, multi-layered process of **monitoring**, **analyzing**, and **reacting** to malicious activity targeting the cyber components, communication channels, and physical processes of a tightly coupled computational–physical system. It combines **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)**, **Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS)**, and **Incident Response (IR)** workflows to preserve the **confidentiality, integrity, availability, and safety** of the CPS.

In the context of KTU 2024 PBCPT604, an *intrusion* is not limited to classical IT break-ins; it also includes:
- **False Data Injection (FDI)** in sensor measurements,
- **Actuator command spoofing** in control loops,
- **Time-synchronization tampering** (e.g., GPS spoofing),
- **Network-layer Denial-of-Service (DoS)** against the controller.

### 1.2 The Three Foundational Pillars

| Pillar | Question it Answers | CPS-Specific Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Detection** | *Is an attack happening?* | Detecting that a temperature sensor value has been inflated by 12 °C outside physical bounds. |
| **Response** | *What do we do now?* | Isolating the compromised PLC segment, switching to a redundant controller, and notifying the SCADA operator. |
| **Mitigation / Recovery** | *How do we limit damage and return to a safe state?* | Rolling the controller firmware to a trusted baseline and re-synchronizing the SCADA state machine. |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Smart Home Security System:** Imagine your house is a CPS. The smart thermostat (sensor), the smart door lock (actuator), and the Wi-Fi router (network) form a small cyber-physical loop. An *Intrusion Detection* system is the **glass-break sensor and motion camera** — it watches the door and the windows. An *Intrusion Response* system is the **siren, the automatic door lock, and the call to the police**. *Mitigation* is the **reinforced door, the safe room, and the insurance policy**. Without detection, no alarm ever rings. Without response, the alarm just annoys the neighbor. Without mitigation, the burglar simply breaks in again next week.

### 1.4 Why IDR is Harder in CPS than in Classical IT

> [!WARNING]
> In IT security, the worst-case impact of a failed detection is *data theft*. In CPS, the worst-case impact is **catastrophic physical damage or loss of human life** (e.g., Stuxnet centrifuges, the Maroochy water breach, the Ukraine power-grid attack of 2015). Hence, KTU 2024 Module 2 places strong emphasis on **safety-aware** and **physics-aware** detection — the system must understand not just the *bits* but the *Newtons, Volts, and Liters* they represent.

### 1.5 Standard Evaluation Metrics (Highlighted)

The following metrics are **mandatory** for any KTU 2024 numerical on IDS performance:

- **True Positive Rate (TPR)** — also called *Detection Rate* or *Sensitivity*.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — also called *False Alarm Rate*.
- **Precision** and **Recall**.
- **F1-Score** — the harmonic mean of precision and recall.
- **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)** and **Mean Time To Respond (MTTR)**.

All are treated formally in Section 2 and derived in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Detection Techniques

### 2.1.1 Signature-Based (Misuse) Detection
- **How it works:** Compares observed events (packet headers, system calls, control commands) against a database of known attack *signatures* (e.g., Snort rules, YARA patterns).
- **Strength:** Very **low false-positive rate (FPR)** for known attacks; trivial to interpret.
- **Weakness:** **Zero-day** attacks pass through undetected. In CPS, the attack catalog is *small but deep* — a single missed signature can be catastrophic.
- **CPS Example:** Detecting the Modbus function code `0x2B` (Read/Write Multiple Registers) when a sensor device is only allowed to read.

### 2.1.2 Anomaly-Based Detection
- **How it works:** Builds a *model of normal behaviour* (statistical, ML, or physics-based) and flags deviations.
- **Sub-flavours:**
  1. **Statistical** — mean, variance, CUSUM, sequential probability ratio test (SPRT).
  2. **Machine-Learning** — One-Class SVM, Isolation Forest, Autoencoders, LSTM.
  3. **Physics-Based** — uses **state-space models** and **residual generators** (parity space, observers, Kalman filters) to detect when measured variables violate conservation laws.
- **Strength:** Can detect **unknown / zero-day** attacks.
- **Weakness:** **Higher FPR**; requires clean training data.
- **CPS Example:** A Kalman filter on a water-tank level predicts a level of 4.20 m; the sensor reports 4.95 m — the residual is too large, so an attack is suspected.

### 2.1.3 Stateful Protocol Analysis
- **How it works:** Tracks the *protocol state machine* (e.g., DNP3, Modbus, IEC 61850 GOOSE) and flags transitions that violate the specification.
- **CPS Example:** Detecting an IEC 61850 GOOSE message with a **stale `stNum` counter** or an **out-of-range `sqNum`**, indicating a replay attack.

### 2.1.4 Hybrid Detection
- **How it works:** Combines signature + anomaly + stateful engines and *fuses* their decisions using a meta-classifier or voting scheme.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Response Strategies

| Strategy | Latency | Automation | CPS Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Alert (Passive)** | High | Manual | Low — too slow for physical loops running at kHz. |
| **Inline Block / Drop (IPS)** | Low | Automatic | High for network-layer attacks. |
| **Quarantine / Micro-segmentation** | Medium | Semi-automatic | High — uses SDN to isolate a compromised PLC. |
| **Reconfiguration / Failover** | Low | Automatic | **Critical** for CPS — switch to redundant controller. |
| **Safe-State Shutdown** | Low | Automatic | Last-resort to protect human life. |

## 2.3 Mitigation Techniques (Defense in Depth for CPS)

1. **Network Segmentation & Zones/Conduits** (ISA/IEC 62443 model).
2. **Moving Target Defense (MTD)** — randomize IP addresses, port numbers, control parameters.
3. **Encryption & Authentication** — TLS, DTLS, MACsec for industrial protocols; HMAC for command integrity.
4. **Redundancy & Diversity** — N-version controllers, heterogeneous sensors.
5. **Patching & Configuration Hardening**.
6. **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)** for dynamic re-routing around compromised nodes.
7. **Trusted Execution Environments (TEE)** and **TPM-based attestation** at the edge.
8. **Resilient Control** — the controller itself is made *attack-aware* (e.g., using control-barrier functions).

## 2.4 The Attack–Detection–Response Lifecycle in CPS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The classic IT *kill-chain* (recon → weaponize → deliver → exploit → install → C2 → actions) is **insufficient** for CPS. The CPS-specific **Cyber–Physical Kill Chain** adds two stages: *Cyber-to-Physical Translation* and *Physical Effect*. KTU expects you to know that IDR must close the loop on *both* the cyber and the physical side.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used in every formula below:**
> - $TP$ = True Positive, $FP$ = False Positive, $TN$ = True Negative, $FN$ = False Negative.
> - $N_{total} = TP + FP + TN + FN$ = total number of test events.
> - The symbol $\vert$ in formulas means *conditional upon*; do **NOT** confuse it with set-cardinality notation. To preserve KTU markdown-table syntax, we write the set-cardinality symbol as $\mid$ and the conditional symbol as $\vert$.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | True Positive Rate (Detection Rate) | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Unitless, range $[0,1]$. |
| 2 | False Positive Rate (False Alarm Rate) | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Unitless, range $[0,1]$. |
| 3 | True Negative Rate (Specificity) | $TNR = 1 - FPR = \dfrac{TN}{FP + TN}$ | Unitless, range $[0,1]$. |
| 4 | False Negative Rate (Miss Rate) | $FNR = 1 - TPR = \dfrac{FN}{TP + FN}$ | Unitless, range $[0,1]$. |
| 5 | Precision (Positive Predictive Value) | $PPV = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Unitless. |
| 6 | Recall (same as TPR) | $Recall = TPR$ | — |
| 7 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{PPV \cdot TPR}{PPV + TPR}$ | Harmonic mean; range $[0,1]$. |
| 8 | Accuracy | $Acc = \dfrac{TP + TN}{N_{total}}$ | Misleading on imbalanced data. |
| 9 | Matthews Correlation Coefficient | $MCC = \dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Best for imbalanced CPS data. |
| 10 | Bayesian Detection (posterior of attack) | $P(A \vert E) = \dfrac{P(E \vert A) \cdot P(A)}{P(E)}$ | $E$ = evidence, $A$ = attack hypothesis. |
| 11 | Marginal probability of evidence | $P(E) = P(E \vert A)\,P(A) + P(E \vert \lnot A)\,P(\lnot A)$ | Total-probability law. |
| 12 | Odds-Likelihood Form | $\dfrac{P(A \vert E)}{P(\lnot A \vert E)} = \dfrac{P(E \vert A)}{P(E \vert \lnot A)} \cdot \dfrac{P(A)}{P(\lnot A)}$ | Useful for log-odds IDS scores. |
| 13 | CUSUM Decision Statistic | $S_n^+ = \max(0,\, S_{n-1}^+ + z_n - k)$ | $z_n$ = standardized residual, $k$ = reference value. |
| 14 | Chi-Squared (χ²) FDI Detector | $g_k = \mathbf{r}_k^{\!\top} \, \Sigma^{-1} \, \mathbf{r}_k \;\; \overset{H_1}{\underset{H_0}{\gtrless}} \;\; \tau$ | $\mathbf{r}_k$ = innovation, $\Sigma$ = covariance, $\tau$ = threshold. |
| 15 | MTTD | $MTTD = \mathbb{E}[t_{detect} - t_{attack\text{-}start}]$ | Seconds / milliseconds. |
| 16 | MTTR | $MTTR = \mathbb{E}[t_{recovered} - t_{detect}]$ | Seconds / milliseconds. |
| 17 | CPS Detection Cost | $C_{total} = c_{FP} \cdot FP + c_{FN} \cdot FN$ | $c_{FP}, c_{FN}$ = cost weights. |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are *not* academic. In an actual smart-grid deployment:
- An **FPR of 1 %** on 10 million sensor readings per day = **100 000 false alarms** per day — operationally unusable.
- A **missed FN** in a nuclear-plant sensor could trigger a multi-million-dollar shutdown.
- Hence the **cost matrix** ($c_{FP}, c_{FN}$) and the **MCC** are the *de-facto* KTU 2024 deliverables when an IDS is being justified in a design problem.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section proves the *why* behind every formula in the cheat sheet. Each step is written out in full and explained in plain English so it can be reproduced in the KTU answer sheet.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Confusion-Matrix Identity

> [!NOTE]
> The four counts $TP, FP, TN, FN$ partition the entire population $N_{total}$, so their sum must equal the total number of test events. This is the *partition-of-unity* identity used in every IDS paper.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= (\text{all positives}) + (\text{all negatives}) \\[2pt]
          &= (TP + FN) + (TN + FP)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Plain-English meaning:** Among all the events the IDS sees, some are truly attacks (positives) and some are benign (negatives). Each event is either correctly handled or not. The four cells of the confusion matrix are mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive.

## 3.2 Derivation of $TPR$ and $FPR$

> [!NOTE]
> TPR asks: *"Out of all the real attacks, how many did we catch?"* FPR asks: *"Out of all the benign events, how many did we wrongly flag?"*

$$
\begin{aligned}
TPR &= \frac{\text{detected attacks}}{\text{all attacks}} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\[2pt]
FPR &= \frac{\text{wrongly flagged benign events}}{\text{all benign events}} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The denominators are *fixed* by reality (the number of true attacks and the number of true benign events). The numerators are *under our control* — they depend on the detector threshold.

## 3.3 Derivation of the F1-Score

> [!NOTE]
> The arithmetic mean of precision and recall can be misleading because one of them can be 1 while the other is 0. The harmonic mean punishes this asymmetry — if either precision or recall is zero, F1 is zero. That is *exactly* what CPS needs: a detector that flags everything is useless (precision = 0) and a detector that flags nothing is useless (recall = 0).

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{PPV \cdot TPR}{PPV + TPR} \\[2pt]
    &= \frac{2}{\frac{1}{PPV} + \frac{1}{TPR}} \\[2pt]
    &= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Substitute $PPV = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ and $TPR = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$, then take the common denominator. The final form $\frac{2\,TP}{2\,TP+FP+FN}$ is the form examiners love to see in KTU 2024 — memorize it.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Bayesian Detection Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the single most important derivation for KTU 2024 PBCPT604. The Bayesian detector computes the *posterior probability* that an attack is happening, given the observed evidence. It is the formal foundation of every probabilistic IDS.

We start from the definition of conditional probability:

$$
P(A \cap E) = P(A \vert E) \cdot P(E) = P(E \vert A) \cdot P(A)
$$

Re-arranging for the posterior $P(A \vert E)$:

$$
P(A \vert E) = \frac{P(E \vert A) \cdot P(A)}{P(E)}
$$

To make this useful, we expand $P(E)$ using the *law of total probability* over the mutually exclusive and exhaustive hypotheses $\{A, \lnot A\}$:

$$
P(E) = P(E \cap A) + P(E \cap \lnot A) = P(E \vert A)\,P(A) + P(E \vert \lnot A)\,P(\lnot A)
$$

Therefore the fully expanded Bayesian detector is:

$$
\boxed{\,P(A \vert E) \;=\; \frac{P(E \vert A)\,P(A)}{P(E \vert A)\,P(A) \;+\; P(E \vert \lnot A)\,P(\lnot A)}\,}
$$

**Plain-English meaning:**
- $P(A)$ is the *prior* — how often attacks occur in the past.
- $P(E \vert A)$ is the *likelihood* — how probable is the observed evidence *if an attack is in progress*.
- $P(E \vert \lnot A)$ is the *likelihood under normal operation*.
- $P(A \vert E)$ is the *posterior* — our updated belief that an attack is happening *now*.

**Engineering example — CPS smart meter:** Suppose a CPS sees an unusual voltage $E$. Historical data gives $P(A) = 0.001$ (1 attack per 1000 events). Under attack, $P(E \vert A) = 0.95$ (the attack almost always produces this signature). Under normal operation, $P(E \vert \lnot A) = 0.05$ (rare but possible). Then:

$$
P(A \vert E) = \frac{0.95 \times 0.001}{0.95 \times 0.001 + 0.05 \times 0.999} = \frac{0.00095}{0.00095 + 0.04995} \approx 0.01866
$$

So even with a strong signature, the *posterior* is only ~1.9 % because the prior is so low. This is the *base-rate fallacy* and explains why naive signature detectors in CPS suffer from FPR.

## 3.5 Derivation of the Log-Odds Form

> [!NOTE]
> Taking logarithms converts products into sums, which is computationally cheaper and numerically stable. This is the form used in logistic-regression-based IDS.

Take the log of the odds-likelihood form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log\!\left(\frac{P(A \vert E)}{P(\lnot A \vert E)}\right)
   &= \log\!\left(\frac{P(E \vert A)}{P(E \vert \lnot A)}\right) + \log\!\left(\frac{P(A)}{P(\lnot A)}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Define $L(E) = \log\!\left(\dfrac{P(A \vert E)}{P(\lnot A \vert E)}\right)$ as the *log-odds score*. The IDS makes a decision by comparing $L(E)$ with a threshold $\tau = 0$ (or a calibrated constant):

$$
L(E) \;\; \overset{H_1}{\underset{H_0}{\gtrless}} \;\; 0
$$

## 3.6 Derivation of the Chi-Squared False-Data-Injection Detector

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the canonical *physics-aware* detector for CPS. It is the answer KTU expects for a 14-mark question on FDI.

Consider a linear discrete-time CPS with measurement model:

$$
\mathbf{y}_k = C \, \mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{v}_k
$$

where $\mathbf{v}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, R)$ is Gaussian noise. A Kalman filter produces the innovation (residual):

$$
\mathbf{r}_k = \mathbf{y}_k - C \, \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1}
$$

Under normal operation (hypothesis $H_0$):

$$
\mathbf{r}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \Sigma_k), \quad \Sigma_k = C P_{k \vert k-1} C^{\!\top} + R
$$

The Mahalanobis distance squared is then:

$$
g_k = \mathbf{r}_k^{\!\top} \, \Sigma_k^{-1} \, \mathbf{r}_k
$$

Under $H_0$, $g_k \sim \chi^2_m$, where $m$ is the dimension of $\mathbf{y}_k$. The decision rule is:

$$
g_k \;\; \overset{H_1}{\underset{H_0}{\gtrless}} \;\; \tau
$$

**Conversion logic:** The covariance $\Sigma_k$ *whitens* the residual, so the quadratic form $g_k$ becomes a sum of $m$ independent unit Gaussians, which is exactly the $\chi^2_m$ distribution. We pick $\tau = \chi^2_{m,\,1-\alpha}$ for a desired false-alarm rate $\alpha$ (e.g., $\alpha = 0.01$).

## 3.7 Derivation of the Cost-Optimal Threshold

> [!NOTE]
> The Bayesian minimum-cost threshold for a binary IDS is the threshold that minimizes $C_{total} = c_{FP}\cdot FP + c_{FN}\cdot FN$. It is derived as follows.

Let $P(A \vert E)$ be the posterior. Raising the threshold reduces $FP$ but increases $FN$; lowering it does the opposite. The expected cost is minimized when:

$$
P(A \vert E) \;\; \overset{H_1}{\underset{H_0}{\gtrless}} \;\; \tau^{\star} \;=\; \frac{c_{FP}}{c_{FP} + c_{FN}}
$$

**Plain-English meaning:** In a CPS, $c_{FN}$ is enormous (a missed attack may crash a turbine), so $\tau^{\star}$ is very small — we are willing to accept many false alarms to never miss a real attack.

## 3.8 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Sub-Part)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An IDS is evaluated on 10 000 events. Of these, 200 are real attacks. The IDS raises 180 alarms, of which 150 are real attacks. Compute $TPR, FPR, Precision, F_1, MCC$.

**Step 1 — Build the confusion matrix.**

We are given:
- $TP = 150$
- Total attacks = $TP + FN = 200 \;\Rightarrow\; FN = 50$
- Total alarms raised = $TP + FP = 180 \;\Rightarrow\; FP = 30$
- $TN = N_{total} - TP - FP - FN = 10\,000 - 150 - 30 - 50 = 9770$

**Step 2 — Compute $TPR$.**

$$
TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $FPR$.**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{30}{30 + 9770} = \frac{30}{9800} \approx 0.00306
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Precision.**

$$
PPV = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{150}{180} \approx 0.8333
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $F_1$.**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot PPV \cdot TPR}{PPV + TPR} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.8333 \cdot 0.75}{0.8333 + 0.75} = \frac{1.25}{1.5833} \approx 0.7895
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $MCC$.**

$$
MCC = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}
$$

Substitute:

$$
MCC = \frac{150 \cdot 9770 - 30 \cdot 50}{\sqrt{180 \cdot 200 \cdot 9800 \cdot 9820}} = \frac{1\,465\,500 - 1500}{\sqrt{3.466 \times 10^{12}}}
$$

$$
MCC = \frac{1\,464\,000}{1.8617 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.786
$$

**Step 7 — Interpret.** $TPR = 75\%$ means we miss 1 in 4 attacks. $FPR \approx 0.3\%$ is excellent. The MCC ≈ 0.79 indicates a *strong* but not perfect detector — exactly the kind of partial-credit answer KTU 2024 expects.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid block diagrams are calibrated for KTU 2024 — they use **alphanumeric node IDs with letter prefixes** (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and **clean uppercase labels** with no markdown formatting inside the double-quoted strings.

## 4.1 Generic CPS Intrusion Detection & Response Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[PHYSICAL PLANT] --> B1[SENSORS]
    B1 --> C1[FIELD NETWORK]
    C1 --> D1[CONTROLLER PLC]
    D1 --> E1[ACTUATORS]
    E1 --> A1
    C1 --> F1[NIDS NETWORK IDS]
    D1 --> G1[HIDS HOST IDS]
    B1 --> H1[PHYSICS RESIDUAL GEN]
    F1 --> I1[CORRELATION ENGINE]
    G1 --> I1
    H1 --> I1
    I1 --> J1{ATTACK CONFIRMED}
    J1 -- YES --> K1[RESPONSE ORCHESTRATOR]
    J1 -- NO --> L1[NORMAL LOG]
    K1 --> M1[QUARANTINE VIA SDN]
    K1 --> N1[FAILOVER TO REDUNDANT CTRL]
    K1 --> O1[SAFE STATE SHUTDOWN]
    K1 --> P1[ALERT TO SCADA OPERATOR]
```

## 4.2 Detection Decision Flow (Signature + Anomaly + Stateful Fusion)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[EVENT CAPTURE PACKET OR SYSTEM CALL] --> S2{FORMAT OK}
    S2 -- NO --> S3[DROP PARSE ERROR]
    S2 -- YES --> S4[SIGNATURE MATCH]
    S4 -- HIT --> S5[ALERT SIGNATURE]
    S4 -- MISS --> S6[ANOMALY SCORE]
    S6 --> S7{SCORE OVER TAU}
    S7 -- YES --> S8[ALERT ANOMALY]
    S7 -- NO --> S9[STATE MACHINE CHECK]
    S9 --> S10{STATE VIOLATION}
    S10 -- YES --> S11[ALERT STATEFUL]
    S10 -- NO --> S12[BENIGN]
    S5 --> S13[META FUSION]
    S8 --> S13
    S11 --> S13
    S12 --> S13
    S13 --> S14[FUSED DECISION]
```

## 4.3 CPS Cyber-Physical Kill Chain with IDR Overlays

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[RECON] --> K2[INTRUSION DELIVERY]
    K2 --> K3[CYBER EXPLOIT]
    K3 --> K4[C2 BEACON]
    K4 --> K5[CYBER TO PHYSICAL TRANSLATION]
    K5 --> K6[PHYSICAL EFFECT]
    R1[IDS ALERT] -.-> K3
    R1 -.-> K4
    R1 -.-> K5
    R2[IPS BLOCK] -.-> K2
    R3[PHYSICS RESIDUAL TRIP] -.-> K5
    R4[SAFE SHUTDOWN] -.-> K6
```

## 4.4 Response Severity Escalation Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[LOW SEVERITY ALERT] --> E2[LOG AND NOTIFY OPERATOR]
    E2 --> E3{REPEAT IN 5 MIN}
    E3 -- NO --> E4[CLOSE TICKET]
    E3 -- YES --> E5[MEDIUM SEVERITY]
    E5 --> E6[QUARANTINE HOST VIA SDN]
    E6 --> E7{CONFIRMED ATTACK}
    E7 -- NO --> E8[RELEASE AND PATCH]
    E7 -- YES --> E9[HIGH SEVERITY]
    E9 --> E10[FAILOVER CONTROLLER]
    E10 --> E11[SAFE STATE SHUTDOWN]
    E11 --> E12[FORENSICS AND RECOVERY]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between signature-based and anomaly-based intrusion detection systems. Give one CPS-specific example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Signature-Based IDS | Anomaly-Based IDS |
|---|---|---|
| Detection basis | Matches events against a database of known attack patterns. | Builds a model of normal behaviour and flags deviations. |
| Zero-day detection | **Cannot** detect unknown attacks. | **Can** detect unknown attacks. |
| False Positive Rate | **Low** | **Higher** |
| CPS example | Detecting a Modbus write to a read-only register (function code 0x05) against a known whitelist. | A Kalman-filter-based residual flagging a temperature sensor reading 12 °C above the physics-predicted value. |
| Computational cost | Low (string / pattern match). | Higher (statistical / ML inference). |

*(Examiner will award full 3 marks only if the table-style contrast and the CPS-specific example are both present.)*

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define True Positive Rate and False Positive Rate. Why is FPR critical in CPS deployments?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **TPR** $= \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — the fraction of real attacks correctly detected.
- **FPR** $= \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ — the fraction of benign events wrongly flagged as attacks.
- FPR is critical in CPS because each false alarm may force a **physical shutdown** or a **controller failover**. In a chemical plant, even a 1 % FPR on 10 000 sensor readings per hour causes 100 unnecessary shutdowns per hour — operationally and economically unacceptable. Hence CPS IDS designs explicitly *minimize FPR*, even at the cost of a slightly higher miss rate, and then use **cost-sensitive thresholds** (Section 2.5, row 17) to re-balance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Module pattern: each Part-B question is 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **You must answer either Choice A or Choice B in full.** Below, both choices are provided as complete 14-mark model answers.

### Choice A — Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of an Intrusion Detection System for a Cyber-Physical System. With a neat block diagram, describe the role of *Network IDS (NIDS)*, *Host IDS (HIDS)*, and *Physics-Based Residual Generator*.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Definition [1 mark]:** An IDS for a CPS is a distributed monitoring system that observes cyber-layer (network packets, system calls) and physical-layer (sensor measurements, actuator commands) events to identify malicious activity.

2. **Architecture [3 marks]:** The CPS IDS is composed of three co-operating engines:
   - **NIDS** taps into the industrial network (e.g., spanning port on a managed switch carrying Modbus/TCP or DNP3 traffic). It inspects packet headers, function codes, and payloads against signature and protocol-state databases.
   - **HIDS** runs on the controller (PLC, RTU, or edge gateway). It monitors system calls, process invocations, firmware integrity (TPM attestation), and local log files.
   - **Physics-Based Residual Generator** uses a *digital twin* of the physical plant — typically a Kalman filter or an observer — to predict what the sensor value *should* be. The difference (residual) is fed to a χ² or CUSUM test.

3. **Correlation & Fusion [2 marks]:** A central correlation engine fuses the three streams using either voting or a Bayesian meta-classifier. A single alert from any one engine is *informative*; two simultaneous alerts are *corroborative*; a cyber-alert plus a physics-alert is *decisive* — this is the *physics-aware* advantage of CPS IDS over classical IT IDS.

4. **Block Diagram [1 mark]:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram — A1 → B1 → C1 → D1 → E1 → A1; C1 → F1 NIDS; D1 → G1 HIDS; B1 → H1 residual generator; all three → I1 correlation engine.)*

**(b) [7 marks]** A CPS IDS is tested on 8000 events. 500 are real attacks. The IDS raises 600 alarms, of which 450 correspond to real attacks. Compute $TPR, FPR, Precision, F_1, MCC$. Comment on the detector's suitability for a *nuclear plant* sensor stream.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Confusion matrix [1 mark].**
- $TP = 450$
- $FN = 500 - 450 = 50$
- $FP = 600 - 450 = 150$
- $TN = 8000 - 450 - 150 - 50 = 7350$

**Step 2 — $TPR$ [1 mark].**
$$
TPR = \frac{450}{500} = 0.90
$$

**Step 3 — $FPR$ [1 mark].**
$$
FPR = \frac{150}{150 + 7350} = \frac{150}{7500} = 0.02
$$

**Step 4 — $Precision$ [1 mark].**
$$
PPV = \frac{450}{600} = 0.75
$$

**Step 5 — $F_1$ and $MCC$ [2 marks].**
$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.75 \cdot 0.90}{0.75 + 0.90} = \frac{1.35}{1.65} \approx 0.8182
$$

$$
MCC = \frac{450 \cdot 7350 - 150 \cdot 50}{\sqrt{600 \cdot 500 \cdot 7500 \cdot 7400}} = \frac{3\,307\,500 - 7500}{\sqrt{1.665 \times 10^{13}}} = \frac{3\,300\,000}{4.080 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.809
$$

**Step 6 — Suitability comment [1 mark].** $TPR = 90\%$ means 1 in 10 attacks is *missed* — **unsuitable** for a nuclear plant where the cost of a missed attack ($c_{FN}$) is essentially infinite. The detector must be *retrained* with a cost-sensitive threshold $\tau^{\star} = \dfrac{c_{FP}}{c_{FP}+c_{FN}}$ biased toward *over-detection*. The 2 % FPR is acceptable *only* if there is a human-in-the-loop verification step before a shutdown is triggered.

---

### Choice B — Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the **cyber-physical kill chain** of an attack on a smart-grid SCADA system. Identify at which stages an NIDS, a physics-based residual detector, and a safe-state shutdown are most effective.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Kill chain stages [3 marks]:** Reconnaissance → Delivery (phishing / USB) → Exploit (IT) → C2 Beacons → Lateral movement to OT network → Cyber-to-physical translation (e.g., spoofed setpoints) → Physical effect (generator trip, line overload).
2. **NIDS effective stages [1 mark]:** *Delivery* and *C2 Beacons* — these are still IP-packet-borne and visible to network taps on the corporate-DMZ boundary.
3. **Physics-based residual effective stages [2 marks]:** *Cyber-to-physical translation* and *Physical effect* — once the attacker has altered setpoints or measurements, the Kalman-filter residual blows up. This is the **unique CPS advantage**: the attack is detectable *even if the cyber-layer is invisible*.
4. **Safe-state shutdown [1 mark]:** Effective at the *Physical-effect* stage as a last line of defence. Reference the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3.

**(b) [7 marks]** A CPS uses a Bayesian IDS. The prior probability of an attack is $P(A) = 0.002$. The likelihood of the observed evidence under attack is $P(E \vert A) = 0.90$, and under normal operation is $P(E \vert \lnot A) = 0.05$. Compute the posterior $P(A \vert E)$. If the cost of a missed attack is 1 000 000 ₹ and the cost of a false alarm is 500 ₹, compute the cost-optimal threshold $\tau^{\star}$ and state the decision rule.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Marginal evidence probability [1 mark].**
$$
P(E) = 0.90 \times 0.002 + 0.05 \times 0.998 = 0.0018 + 0.0499 = 0.0517
$$

**Step 2 — Posterior [2 marks].**
$$
P(A \vert E) = \frac{0.90 \times 0.002}{0.0517} = \frac{0.0018}{0.0517} \approx 0.0348
$$

So even with a strong signature, the posterior is only ~3.48 % because the attack is so rare. This is the **base-rate fallacy**.

**Step 3 — Cost-optimal threshold [2 marks].**
$$
\tau^{\star} = \frac{c_{FP}}{c_{FP} + c_{FN}} = \frac{500}{500 + 1\,000\,000} = \frac{500}{1\,000\,500} \approx 4.998 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule [1 mark].**
$$
P(A \vert E) \;\; \overset{H_1}{\underset{H_0}{\gtrless}} \;\; 4.998 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Because $0.0348 \gg 4.998 \times 10^{-4}$, we **raise the alarm** ($H_1$: attack).

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation [1 mark].** The cost-asymmetric threshold is *extremely small* — we are willing to live with 1999 false alarms to avoid even a single missed attack in a safety-critical CPS.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing TPR and Precision.** TPR is *row-wise* (denominator uses the *true* class); Precision is *column-wise* (denominator uses the *predicted* class). Examiners will award *zero* partial credit if you mix them up.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4×4 table that links IDS to the CPS kill chain.** KTU 2024 explicitly tests whether you can map *which detector catches which stage*. Vague answers like "IDS detects attacks" get 1 out of 7 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the base-rate calculation** in Bayesian questions. Writing only $P(A \vert E) = P(E \vert A) P(A) / P(E)$ *without expanding* $P(E)$ is treated as incomplete — full marks require the total-probability expansion.
> 4. **Not writing units** on MTTD / MTTR. The answer "0.05" without "seconds" loses a mark.
> 5. **Failing to comment on CPS-specific suitability** — every numerical answer must end with a one-sentence *engineering interpretation* (e.g., "suitable / not suitable for a nuclear plant because…").

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 PBCPT604 Module 2):**

- [x] **IDR = Detection + Response + Mitigation**; all three are *equally* required.
- [x] **Three detection families:** Signature, Anomaly, Stateful — *hybrid* is the CPS best-practice.
- [x] **CPS-specific detectors:** Physics-based residual (Kalman / observer), protocol-state checks (DNP3, IEC 61850, Modbus).
- [x] **Cyber-Physical Kill Chain** has *seven* stages; IDR must overlay *all* of them.
- [x] **Confusion matrix** = $\{TP, FP, TN, FN\}$; $N_{total} = TP+FP+TN+FN$.
- [x] **TPR** uses real-attack denominator; **FPR** uses real-benign denominator.
- [x] **F1 = 2TP / (2TP + FP + FN)** — the only form to memorize.
- [x] **MCC** is the *only* honest metric for *imbalanced* CPS data.
- [x] **Bayes posterior:** $P(A \vert E) = \dfrac{P(E \vert A) P(A)}{P(E \vert A) P(A) + P(E \vert \lnot A) P(\lnot A)}$ — *always* expand $P(E)$.
- [x] **χ² FDI detector:** $g_k = \mathbf{r}_k^{\!\top} \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{r}_k \sim \chi^2_m$ under $H_0$.
- [x] **CUSUM** is the standard *sequential* test for slow-drift attacks.
- [x] **Cost-asymmetric threshold:** $\tau^{\star} = c_{FP} / (c_{FP} + c_{FN})$.
- [x] **Response ladder:** Alert → Quarantine (SDN) → Failover → Safe-state shutdown.
- [x] **MTTD + MTTR** are the *operational* KPIs of any CPS SOC.
- [x] **Defense in depth** is non-negotiable: segmentation + MTD + encryption + redundancy + resilient control.
- [x] Always finish numerical answers with an **engineering interpretation** sentence — it is the differentiator between a 7-mark and a 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incident Response Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT604 - ADVANCED CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Incident Response Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Incident Response (IR)** in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) is the structured, coordinated, and pre-planned set of technical, procedural, and managerial actions undertaken by an organization to effectively detect, contain, eradicate, and recover from security incidents that compromise the confidentiality, integrity, availability, or safety of its cyber-physical assets (sensors, actuators, controllers, PLCs, SCADA, IoT, OT networks, and cloud-edge backends).

According to **NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2** (Computer Security Incident Handling Guide), an *incident* is "a violation or imminent threat of violation of computer security policies, acceptable use policies, or standard security practices." For CPS, the definition extends to include **safety incidents** (e.g., physical damage, harm to humans, environmental release) and **operational incidents** (e.g., process shutdown, equipment damage).

An **Incident Response Strategy** is the high-level plan that defines the *who, what, when, where, and how* of an organization's response, encompassing policies, teams, communication, tooling, and decision-making frameworks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBCPT604 - Module 2):**
> Incident Response Strategies cover the end-to-end handling model, preparation, detection, analysis, containment, eradication, recovery, and post-incident activity, with CPS-specific emphasis on safety-critical containment, real-time decision loops, and integration with operational technology (OT) environments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room

Imagine a **Cyber-Physical System (e.g., a smart power grid)** as a *patient* and the **Incident Response Team** as a *hospital's emergency response unit*:

| Hospital ER Element | CPS Incident Response Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Triage nurse on arrival | Initial alert from SIEM/IDS sensor |
| Emergency declaration code | Incident classification (Severity 1–4) |
| Specialist team dispatched | CSIRT / SOC analyst team |
| Surgery to remove cause | Eradication of malware / rootkit |
| ICU / recovery ward | System restoration, business continuity |
| Post-treatment medical report | Post-incident report & lessons learned |
| Annual emergency drills | Tabletop exercises, red/blue team drills |

Just as an ER *prepares* (stocks blood, trains staff, rehearses drills) *before* a patient arrives, an effective CPS incident response strategy requires **continuous preparation** — because in CPS, the cost of a slow or wrong response can mean physical damage or loss of life, not just data loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics Used in CPS Incident Response (Memorize for KTU):**
> - **MTTD** — Mean Time to Detect
> - **MTTC** — Mean Time to Contain
> - **MTTR** — Mean Time to Recover / Remediate
> - **MTTA** — Mean Time to Acknowledge
> - **RTO** — Recovery Time Objective
> - **RPO** — Recovery Point Objective

## 1.3 Why Incident Response is Critical in CPS

Unlike traditional IT, where a security breach primarily affects *data*, a CPS breach affects the **physical world**. The **Stuxnet** attack (2010) on Iranian uranium centrifuges and the **Triton/TRISIS** attack (2017) on a Saudi petrochemical plant's safety instrumented system (SIS) are canonical examples. Both attacks bypassed cyber defenses and directly manipulated physical actuators — proving that without a robust, safety-aware IR strategy, CPS incidents escalate to *physical disasters*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Safety vs. Security Dichotomy in CPS IR:**
> In IT, the responder's first instinct is *containment* (isolate the system). In CPS, immediate isolation may cause MORE physical harm (e.g., shutting down a chemical reactor unsafely). Hence, CPS IR must be **safety-first, security-second** — often a counterintuitive priority for IT-trained responders.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The NIST Incident Response Process (Reference Model)

The **NIST SP 800-61** four-phase model is the *de facto* academic and industry reference. Every KTU examiner expects students to know these phases in order.

### Phase 1 — Preparation
The most important and ongoing phase. Done *before* an incident occurs.
- Establish policies, IR plans, communication templates.
- Set up CSIRT/SOC, define roles (Incident Commander, Lead Investigator, Communications Lead, Legal, Safety Officer).
- Deploy monitoring tools (SIEM, IDS/IPS, OT anomaly detection).
- Conduct training, tabletop exercises, and red-team drills.
- Maintain hardware/software asset inventory, network diagrams, baseline behavior.
- Pre-stage forensic toolkits, offline backups, and clean OS images.

### Phase 2 — Detection & Analysis
- **Detection sources:** IDS/IPS alerts, SIEM correlations, endpoint EDR, user reports, vendor advisories, OT anomaly alarms (e.g., a PLC command outside its expected state machine).
- **Analysis tasks:** Triage alerts → correlate events → confirm incident → classify (type, scope, severity, attacker attribution).
- **Indicators of Compromise (IoCs):** IP addresses, file hashes, domain names, registry keys, abnormal Modbus/Profinet traffic patterns.
- **Documentation:** Preserve volatile evidence (RAM, network connections, running processes) before powering off.

### Phase 3 — Containment, Eradication & Recovery

> [!NOTE]
> NIST actually treats this as one phase, but in CPS practice it is split into three discrete decision points because of **safety implications**.

- **Containment Strategies** (see Section 2.2 for full taxonomy):
  * *Short-term:* Block IP, kill process, disable account.
  * *Long-term:* Patch system, segment network, rebuild from clean image.
- **Eradication:** Remove malware, close vulnerabilities, reset credentials, audit all access logs for lateral movement.
- **Recovery:** Restore from verified backups, validate system integrity, monitor for reinfection, gradually return to production. Validate that **safety interlocks** are functional before reconnecting to physical processes.

### Phase 4 — Post-Incident Activity
- Conduct a **lessons-learned** meeting within 1–2 weeks.
- Update IR plan, playbooks, signatures, training.
- File regulatory/legal reports (e.g., NIS2, NERC-CIP, IEC 62443 incident reports).
- Calculate metrics (MTTD, MTTR) and feed into continuous improvement.

## 2.2 Containment Strategies — The CPS-IR Decision Matrix

Containment in CPS must be **risk-balanced** — every decision trades off *security* (stop the attacker) against *safety* (don't break the physical process) and *operational continuity* (don't shut down production).

| Containment Type | Action Example | CPS-Specific Consideration |
|---|---|---|
| **Network Segmentation** | Move PLC to isolated VLAN | Validate that real-time control loops still meet latency SLAs |
| **Process Shutdown** | Emergency stop (E-stop) the physical process | **Last resort** — may be required for *safety*, not security |
| **Failover to Backup** | Switch to redundant controller | Verify backup is clean and synchronized |
| **Disable Compromised Account** | Revoke engineer credentials | Check no active safety-critical sessions are using that account |
| **Sandboxing / Honeypot** | Redirect attacker to decoy | Used for analysis, not containment |
| **Rollback Firmware** | Restore PLC firmware from golden image | Ensure rollback does not violate regulatory validation (e.g., FDA, IEC 61511) |
| **Physical Isolation** | Disconnect device from network | Safest but most disruptive — must not breach safety case |

## 2.3 Incident Classification & Severity Scoring

A formal severity score drives resource allocation, escalation, and SLA. A common model adapts **CVSS-like** logic to CPS:

$$ S = (L \times I \times S_s) + C $$

Where:
- $S$ = Final Severity Score
- $L$ = Likelihood of attack success (1–5)
- $I$ = Business/Operational Impact (1–5)
- $S_s$ = Safety Impact (1–5, weighted heavily in CPS)
- $C$ = Compliance/Regulatory penalty factor (0–5)

A score of **≥ 70** is typically a **Severity 1 (Critical)** event requiring immediate CSIRT mobilization and executive notification.

## 2.4 Recovery Time & Availability Formulas

For CPS, **availability** is often a regulatory requirement (e.g., 99.99% = 52.6 minutes downtime/year for a power substation). The relationship between MTTR, MTBF, and Availability is foundational:

$$ A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\% $$

Where:
- $A$ = System availability (%)
- $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures
- $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair (or Recover)

For incident response, the **RTO** and **RPO** objectives must be compared against the MTTR actually achieved:

$$ MTTR_{achieved} \leq RTO $$

$$ DataLoss_{acceptable} = RPO \times ProcessDataRate $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** If a question asks "design an IR strategy for a CPS," you must explicitly mention **safety-first triage**, **OT-aware containment**, **forensic preservation of PLC logic**, and **RTO/RPO aligned with safety case**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | SLA compliance for critical infrastructure |
| MTTD | $\dfrac{\sum (T_{detected} - T_{occurred})}{N_{incidents}}$ | Measures sensor/SIEM effectiveness |
| MTTA | $\dfrac{\sum (T_{acknowledged} - T_{detected})}{N}$ | Measures SOC staffing & alert routing |
| MTTC | $\dfrac{\sum (T_{contained} - T_{detected})}{N}$ | Measures containment speed |
| MTTR | $\dfrac{\sum (T_{recovered} - T_{contained})}{N}$ | Measures recovery speed |
| RTO | Max tolerable downtime | Drives recovery architecture |
| RPO | Max tolerable data loss | Drives backup frequency |
| Severity Score | $S = L \times I \times S_s + C$ | Resource allocation & escalation |
| Risk | $R = Probability \times Impact$ | Pre-incident prioritization |
| Sigma Level (process) | $6\sigma = 3.4$ DPMO | Quality of IR process maturity |

> All equations use standard time units (seconds, minutes, hours). For real-time CPS, $MTTD$ and $MTTC$ are often measured in **milliseconds** for safety-critical loops.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Smart Grids:** IR strategies must coordinate with utility control centers to prevent cascading blackouts (e.g., the 2015 Ukraine power grid attack).
- **Medical CPS (IoMT):** IR must preserve life-support device function; containment ≠ shutdown.
- **Industrial Manufacturing (IIoT):** IR aligns with **IEC 62443-2-1** and **NIST 800-82** OT-specific guidance.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** IR must include over-the-air (OTA) patching and safety-driver handover protocols.
- **Smart Buildings / Critical Infrastructure:** Coordinated IR with emergency services and regulators.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Computing MTTD and MTTR from Incident Log

**Problem (KTU-Style Numerical):** A CPS operator logs the following 4 security incidents on a SCADA system over a quarter. Compute the Mean Time to Detect (MTTD), Mean Time to Contain (MTTC), and Mean Time to Recover (MTTR). Determine if the system meets its RTO of **2 hours** and RPO of **15 minutes** (data rate = 4 samples/sec).

| Incident | T\_occurred (h) | T\_detected (h) | T\_contained (h) | T\_recovered (h) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I-001 (Ransomware on HMI) | 0.00 | 4.50 | 6.00 | 18.00 |
| I-002 (Unauthorized Modbus write) | 0.00 | 0.75 | 1.25 | 2.00 |
| I-003 (Firmware tamper) | 0.00 | 12.00 | 14.00 | 30.00 |
| I-004 (Phishing → lateral move) | 0.00 | 1.50 | 3.00 | 7.50 |

### Step 1: Compute Detection Delays (T\_detected − T\_occurred)

For I-001:
$$ \Delta T_{detect,1} = 4.50 - 0.00 = 4.50 \text{ hours} $$

For I-002:
$$ \Delta T_{detect,2} = 0.75 - 0.00 = 0.75 \text{ hours} $$

For I-003:
$$ \Delta T_{detect,3} = 12.00 - 0.00 = 12.00 \text{ hours} $$

For I-004:
$$ \Delta T_{detect,4} = 1.50 - 0.00 = 1.50 \text{ hours} $$

### Step 2: Compute MTTD (Mean Time to Detect)

$$ MTTD = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta T_{detect,i}}{N} = \frac{4.50 + 0.75 + 12.00 + 1.50}{4} $$

$$ MTTD = \frac{18.75}{4} = 4.6875 \text{ hours} \approx 4.69 \text{ h} $$

### Step 3: Compute Containment Delays (T\_contained − T\_detected)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{contain,1} &= 6.00 - 4.50 = 1.50 \text{ h} \\
\Delta T_{contain,2} &= 1.25 - 0.75 = 0.50 \text{ h} \\
\Delta T_{contain,3} &= 14.00 - 12.00 = 2.00 \text{ h} \\
\Delta T_{contain,4} &= 3.00 - 1.50 = 1.50 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute MTTC

$$ MTTC = \frac{1.50 + 0.50 + 2.00 + 1.50}{4} = \frac{5.50}{4} = 1.375 \text{ hours} \approx 1.38 \text{ h} $$

### Step 5: Compute Recovery Delays (T\_recovered − T\_contained)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{recover,1} &= 18.00 - 6.00 = 12.00 \text{ h} \\
\Delta T_{recover,2} &= 2.00 - 1.25 = 0.75 \text{ h} \\
\Delta T_{recover,3} &= 30.00 - 14.00 = 16.00 \text{ h} \\
\Delta T_{recover,4} &= 7.50 - 3.00 = 4.50 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Compute MTTR (Mean Time To Recover — using T\_recovered − T\_detected as per NIST convention)

Using the NIST IR convention, MTTR is measured from detection to full recovery:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{MTTR,1} &= 18.00 - 4.50 = 13.50 \text{ h} \\
\Delta T_{MTTR,2} &= 2.00 - 0.75 = 1.25 \text{ h} \\
\Delta T_{MTTR,3} &= 30.00 - 12.00 = 18.00 \text{ h} \\
\Delta T_{MTTR,4} &= 7.50 - 1.50 = 6.00 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$ MTTR = \frac{13.50 + 1.25 + 18.00 + 6.00}{4} = \frac{38.75}{4} = 9.6875 \text{ hours} \approx 9.69 \text{ h} $$

### Step 7: Check RTO Compliance

$$ MTTR_{achieved} = 9.69 \text{ h} \quad ; \quad RTO = 2 \text{ h} $$

Since $9.69 \text{ h} \gg 2 \text{ h}$, **RTO is violated**. The IR strategy must be improved (e.g., pre-staged forensic images, automated containment playbooks, on-call OT engineer rotation).

### Step 8: Check RPO Compliance

$$ DataLoss_{acceptable} = RPO \times DataRate = 15 \text{ min} \times 60 \text{ s/min} \times 4 \text{ samples/s} = 3600 \text{ samples} $$

If backups run every 15 minutes, up to 3600 telemetry samples may be lost per incident. In CPS contexts (e.g., turbine vibration), this is **often unacceptable** — RPO must drop to seconds, requiring **continuous replication** instead of periodic snapshots.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Incident Response Frameworks

| Framework | Originator | Phases / Stages | CPS Suitability | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NIST SP 800-61** | NIST, USA | Preparation → Detection → Containment/Eradication/Recovery → Post-Incident | Moderate (IT-centric) | **Highest** — standard KTU reference |
| **NIST SP 800-82** | NIST, USA | OT-specific guidance on 800-61 | **High** | High for OT/CPS questions |
| **NIST CSF** | NIST, USA | Identify → Protect → Detect → Respond → Recover | High | High |
| **ISO/IEC 27035** | ISO | Plan → Detect → Assess → Respond → Lessons | High | High (international standard) |
| **IEC 62443-2-1** | IEC / ISA | OT security program incl. IR | **Very High** for ICS | Moderate (advanced) |
| **SANS PICERL** | SANS Institute | Preparation → Identification → Containment → Eradication → Recovery → Lessons (6 phases) | High | Moderate |
| **ENISA IR** | EU Agency | Service categories: Pre-event, Mid-event, Post-event | High | Low–Moderate |
| **ITIL 4 Incident Mgmt** | AXELOS | Logging → Categorization → Prioritization → Resolution → Closure | Service-oriented | Low (not CPS-specific) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Module 2 questions, **always default to the NIST SP 800-61 four-phase model** in your answer, supplemented with **NIST SP 800-82** for OT-specific points.

---

## 3.3 CPS-Specific IR Decision Tree (Reasoning Walkthrough)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the analytical reasoning a KTU examiner expects when you answer "How would you handle a CPS incident?"

**Trigger Event:** SCADA HMI shows anomalous valve position. Operator cannot confirm if it is sensor fault, operator error, or cyber attack.

1. **Step 1 — Safety First (Triage):** Is there an *immediate physical hazard* (pressure build-up, overheating, gas leak)?
   - **YES → Initiate Safety Shutdown per pre-approved Safety Instrumented System (SIS) procedure. Notify on-call Safety Officer FIRST.**
   - **NO → Proceed to Step 2.**

2. **Step 2 — Preserve Evidence:** Capture volatile data: current process state, PLC program checksum, network PCAP, HMI screenshots, operator log entries. Do NOT power cycle.

3. **Step 3 — Confirm Incident:** Cross-check anomaly against baseline. Consult OT vendor. Check for known IoC signature.

4. **Step 4 — Classify & Escalate:** Apply severity score $S = L \times I \times S_s + C$. If $S \geq 70$, page CSIRT Lead, Legal, PR, Executive.

5. **Step 5 — Contain (Safety-Aware):** Choose containment that does NOT breach safety case. E.g., block external network but keep internal control loop intact.

6. **Step 6 — Eradicate & Recover:** Rebuild from golden image; re-validate safety function; run integrity check on PLC logic; re-flash firmware from signed source.

7. **Step 7 — Post-Incident:** Forensic root-cause analysis (RCA). Update IR plan. File regulatory report (e.g., CISA, NCIIPC, CERT-In).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Incident Response. List the four phases of the NIST Incident Response Process.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Incident Response is the structured set of procedures undertaken by an organization to detect, analyze, contain, eradicate, and recover from security incidents affecting its information systems, while minimizing impact and enabling rapid restoration of services.

The four phases of the NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response Process are:
1. **Preparation** — Establishing policies, teams, tools, and training *before* incidents occur.
2. **Detection and Analysis** — Identifying potential incidents, triaging alerts, confirming scope and severity.
3. **Containment, Eradication, and Recovery** — Limiting damage, removing the cause, and restoring systems.
4. **Post-Incident Activity** — Lessons learned, plan updates, regulatory reporting, and metric recalculation. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for phases)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between MTTD, MTTC, and MTTR. Why are these metrics particularly important in CPS environments?
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **MTTD (Mean Time to Detect):** Average time elapsed from incident occurrence to its detection by monitoring systems. Measures sensor/SIEM effectiveness.
- **MTTC (Mean Time to Contain):** Average time from detection to successful containment. Measures the speed of response.
- **MTTR (Mean Time to Recover/Repair):** Average time from detection (or containment) to full system recovery. Measures restoration speed. *(1.5 marks for definitions)*

These metrics are critical in CPS because CPS environments are **safety-critical** — every additional second of compromise can translate to physical damage, environmental release, or loss of life. Low MTTD and MTTC reduce the attacker's *dwell time*, limiting blast radius. MTTR is bounded by the RTO defined in the safety case. *(1.5 marks for CPS relevance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part B questions offer *internal choice*. Below, two independent alternative questions are provided — students answer **either** Option A **or** Option B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — CPS Incident Response Strategy Design

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question for 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the four phases of the NIST Incident Response Process. For each phase, give **two CPS-specific examples** of activities undertaken. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A smart water treatment plant uses SCADA to control chemical dosing pumps. One day, operators notice a sudden, unauthorized change in chlorine dosage setpoints. **Design an Incident Response Strategy** for this scenario, clearly addressing: (i) initial safety triage, (ii) containment options, (iii) eradication, and (iv) recovery with safety validation. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) NIST IR Phases with CPS Examples (7 marks):**

1. **Preparation (1.75 marks):**
   - *CPS Example 1:* Maintain a **golden image** of every PLC's firmware and ladder logic, stored offline and cryptographically signed.
   - *CPS Example 2:* Conduct **tabletop exercises** simulating a Triton-style attack on the Safety Instrumented System (SIS), with the on-call Safety Officer participating.

2. **Detection and Analysis (1.75 marks):**
   - *CPS Example 1:* Deploy an **OT-aware IDS** (e.g., Dragos, Nozomi) that understands Modbus/Profinet/DNP3 protocols and flags anomalous write commands to setpoint registers.
   - *CPS Example 2:* Baseline **process behavior** using digital twins; flag deviations in real-time (e.g., chlorine flow vs. expected PID curve).

3. **Containment, Eradication, Recovery (1.75 marks):**
   - *CPS Example 1:* **Network segmentation** at the Purdue Level 1–2 boundary, isolating compromised controllers without breaking the safety loop.
   - *CPS Example 2:* **Failover to a redundant controller** with verified-clean firmware, then revalidate all safety interlocks before resuming operation.

4. **Post-Incident Activity (1.75 marks):**
   - *CPS Example 1:* Conduct a **root-cause analysis** distinguishing cyber cause vs. process cause; update HMI alarm thresholds accordingly.
   - *CPS Example 2:* File a **mandatory regulatory report** (e.g., CISA, NCIIPC, CERT-In) within statutory time window; recalculate MTTD/MTTR.

**(b) Incident Response Strategy — Smart Water Treatment Plant (7 marks):**

**(i) Initial Safety Triage (2 marks):**
The first responder (on-call OT engineer) must first ask: *Is there an immediate public health risk?* Unauthorized chlorine under-dose allows pathogens in drinking water; over-dose creates toxic byproducts. **Page the Plant Safety Manager immediately.** Do NOT shut down the plant yet — a sudden shutdown may cause water hammer, pipe bursts, or uncontrolled chemical reactions. Document the anomaly with HMI screenshots, PLC register values, and operator logs.

**(ii) Containment Options (2 marks):**
- *Network-level:* Block the engineering workstation's network access to the SCADA server; enforce one-way (diode) data flow from corporate to OT.
- *Process-level:* Place the chlorine pump controller in **manual mode** with operator-set flow rates; this prevents attacker from sending new setpoints while preserving the ability to maintain safe dosing.
- *Account-level:* Disable the compromised operator account; rotate all SCADA credentials; force re-authentication.

**(iii) Eradication (1.5 marks):**
- Image the affected SCADA server forensically (preserve volatile data first: RAM, network connections, running services).
- Identify attack vector (likely phishing, lateral movement from IT, or compromised remote access).
- Remove unauthorized software/accounts; re-flash PLC from golden image; verify firmware signatures.
- Apply patches to engineering workstation OS; update antivirus signatures.

**(iv) Recovery with Safety Validation (1.5 marks):**
- Restore SCADA from clean backup; run **end-to-end safety interlock test** (e.g., simulated low-chlorine alarm must trigger E-stop).
- Re-validate PID loops against the digital twin baseline.
- Operate in **elevated monitoring mode** for 72 hours, watching for re-infiltration.
- Re-baseline network traffic; close the IR ticket only after 30 days of clean operation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — IR Metrics, Severity, and CPS Containment

**[KTU University Exam - Alternative Model Question for 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Define MTTD, MTTC, and MTTR. For a CPS safety system, the IR team records the following four incidents (times in minutes): *(7 marks)*

| Incident | T\_occurred | T\_detected | T\_contained | T\_recovered |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 30 | 45 | 180 |
| B | 0 | 5 | 15 | 60 |
| C | 0 | 60 | 90 | 240 |
| D | 0 | 10 | 25 | 90 |

Calculate MTTD, MTTC, and MTTR. If the RTO is **2 hours (120 min)**, is it met? Justify.

**(b)** Discuss the various **containment strategies** in CPS Incident Response. Why is the *safety-first, security-second* principle a critical deviation from traditional IT IR? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) MTTD, MTTC, MTTR Calculation (7 marks):**

**MTTD Calculation (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{detect,A} &= 30 - 0 = 30 \text{ min} \\
\Delta T_{detect,B} &= 5 - 0 = 5 \text{ min} \\
\Delta T_{detect,C} &= 60 - 0 = 60 \text{ min} \\
\Delta T_{detect,D} &= 10 - 0 = 10 \text{ min} \\
MTTD &= \frac{30 + 5 + 60 + 10}{4} = \frac{105}{4} = 26.25 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**MTTC Calculation (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{contain,A} &= 45 - 30 = 15 \text{ min} \\
\Delta T_{contain,B} &= 15 - 5 = 10 \text{ min} \\
\Delta T_{contain,C} &= 90 - 60 = 30 \text{ min} \\
\Delta T_{contain,D} &= 25 - 10 = 15 \text{ min} \\
MTTC &= \frac{15 + 10 + 30 + 15}{4} = \frac{70}{4} = 17.5 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**MTTR Calculation (2 marks):**
Using NIST convention (T\_recovered − T\_detected):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{recover,A} &= 180 - 30 = 150 \text{ min} \\
\Delta T_{recover,B} &= 60 - 5 = 55 \text{ min} \\
\Delta T_{recover,C} &= 240 - 60 = 180 \text{ min} \\
\Delta T_{recover,D} &= 90 - 10 = 80 \text{ min} \\
MTTR &= \frac{150 + 55 + 180 + 80}{4} = \frac{465}{4} = 116.25 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**RTO Compliance Check (1 mark):**
$$ MTTR = 116.25 \text{ min} \quad ; \quad RTO = 120 \text{ min} $$
$$ 116.25 \text{ min} < 120 \text{ min} \implies RTO \text{ is MET, but with only 3.75 min margin.} $$
**Conclusion:** RTO is technically met, but the margin is dangerously thin. The team should aim to reduce MTTR below 90 min for safety buffer.

**(b) CPS Containment Strategies (7 marks):**

**Six Key Containment Strategies (4.5 marks):**

1. **Network Segmentation:** Isolate compromised systems using firewalls, VLANs, DMZs at Purdue Model boundaries. *CPS caveat:* Ensure real-time control loops still meet latency requirements.

2. **Process-Level Isolation:** Place physical processes in manual or local control; bypass the compromised control system. *Example:* A field operator directly actuates a valve, bypassing the PLC.

3. **Account & Credential Containment:** Disable compromised user accounts, rotate shared secrets, force multi-factor re-authentication. *CPS caveat:* Verify that no active safety-critical session is using the account before disabling.

4. **Failover to Redundant Systems:** Switch to backup PLC, HMI, or controller. *CPS caveat:* Verify the backup is itself clean and synchronized; an attacker who has compromised primary may have compromised backup.

5. **Firmware Rollback / Golden Image Restoration:** Re-flash PLC with a cryptographically signed, known-good image. *CPS caveat:* Regulatory frameworks (e.g., FDA 21 CFR Part 11, IEC 61511) may require re-validation of safety functions after firmware change.

6. **Emergency Shutdown (E-Stop):** Last-resort physical shutdown of the process. *CPS caveat:* May itself cause safety incidents (e.g., water hammer, chemical exotherm); must be executed via pre-approved SIS logic, NOT ad-hoc.

**Why "Safety-First, Security-Second" is a CPS-Specific Principle (2.5 marks):**

In traditional IT IR, the responder's first instinct is to **isolate the affected system** to prevent lateral spread — even at the cost of business disruption. In CPS, this reflex is dangerous because:

- **Physical state is unforgiving:** A chemical plant, turbine, or water pump cannot simply be "powered off" mid-process. A naive network isolation may leave a physical system in an unsafe state (e.g., valves half-open, pressure rising).
- **Safety cases are legal documents:** Disabling a safety-critical controller may violate the formal Safety Integrity Level (SIL) certification (IEC 61508/61511). Doing so without authorization can have legal liability and physical consequences.
- **The attacker's goal may be the response itself:** Adversaries like TRISIS deliberately target SIS to provoke an unsafe shutdown. A reflexive "isolate" can be the attacker's *intended outcome*.
- **Human life is the ultimate metric:** RTO and MTTR are bounded not by data loss but by the maximum time the physical process can safely run in degraded mode.

Therefore, CPS IR requires a **safety officer in the loop** who can authorize containment actions only after confirming the *physical* state is safe. This is the **safety-first, security-second** principle — and it is the single biggest differentiator between IT IR and CPS IR.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "Preparation" phase** — most students describe only 3 phases. Preparation is the *most* marks-rich phase.
> 2. **Conflating IT IR with CPS IR** — generic answers that don't address safety, OT protocols (Modbus/DNP3), or the Purdue Model will lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping post-incident activity** — students often end at "recovery" and miss lessons learned and metric recalculation.
> 4. **Unit errors in numerical questions** — MTTD/MTTR must be in consistent time units. Convert minutes ↔ hours carefully.
> 5. **Ignoring RTO/RPO in design questions** — always compare achieved metrics against declared objectives.
> 6. **No mention of regulatory reporting** — IEC 62443, NIST 800-82, CISA, NCIIPC references add marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2: Incident Response Strategies**

### Core Concepts
- **Incident:** Any violation (actual or imminent) of security/safety policy in a CPS.
- **IR Strategy:** Pre-planned, coordinated response covering people, process, technology.
- **CSIRT:** Computer Security Incident Response Team — the organizational unit executing IR.

### NIST 4-Phase Lifecycle (Memorize Order!)
1. **Preparation** — policies, training, tools, baselines, golden images, tabletop drills.
2. **Detection & Analysis** — alert triage, IoC correlation, severity scoring, scope assessment.
3. **Containment, Eradication, Recovery** — short-term vs. long-term containment; remove threat; restore with validation.
4. **Post-Incident Activity** — lessons learned, plan update, regulatory report, metrics.

### Key Metrics (with Formulas)
- **MTTD** = $\sum (T_{detected} - T_{occurred}) / N$
- **MTTC** = $\sum (T_{contained} - T_{detected}) / N$
- **MTTR** = $\sum (T_{recovered} - T_{detected}) / N$
- **Availability** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$
- **Severity Score** $S = L \times I \times S_s + C$
- **RTO** = max tolerable downtime; **RPO** = max tolerable data loss.

### CPS-Specific Differentiators (High-Weight KTU Points)
- **Safety-first, security-second** — physical harm trumps data confidentiality.
- **OT-aware tooling** — protocols (Modbus, DNP3, Profinet, OPC UA), Purdue Model.
- **Pre-approved Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** procedures for emergency shutdown.
- **Regulatory alignment:** IEC 62443, NIST 800-82, IEC 61508/61511, ISO 27035.
- **Golden images** for PLC firmware and HMI workstations.
- **Forensic preservation** of ladder logic, PLC program checksums, RTU state.

### Frameworks Comparison (Quick Recall)
- **NIST SP 800-61** = primary academic reference (4 phases).
- **SANS PICERL** = extended 6-phase model.
- **NIST CSF** = Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover (5 functions).
- **ISO 27035** = international IR standard.
- **IEC 62443** = OT/CPS specific security standard.

### Common Pitfall Triggers (Avoid These)
- Treating CPS IR as identical to IT IR.
- Recommending immediate network isolation without safety check.
- Ignoring regulatory and compliance obligations.
- Skipping the **Preparation** phase.
- Failing to validate safety interlocks after recovery.
- Reporting metrics without comparing to RTO/RPO.

### One-Line Exam Summary
> "CPS Incident Response is a **safety-aware, NIST-aligned, continuously-prepared** process for detecting, containing, eradicating, and recovering from cyber-physical incidents while protecting human life, the environment, and operational continuity."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT604 - ADVANCED CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** is a **fixed-priority, preemptive** scheduling algorithm specifically designed for **periodic real-time tasks** in hard real-time systems. It was formally introduced by **Liu and Layland (1973)** in their seminal paper *"Scheduling Algorithms for Multiprogramming in a Hard Real-Time Environment."*

Under RMS, every periodic task $\tau_i$ is assigned a **static priority** that is **inversely proportional to its period** $T_i$. Mathematically, the RMS priority ordering is:

$$P_i > P_j \iff T_i < T_j$$

Where:
- $P_i$ = Priority of task $\tau_i$
- $T_i$ = Period of task $\tau_i$ (time between successive activations)
- $T_j$ = Period of task $\tau_j$

In plain terms: **the task that arrives most frequently (shortest period) gets the highest priority**, and this priority never changes during runtime.

### 1.2 Key Task Model Parameters (KTU Standard Notation)

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $T_i$ | **Period** of task $\tau_i$ (time between two consecutive job releases) |
| $C_i$ | **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** of task $\tau_i$ |
| $D_i$ | **Relative Deadline** of task $\tau_i$ |
| $U_i$ | **Utilization** of task $\tau_i$ = $C_i / T_i$ |
| $U_{sum}$ | **Total Processor Utilization** = $\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i / T_i$ |
| $R_i$ | **Worst-Case Response Time** of task $\tau_i$ |
| $hp(i)$ | Set of tasks with **higher** priority than $\tau_i$ |
| $n$ | Total number of tasks in the system |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of RMS like a hospital Emergency Room triage system:** 
> 
> Imagine a hospital where patients arrive at different intervals. A heart-attack patient arrives every few minutes (high frequency → high priority), while a routine check-up patient arrives once a month (low frequency → low priority). **The doctor (CPU) always treats the heart-attack patient FIRST, even if the check-up patient arrived earlier.** 
> 
> The "rate" of arrival decides the priority. The **faster** the patient comes, the **higher** the urgency, the **higher** the priority. This is exactly how RMS works with periodic tasks!

### 1.4 Liu & Layland's Original Assumptions (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the classical RMS analysis to be valid, the following **four (4) assumptions** must hold:
> 
> 1. **All tasks are periodic** (jobs arrive at regular intervals $T_i$)
