<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set-A]

**Q: Differentiate between time-triggered and event-triggered communication protocols used in Cyber-Physical Systems. Give one industrial example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Time-Triggered (TT) | Event-Triggered (ET) |
|---|---|---|
| Transmission trigger | Pre-scheduled offline table | Occurrence of an event |
| Clock dependency | Requires globally synchronized clock | No global clock required |
| Worst-case latency | Bounded and deterministic (known a priori) | Depends on load; can degrade under contention |
| Flexibility | Low — schedule must be re-computed for changes | High — adapts dynamically to traffic |
| Typical use | Safety-critical control, avionics | Diagnostics, alarms, soft control |
| **Example** | **TTP, TTEthernet, FlexRay static segment** | **CAN, standard Ethernet, ARINC 429** |

*(Award 1 mark for clear distinction on triggering, 1 mark for latency predictability, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

#### Question A2 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]

**Q: List and briefly explain any three Quality-of-Service (QoS) metrics essential for real-time communication in CPS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Latency (1 mark):** The end-to-end delay from message generation at source to delivery at destination. For hard real-time CPS, latency must be bounded by the application's deadline (e.g., < 1 ms for robotic control, < 4 ms for synchrophasor smart-grid applications).

2. **Jitter (1 mark):** The variation in latency between successive deliveries of the same stream. High jitter degrades control-loop stability. Time-Triggered protocols minimize jitter by design; Ethernet TSN targets < 1 µs.

3. **Packet Loss / Reliability (1 mark):** The fraction of messages that fail to arrive before their deadline. Hard real-time systems demand **DMR = 0**; industrial protocols like WirelessHART and 5G URLLC enforce reliability $\geq 99.999\%$.

*(Optional 4th metric for completeness: bandwidth, determinism, MTBF.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Regular]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and operation of the **FlexRay** protocol for automotive Cyber-Physical Systems. Discuss its static and dynamic segments, dual-channel redundancy, and global time synchronization mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Controller Area Network (CAN)** arbitration mechanism using the dominant-recessive bit principle. Discuss how CAN FD enhances classical CAN and compute the worst-case transmission time for a 64-byte CAN FD frame at 2 Mbps data phase. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — (a) FlexRay Architecture (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Overview [1 mark]**
FlexRay is a hybrid time-triggered + event-triggered protocol designed by the FlexRay Consortium (BMW, Daimler, Bosch, Freescale, NXP, VW) for high-speed X-by-wire automotive applications. It supports data rates up to **10 Mbps** per channel and provides deterministic communication with cycle times as low as **1 ms**.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram (description, 2 marks)**
A FlexRay node consists of:
- **Host CPU** (application layer)
- **FlexRay Communication Controller** (CC) — handles protocol engine
- **Bus Guardian** (BG) — independent hardware watchdog preventing babbling-idiot faults
- **Two physical channels — Channel A and Channel B** — redundant physical links
- **Bus Driver** — physical layer transceiver

Both channels carry identical traffic in parallel (redundancy), so any single-channel failure does not disrupt communication.

**Step 3 — Communication Cycle Structure [2 marks]**
The FlexRay cycle is partitioned into four windows:

- **Static Segment (ST):** TDMA-based, contains a fixed number of static slots (configurable 1–1023). Each slot is assigned to one node. Hard real-time traffic here.
- **Dynamic Segment (DYN):** FTDMA (Flexible TDMA) using minislots. Arbitration by frame ID priority. Soft real-time, event-driven traffic.
- **Symbol Window (SW):** Used for startup, wakeup, and test symbols.
- **Network Idle Time (NIT):** Slack for clock drift compensation and cycle counter increment.

**Step 4 — Synchronization [1 mark]**
Each node measures the deviation of received sync frames and applies a clock correction algorithm (rate + offset correction). Global time precision achieved is typically **< 1 µs** across all nodes, enabling deterministic dual-channel alignment.

**Step 5 — Why FlexRay for CPS [1 mark]**
FlexRay supports fault-tolerant, high-bandwidth, deterministic communication needed for steer-by-wire, brake-by-wire, and adaptive cruise control — applications where classical CAN's 1 Mbps and soft-RT latency is insufficient.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — (b) CAN Arbitration and CAN FD (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Classical CAN Arbitration [2 marks]**
CAN uses CSMA/CA with **bitwise non-destructive priority arbitration**. Each frame starts with an 11-bit (CAN 2.0A) or 29-bit (CAN 2.0B) **Identifier (ID)** field, where **lower ID = higher priority**. The bus uses a wired-AND physical layer:

- A **dominant bit (logical 0)** always overrides a **recessive bit (logical 1)**.
- All nodes transmit simultaneously; the first node to detect that its recessive bit was overwritten by a dominant one **loses arbitration and immediately backs off**.
- The winning node continues without bit loss — hence *non-destructive*.

This guarantees the highest-priority message always wins in bounded time = end-of-identifier field, with no retransmission penalty for the winner.

**Step 2 — CAN FD Enhancements [2 marks]**
CAN FD (CAN with Flexible Data rate, ISO 11898-1:2015) extends classical CAN:

- **Data field length increased** from 8 bytes to **64 bytes** — fewer frames per transaction.
- **Two bit rates** — arbitration phase at nominal rate (e.g., 1 Mbps), data phase at higher rate (e.g., 2, 5, or 8 Mbps).
- **Improved CRC** (up to 21-bit CRC for longer frames).
- **Improved error detection** for payloads > 16 bytes.

**Step 3 — Worst-Case Transmission Time Calculation [3 marks]**

For a **64-byte (512-bit)** CAN FD frame at 2 Mbps data phase, with nominal arbitration phase at 1 Mbps:

Assume:
- Arbitration + header: 1 Mbps
- Data + CRC + ACK: 2 Mbps
- Header portion (SOF, ID, IDE, FDF, r0, BRS, DLC, stuff bits) ≈ 30 bits at 1 Mbps
- Data field: 512 bits at 2 Mbps
- CRC field: 33 bits (worst case) at 2 Mbps
- ACK + EOF + IFS: 19 bits at 1 Mbps

$$T_{arb} = \frac{30 \text{ bits}}{1 \times 10^6} = 30 \text{ }\mu s$$

$$T_{data} = \frac{512 + 33}{2 \times 10^6} = 272.5 \text{ }\mu s$$

$$T_{tail} = \frac{19}{1 \times 10^6} = 19 \text{ }\mu s$$

$$T_{tx,total} \approx 30 + 272.5 + 19 = 321.5 \text{ }\mu s$$

This is **2–3× faster** than classical CAN transmitting eight 8-byte frames to move 64 bytes of data, demonstrating why CAN FD is the de-facto upgrade for automotive CPS.

---

#### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary] — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the **TTEthernet (SAE AS6802)** architecture. Explain its three traffic classes — Time-Triggered (TT), Rate-Constrained (RC), and Best-Effort (BE) — with their respective QoS guarantees and synchronization mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **WirelessHART** protocol stack and its suitability for industrial CPS. Discuss how TDMA scheduling combined with channel hopping achieves determinism in a wireless environment, and compute the maximum number of superframes per 10-second period if each superframe contains 100 slots of 10 ms each. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — (a) TTEthernet Architecture (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Standard and Context [1 mark]**
TTEthernet (SAE AS6802) is a time-triggered Ethernet standard developed by TTTech for avionics and industrial CPS. It reuses IEEE 802.3 Ethernet physical and data-link layers but adds deterministic scheduling on top.

**Step 2 — Three Traffic Classes [3 marks]**

| Class | Triggering | Latency Bound | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TT (Time-Triggered)** | Pre-scheduled offline; transmitted at known instants | Bounded by one cluster cycle (< 100 µs typical) | Flight control, brake-by-wire |
| **RC (Rate-Constrained)** | Event-driven; admitted only if bandwidth allowance remains | Bounded by RC bandwidth allocation (e.g., < 1 ms) | Audio/video, diagnostics |
| **BE (Best-Effort)** | Standard Ethernet, no guarantees | No bound | Firmware updates, logging |

TT frames have **absolute priority** over RC and BE. RC is policed by a leaky-bucket mechanism. BE uses residual bandwidth.

**Step 3 — Synchronization Mechanism [2 marks]**
Synchronization is performed using **Protocol Control Frames (PCFs)** exchanged among **Synchronization Masters (SMs)**. The procedure:
- SMs transmit PCFs at scheduled times.
- **Compression Masters (CMs)** collect PCFs and compute a **cluster consensus time** (average of all received PCF timestamps).
- **Synchronization Clients (SCs)** correct their local clocks to match the consensus.
- Achieved synchronization precision: **< 1 µs** (typical 100 ns).

**Step 4 — Suitability for CPS [1 mark]**
TTEthernet allows mixed-criticality traffic on a single physical network, reducing harness weight in aircraft and enabling partitioned safety certification (e.g., DAL-A flight controls and DAL-D passenger Wi-Fi on the same cable).

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — (b) WirelessHART for Industrial CPS (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — WirelessHART Overview [1 mark]**
WirelessHART (IEC 62591) is the **first open wireless standard** specifically designed for process automation. Built on IEEE 802.15.4 PHY (2.4 GHz, O-QPSK, 250 kbps), it adds a deterministic MAC layer using **TDMA + channel hopping + mesh routing**.

**Step 2 — Protocol Stack Layers [2 marks]**
- **Physical Layer:** IEEE 802.15.4, 2.4 GHz, 16 channels (11–26), 250 kbps.
- **Data Link Layer:** TDMA-based with 10 ms time slots; CSMA/CA only for association.
- **Network Layer:** Mesh routing with redundant paths; every device is a router.
- **Application Layer:** HART command set (compatible with wired HART).

**Step 3 — Determinism via TDMA + Channel Hopping [2 marks]**
- All transmissions are scheduled in absolute time slots assigned by the **Network Manager**.
- A **superframe** consists of a fixed number of slots; multiple superframes repeat over the **network period** (typically 10 s).
- **Channel hopping**: each slot is assigned a different channel (from a predefined hopping sequence), so narrow-band interference and Rayleigh fading are statistically overcome.
- Combined effect: predictable latency, high reliability (> 99.9% typical), and resilience to industrial multipath and EMI.

**Step 4 — Superframe Calculation [2 marks]**

Given:
- Network period: $T_{NP} = 10$ s = $10{,}000$ ms
- Slots per superframe: $S_{SF} = 100$
- Slot duration: $T_{slot} = 10$ ms
- Superframe duration: $T_{SF} = S_{SF} \cdot T_{slot} = 100 \times 10 = 1000$ ms

Maximum number of superframes per network period:

$$N_{SF} = \left\lfloor \frac{T_{NP}}{T_{SF}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{10{,}000}{1{,}000} \right\rfloor = 10 \text{ superframes}$$

If channel redundancy requires retransmission within a network period, the effective number of *useful* superframes reduces accordingly.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic (Read Before Writing Your Exam!)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the blocking term $B_m$** in CAN worst-case response time — costs 2 marks. **Examiner's tip:** always mention "non-preemptive arbitration causes blocking by lower-priority frames."
>
> 2. **Confusing propagation delay with transmission delay.** Transmission delay = bits / bit-rate; propagation delay = distance / wave-speed. Mixing them up loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Omitting preamble/IFG overhead** in TDMA slot calculation. Real MAC layers have PHY preamble and inter-frame gap — neglecting these overstates slot utilization.
>
> 4. **Mis-stating the relationship "lower CAN ID = higher priority."** Students often write it backwards.
>
> 5. **Treating FlexRay as purely time-triggered.** FlexRay has a **dynamic segment** — write "hybrid protocol" explicitly.
>
> 6. **Missing the role of the Bus Guardian in FlexRay.** This hardware fault-tolerance block is unique and a frequent 2-mark question.
>
> 7. **In WirelessHART, not mentioning channel hopping.** Just saying "TDMA" is incomplete — examiners expect explicit mention of frequency diversity.
>
> 8. **Writing "real-time = fast."** Real-time ≠ fast. Real-time = **deterministic within a deadline**. Wrong definition = lose 1 mark in any definition question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — High-Density Summary (Read 30 min Before Exam)**

- ☐ **Real-Time ≠ Fast.** Real-Time = **deterministic deadline satisfaction**. Memorize this distinction.
- ☐ **Three triggering types:** TT (pre-scheduled), ET (event-driven), Hybrid (TT + ET coexisting).
- ☐ **Five core metrics:** Latency, Jitter, WCET, Determinism, Reliability ($MTBF$ / $DMR$).
- ☐ **Joseph–Pandya formula** for CAN-style response time — remember **$B_m$ blocking term**!
- ☐ **TDMA cycle time** = sum of all slot times including preamble + IFG.
- ☐ **CAN ID = priority:** lower ID → higher priority; logical 0 (dominant) wins over 1 (recessive).
- ☐ **CAN FD upgrades:** 64-byte payload, dual bit rate (slower arbitration, faster data), improved CRC.
- ☐ **FlexRay cycle:** Static (TDMA) + Dynamic (FTDMA) + Symbol + NIT. Dual channel = redundancy. Bus Guardian = fault containment.
- ☐ **TTEthernet (AS6802):** 3 traffic classes TT > RC > BE. PCF-based sync, < 1 µs precision.
- ☐ **WirelessHART:** IEEE 802.15.4 + TDMA (10 ms slots) + channel hopping + mesh routing. 10 s network period.
- ☐ **TSN (IEEE 802.1Q):** Time-Aware Shaper with scheduled gate operations; sub-100 µs latency on standard Ethernet.
- ☐ **EtherCAT:** processing-on-the-fly; cycle times < 100 µs; distributed clock sync.
- ☐ **5G URLLC:** target 1 ms latency, $1-10^{-5}$ reliability; enables wireless hard real-time CPS.
- ☐ **OPC UA Pub/Sub over TSN:** the Industry 4.0 lingua franca for vendor-neutral CPS communication.
- ☐ **Industrial verticals mapping:** Automotive → CAN/FlexRay/CAN FD; Avionics → TTP/TTEthernet; Process → WirelessHART; Factory → EtherCAT/PROFINET IRT.
- ☐ **Exam mantra:** Always write **"bounded latency"** + **"deterministic"** + **"deadline satisfaction"** when defining any real-time concept.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network-on-Chip (NoC) and Time-Triggered Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT604 - ADVANCED CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network-on-Chip (NoC) and Time-Triggered Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Network-on-Chip (NoC) — Formal Definition

A **Network-on-Chip (NoC)** is a packet-switched on-chip interconnection network that interconnects multiple Intellectual Property (IP) cores, processing elements (PEs), memory blocks, and I/O subsystems on a single System-on-Chip (SoC) using routers, point-to-point links, and a layered communication protocol stack. It replaces shared-bus and crossbar architectures when the number of integrated cores exceeds roughly **8 to 16**, where the scalability, energy, and bandwidth limitations of buses become critical bottlenecks in Cyber-Physical System (CPS) SoCs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Statement:** *An NoC is a structured, modular, packet-based communication sub-system that provides scalable, predictable, and parallel data transport between computational and storage resources on a single die, with well-defined Quality-of-Service (QoS) guarantees.*

### 1.2 Time-Triggered Protocol (TTP) — Formal Definition

A **Time-Triggered Protocol (TTP)** is a deterministic, clock-synchronized communication protocol in which all message transmissions are scheduled at **pre-determined, statically allocated time slots** within a recurring TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access) round. The most prominent industrial implementations are **TTP/C (SAE AS6003)** for safety-critical automotive/aerospace CPS and **Time-Triggered Ethernet (SAE AS6802)** for high-speed distributed CPS clusters.

> [!NOTE]
> Time-triggered systems belong to the class of **deterministic CPS architectures**, where temporal correctness is as important as logical correctness — a fundamental requirement in ISO 26262 (ASIL-D) and DO-178C compliant systems.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Why It Works |
|---|---|---|
| **NoC** | A city's *road network* with traffic lights (routers) controlling cars (packets) | Just as a city road network scales better than a single-lane alley, an NoC scales to many cores better than a shared bus. |
| **Time-Triggered Protocol** | A *pre-scheduled railway timetable* where every train leaves its platform at a fixed second | Predictability: just as no two trains collide because slots are pre-booked, no two nodes transmit simultaneously because slots are pre-assigned. |
| **TDMA Round** | A *class timetable* where each teacher gets a fixed period | Each node gets its own exclusive communication window, guaranteeing bounded latency. |
| **Router** | A *traffic roundabout* directing incoming vehicles to outgoing streets | The router decodes the destination address and forwards the packet along the correct outgoing link. |
| **Wormhole Switching** | A *snake* whose head has entered the next room before its tail has left the current one | The packet is forwarded flit-by-flit, reducing per-hop buffering drastically. |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU:** *NoC = Many Cores talking like Internet (routers, packets, addresses). TTP = Many ECUs talking like Train Timetable (slots, sync, deterministic).*

### 1.4 Key Terminology Standardized Across Industry

- **PE (Processing Element)** — IP core, CPU, DSP, or memory tile
- **NI (Network Interface)** — Bridges a PE to the NoC, packetizes/de-packetizes data
- **Flit (Flow Control Unit)** — Smallest unit on which flow control is performed (e.g., **64 bits**)
- **Phit (Physical Unit)** — Number of bits transferred per clock cycle on a physical link
- **TDMA Round (Cluster Cycle)** — One complete sequence of all node slots
- **Global Time Base** — Time reference common to all cluster nodes
- **Guard Band** — Idle time inserted between slots to absorb clock drift
- **PCF (Protocol Control Frame)** — Synchronization frame in TTEthernet (AS6802)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 NoC Architectural Layers

The NoC architecture is decomposed into **four functional layers** that mirror the OSI reference model:

1. **Physical Layer** — Wires, repeaters, clock distribution, link drivers
2. **Data-Link Layer** — Flit-level framing, error detection (CRC), flow control (credits, ACK/NACK)
3. **Network Layer** — Routing (XY, adaptive), addressing, topology
4. **Transport/Application Layer** — End-to-end sessions, QoS, OS-level API

### 2.2 NoC Topology Comparison

| Topology | Diameter (D) | Bisection BW | Node Degree | CPS Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2D Mesh (n×n) | $2(n-1)$ | $n$ links | 4 (edge: 3, corner: 2) | General-purpose MPSoC |
| 2D Torus (n×n) | $n$ | $2n$ | 4 (uniform) | High-throughput DPS |
| Ring | $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor$ | 2 | 2 | Low-cost, predictable |
| Star | 2 | 1 (center) | 1 (center: N-1) | Centralized control |
| Fat-Tree | $2 \log_2 N$ | High | Variable | Data-center-on-chip |
| Octagon | 3 | 4 | 3 | 8-core DSP clusters |
| Hypercube (k-dim) | $k$ | $2^{k-1}$ | $k$ | Fault-tolerant avionics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bisection Bandwidth (B_bis):** Total bandwidth crossing an imaginary line that cuts the network into two equal halves. **It is the dominant metric of NoC throughput for uniform random traffic.**

### 2.3 Routing Algorithms — Operational Logic

**Step 1: Receive** — Header flit arrives at input port of router.

**Step 2: Decode** — Destination address parsed; route computed.

**Step 3: Arbitrate** — Output port requested; crossbar arbitrates with other inputs requesting same output.

**Step 4: Forward** — Crossbar connects input to output; flit is forwarded.

**Step 5: Update** — Virtual-channel (VC) / credit counters decremented.

#### Deterministic vs. Adaptive Routing

| Property | Deterministic (XY) | Adaptive (e.g., DyAD) |
|---|---|---|
| Path | Unique, function of (src, dst) | Depends on congestion state |
| Implementation | Combinational logic | Routing table + probes |
| Deadlock-free | Always (in mesh) | Requires VC or turn model |
| Load balancing | Poor | Good |
| Latency predictability | **High** ← Preferred in CPS | Variable |
| Typical KTU application | Hard real-time CPS | Soft real-time media |

#### Turn Model Rules (Escape Deadlocks)

The **Turn Model** prohibits one turn in each of two cyclic sub-networks to break the four cyclic dependencies. Three variants:

- **West-First:** First go West, then any combination
- **North-Last:** Last move is North
- **Negative-First:** First go in the negative direction (South or West)

### 2.4 Switching Techniques — Core 'Why' and 'How'

| Technique | Buffer per Packet | Per-Hop Latency | Buffer per Node | Why Used in CPS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Store-and-Forward (SAF) | Full packet | $L/B + t_r$ | High | Reliability, not for NoC |
| Virtual Cut-Through (VCT) | Full packet (only on contention) | $H \cdot t_r + L/B$ | High | Throughput-optimal |
| **Wormhole** | Few flits per VC | $H \cdot t_r + L/B$ | **Low** | **Preferred in NoC — low area** |
| Circuit Switching | Path-setup flits only | $L/B$ after setup | Minimal | Streaming, predictable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wormhole is the dominant NoC switching scheme** because it requires only a few flit buffers per virtual channel (~4–8 flits), enabling high-frequency, low-area routers — a critical constraint in CPS edge SoCs.

### 2.5 Time-Triggered Architecture (TTA) — Operational Logic

**Step 1: Cold Start** — One node designated *Cold-Start Master* broadcasts initial time.

**Step 2: Sync Phase** — All nodes exchange MACs (Membership Acknowledgment) and time-stamps.

**Step 3: Drift Correction** — Each node computes its own drift $\delta_i$ w.r.t. cluster time and adjusts.

**Step 4: Steady State** — Cluster Cycle (TDMA round) proceeds; each node transmits only in its slot.

**Step 5: Fault Detection** — A node that fails to transmit in its slot is voted out (Byzantine fault tolerance).

### 2.6 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_{mesh} = 2(\sqrt{N}-1)$ | Diameter of $n \times n$ mesh, $N=n^2$ | hops |
| 2 | $D_{torus} = \lfloor \sqrt{N}/2 \rfloor$ | Diameter of $n \times n$ wrapped torus | hops |
| 3 | $L_{wormhole} = H \cdot t_r + (L/B) + t_c$ | End-to-end latency (wormhole) | seconds |
| 4 | $L_{VCT} = H \cdot t_r + (L/B) + t_c$ | Same as wormhole **if no blocking** | seconds |
| 5 | $L_{SAF} = H \cdot (t_r + L/B) + t_c$ | Store-and-forward latency | seconds |
| 6 | $\Theta_{max} = 2 B_{link} / N$ | Max throughput per node (uniform mesh) | flits/cycle |
| 7 | $B_{bis} = N \cdot B_{link} / 2$ | Bisection bandwidth of mesh | bits/s |
| 8 | $E_{bit} = E_{router} + E_{link}$ | Per-bit energy: router + link | J/bit |
| 9 | $\rho = L / (L + H)$ | Flit occupancy fraction (wormhole) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $f_{TDMA} = N_{slots} \cdot T_{slot}$ | Cluster cycle period | seconds |
| 11 | $\pi = 2 \cdot \Delta_{max}$ | Cluster precision (TTP/C, AS6802) | seconds |
| 12 | $\Delta_{max} \leq T_{slot} - 2t_{prop} - t_{guard}$ | Max tolerable drift per node | seconds |
| 13 | $\eta_{TDMA} = (T_{slot} - T_{guard}) / T_{slot}$ | Slot utilization efficiency | dimensionless |
| 14 | $T_{slot} = (L_{msg} / B) + 2 t_{prop} + T_{guard}$ | Slot duration budget | seconds |
| 15 | $U = \frac{\sum T_{active}}{\sum T_{slot}}$ | Channel utilization (n slots) | dimensionless |

Where:
- $H$ = number of hops, $L$ = packet length (bits), $B$ = link bandwidth (bits/s)
- $t_r$ = router pipeline delay, $t_c$ = contention delay, $t_{prop}$ = wire propagation delay
- $\pi$ = synchronization precision, $\Delta_{max}$ = max clock drift
- $T_{guard}$ = guard band to absorb drift

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (ISO 26262 ASIL-D):** TTP/C powers the *By-Wire* systems in Airbus A380, Boeing 787, Audi zFAS, Bosch ESP.
- **Industrial Automation:** TTEthernet (AS6802) deployed in the *NASA Orion* crew vehicle and Airbus A350 integrated modular avionics.
- **Edge-AI CPS SoCs:** Mesh NoCs (e.g., Tilelink, Ariane, OpenPiton) interconnect RISC-V clusters in robotics and autonomous driving SoCs.
- **Medical CPS:** TTP enables pacemaker firmware verification (Premisso project) by providing deterministic execution budgets.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Latency of Wormhole Switching

We derive the end-to-end latency for a packet of length $L$ bits traversing $H$ hops over links of bandwidth $B$ bits/s, with per-router pipeline delay $t_r$ and zero contention.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{wormhole} &= \underbrace{H \cdot t_r}_{\text{per-hop pipeline}} + \underbrace{\frac{L}{B}}_{\text{serialization of last flit}} + t_c \\[4pt]
\text{Header flit traverses H routers: } & t_h = H \cdot t_r \\[4pt]
\text{Body flits pipeline behind: } & t_b = \frac{L}{B} \\[4pt]
\text{Therefore:} \quad L &= H \cdot t_r + \frac{L}{B} + t_c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- The header flit must cross $H$ routers, each consuming $t_r$ cycles → contributes $H \cdot t_r$.
- Once the header is in flight, the body is pipelined; the time for the **entire packet** to be clocked out of the source is $\frac{L}{B}$ — this is the *serialization latency*.
- The tail flit exits the destination after the same $\frac{L}{B}$ since wormhole keeps the path open.
- $t_c$ is the waiting time due to blocked output ports.

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU style):**
Given: $H = 4$ hops, $L = 256$ bits, $B = 32$ Gbps, $t_r = 5$ ns, $t_c = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H \cdot t_r &= 4 \times 5~\text{ns} = 20~\text{ns} \\[4pt]
L / B &= 256 / (32 \times 10^{9}) = 8 \times 10^{-9}~\text{s} = 8~\text{ns} \\[4pt]
L_{wormhole} &= 20 + 8 + 0 = 28~\text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Compare with **Store-and-Forward** under the same conditions:
> $L_{SAF} = H \cdot (t_r + L/B) = 4 \times (5 + 8) = 52$ ns — almost **2× the wormhole latency**. This is why NoCs use wormhole.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cluster Cycle Period for TTP/C

For a TTP/C cluster of $N$ nodes, each granted one slot per cluster cycle, with message length $L_{msg}$ bits, link bandwidth $B$ bits/s, propagation delay $t_{prop}$, and a guard band $T_{guard}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{slot} &= \frac{L_{msg}}{B} + 2 t_{prop} + T_{guard} \\[4pt]
T_{cycle} &= N \cdot T_{slot} + T_{idle} \\[4pt]
T_{cycle} &= N \cdot \left( \frac{L_{msg}}{B} + 2 t_{prop} + T_{guard} \right) + T_{idle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- Each slot must hold (a) the message transmission time $L_{msg}/B$, (b) round-trip propagation $2 t_{prop}$ for ACKs, and (c) a guard band.
- The cluster cycle is the sum of all $N$ slots plus any idle time used for reconfiguration or async traffic.

**Numerical Example (KTU style):**
$N = 8$ nodes, $L_{msg} = 1024$ bits, $B = 1$ Mbps, $t_{prop} = 1~\mu s$, $T_{guard} = 5~\mu s$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{slot} &= \frac{1024}{10^{6}} + 2 \times 10^{-6} + 5 \times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
&= 1024 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times 10^{-6} + 5 \times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
&= 1.031 \times 10^{-3}~\text{s} = 1.031~\text{ms} \\[4pt]
T_{cycle} &= 8 \times 1.031~\text{ms} = 8.248~\text{ms} \\[4pt]
f_{cycle} &= 1 / T_{cycle} \approx 121.24~\text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Tolerable Clock Drift

For the cluster to remain synchronized, a node's drift $\Delta_i$ between two sync events (period $T_{sync}$) must be less than the slack within one slot:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{max} &\le T_{slot} - 2 t_{prop} - T_{guard} \\[4pt]
\text{and} \quad \Delta_{max} &= \rho_{osc} \cdot T_{sync}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\rho_{osc}$ is the oscillator's relative drift (e.g., $\rho_{osc} = 50$ ppm = $50 \times 10^{-6}$).

**Numerical Example:**
$T_{slot} = 1.031$ ms, $t_{prop} = 1~\mu s$, $T_{guard} = 5~\mu s$, $\rho_{osc} = 50$ ppm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{max} &\le 1031 - 2 - 5 = 1024~\mu s \\[4pt]
T_{sync}^{max} &= \Delta_{max} / \rho_{osc} = 1024 \times 10^{-6} / (50 \times 10^{-6}) = 20.48~\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means resynchronization must occur **at least every 20 seconds** — practically, TTP/C resyncs every cycle (few ms) for safety.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Throughput Bound for 2D Mesh Under Uniform Traffic

For an $n \times n$ mesh ($N = n^2$ nodes) with uniform random traffic and each link bandwidth $B_{link}$ flits/cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average hop count } \bar{H} &\approx \frac{2n}{3} \\[4pt]
\text{Saturating throughput per node } \Theta_{sat} &\approx \frac{2 B_{link}}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

The $\frac{2n}{3}$ result comes from averaging Manhattan distance over the $N^2$ source-destination pairs; the $\frac{1}{N}$ factor emerges from bisection bandwidth sharing (Dally's result).

**Numerical Example:**
$n = 8$ mesh, $N = 64$, $B_{link} = 1$ flit/cycle.

$$
\Theta_{sat} = \frac{2 \times 1}{64} = 0.03125~\text{flits/cycle/node}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This is why larger meshes see *lower* per-node throughput for uniform traffic — scalability is a fundamental NoC design trade-off and a common **KTU 14-mark** derivation question.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing NoC vs. TTP

| Criterion | NoC (intra-chip) | TTP (inter-chip / cluster) |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Intra-SoC, nanosecond | Inter-ECU, microsecond–millisecond |
| Synchronization | Local clock / mesochronous | Global time, sub-microsecond precision |
| Topology | Mesh, torus, fat-tree | Star, bus, dual-redundant bus |
| Determinism | Best-effort to hard real-time | **Hard real-time by construction** |
| Standardization | None (proprietary) | SAE AS6003 (TTP/C), AS6802 (TTE) |
| CPS Domain | Edge-AI, on-chip sensor fusion | Automotive x-by-wire, avionics |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 NoC 2D Mesh Topology with XY Router

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P00["PE 00"]:::pe --> R0["R 00"]:::r
    P01["PE 01"]:::pe --> R0
    P10["PE 10"]:::pe --> R1["R 10"]:::r
    P11["PE 11"]:::pe --> R1

    R0 <-->|East Link| R1
    R0 <-->|South Link| R2["R 01"]:::r
    R1 <-->|South Link| R3["R 11"]:::r
    R2 <-->|East Link| R3

    R2 <-->|North| R0
    R3 <-->|North| R1

    classDef pe fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef r fill:#ffd966,stroke:#996600,color:#000
```

**Description of flow:** Each tile contains one PE attached to one router (R). Routers are connected to North/South/East/West neighbors; XY routing first moves in X (horizontal), then Y (vertical) — guaranteeing deadlock-free traversal.

### 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Wormhole NoC Router

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NI["Network Interface\nfrom PE"] --> INBUF["Input Buffer\n(4-8 flits per VC)"]:::buf
    INBUF --> RC["Route Compute\n+ VC Alloc"]:::logic
    RC --> SA["Switch Allocator"]:::logic
    SA --> XB["Crossbar\n5x5 fabric"]:::xb
    XB --> OUTBUF["Output Buffer / Link"]:::buf
    OUTBUF --> EXT["To Neighbor Router"]

    classDef buf fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000
    classDef xb fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
```

**Key datapath stages (4-stage pipeline):**
1. **Buffer Write (BW)** — incoming flit stored
2. **Route Compute (RC)** — destination parsed
3. **VC Allocation (VCA)** — virtual channel assigned
4. **Switch Allocation (SA)** + **Crossbar Traversal (ST)** — flit forwarded

### 4.3 Time-Triggered Architecture (TTA) Cluster

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CM["Cold-Start\nMaster"]:::master
    N1["Node 1\n(ECU: Brake)"]:::node
    N2["Node 2\n(ECU: Steer)"]:::node
    N3["Node 3\n(ECU: Throttle)"]:::node
    N4["Node 4\n(Gateway)"]:::node

    CM <-->|Time Sync + Data| BUS1["Channel A\n(Redundant)"]:::ch
    CM <-->|Time Sync + Data| BUS2["Channel B\n(Redundant)"]:::ch
    N1 <--> BUS1
    N1 <--> BUS2
    N2 <--> BUS1
    N2 <--> BUS2
    N3 <--> BUS1
    N3 <--> BUS2
    N4 <--> BUS1
    N4 <--> BUS2

    classDef master fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef ch fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#666666,color:#000
```

**Description of flow:** Two physically replicated channels provide Byzantine fault tolerance. The Cold-Start Master initiates cluster formation; once running, all nodes use the **Globally Synchronized Time Base** and transmit strictly within their pre-assigned TDMA slots.

### 4.4 TDMA Cluster Cycle (Round) — Slot Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title TTP/C Cluster Cycle - 8 nodes - TDMA round
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s

    section Channel A
    Slot 1 (Node 1)    :a1, 0,    1ms
    Slot 2 (Node 2)    :a2, 1ms,  1ms
    Slot 3 (Node 3)    :a3, 2ms,  1ms
    Slot 4 (Node 4)    :a4, 3ms,  1ms
    Slot 5 (Node 5)    :a5, 4ms,  1ms
    Slot 6 (Node 6)    :a6, 5ms,  1ms
    Slot 7 (Node 7)    :a7, 6ms,  1ms
    Slot 8 (Node 8)    :a8, 7ms,  1ms
```

**Description of flow:** Each node owns an exclusive 1 ms window. The cycle repeats every 8 ms. Guard bands (not shown) sit at slot boundaries to absorb clock drift.

### 4.5 Time-Triggered Ethernet (AS6802) Synchronization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SM1["Sync Master 1"]:::sm
    SM2["Sync Master 2"]:::sm
    CM1["Compression Master 1"]:::cm
    CM2["Compression Master 2"]:::cm
    N1["End System A"]:::es
    N2["End System B"]:::es
    SW1["TTE Switch"]:::sw
    SW2["TTE Switch"]:::sw

    SM1 --> SW1
    SM2 --> SW2
    CM1 --> SW1
    CM2 --> SW2
    N1 --> SW1
    N2 --> SW2
    SW1 <--> SW2

    classDef sm fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,color:#000
    classDef cm fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef es fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef sw fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
```

**Description of flow:**
1. **Sync Masters (SMs)** transmit *PCF (Protocol Control Frames)* at pre-defined times.
2. **Switches** receive and forward PCFs, recording arrival times.
3. **Compression Masters (CMs)** compute the *integrated/grand-master time* and broadcast it back.
4. **End Systems** align their local clocks to the grand-master time, achieving **sub-microsecond cluster precision**.
