# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define IoT Integration in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. List the five layers of the IoT-CPS reference architecture.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
IoT Integration in CPS is the systematic process of interconnecting heterogeneous physical sensors, actuators, communication networks, edge/fog computing nodes, and cloud services into a unified, interoperable, real-time information fabric that enables closed-loop interaction between the cyber and physical domains.

**Five Layers (2 marks):**
1. **Perception Layer** – Sensors and actuators interacting with the physical world.
2. **Network Layer** – Communication protocols (Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRa, BLE, MQTT, CoAP).
3. **Middleware / Edge-Fog Layer** – Protocol translation, local processing, security.
4. **Application / Service Layer** – APIs, microservices, digital twin, dashboards.
5. **Business / Management Layer** – SLAs, billing, compliance, lifecycle management.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional]**
*Differentiate between MQTT and CoAP protocols for IoT-CPS integration. State one scenario where each is preferred.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | **MQTT** | **CoAP** |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP (reliable) | UDP (lightweight) |
| Pattern | Publish/Subscribe with broker | Request/Response (REST-like) |
| Header size | 2 bytes minimum | 4 bytes minimum |
| QoS | 0, 1, 2 (at-least/at-most/exactly once) | Confirmable / Non-confirmable |
| Latency | ~50–200 ms | ~10–50 ms |
| Use case | Smart home telemetry (high reliability) | Constrained sensor networks (low power) |

**Preferred scenarios (1 mark):**
- **MQTT preferred:** Smart home hub publishing temperature to a cloud dashboard — reliability matters more than latency.
- **CoAP preferred:** Battery-powered soil moisture sensor in a smart farm — low energy, small payload, periodic POST.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each**. You must answer **either Q-A OR Q-B** (internal choice).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **five-layer IoT-CPS reference architecture** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each layer in enabling closed-loop cyber-physical interaction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A linear IoT sensor network consists of $N = 20$ nodes. Each node transmits a packet of $k = 2000$ bits over a distance $d = 15$ m. Use the first-order radio model with $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit and $\varepsilon_{amp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ (multipath, $n=4$). Compute the **total energy dissipation** and the **network lifetime** if each node has a 3.7 V, 1500 mAh battery and transmits once every 30 seconds. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The five-layer IoT-CPS reference architecture (aligned with ITU-T Y.2060 and oneM2M) is:

**Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer (1.5 marks):**
This layer interfaces directly with the physical process. It includes:
- Sensors (temperature, pressure, vibration, gas, vision, LiDAR, IMU).
- Actuators (motors, valves, relays, heaters).
- Hardware abstraction for vendor-independent integration.
- Energy harvesting interfaces (solar, vibrational).
- Multi-modal sensor fusion (Kalman, particle filters) to estimate the state vector $\mathbf{x}(t)$.

**Layer 2 — Network / Transmission Layer (1.5 marks):**
Handles data transport. Key protocols:
- **Short-range:** BLE, Zigbee, Wi-Fi.
- **Long-range:** LoRa, NB-IoT, Sigfox.
- **Wired industrial:** CAN, Modbus, Profinet, TSN.
- **IP-based:** TCP/UDP, MQTT, CoAP, DDS, AMQP.
For real-time CPS, **DDS** and **TSN** are preferred due to bounded latency.

**Layer 3 — Middleware / Edge-Fog Layer (1.5 marks):**
The "smart glue" providing:
- Protocol translation (e.g., Modbus → MQTT → Kafka).
- Stream processing (Apache Flink, Spark Streaming).
- Local analytics and edge ML inference.
- Security (TLS/DTLS, OAuth 2.0, X.509).
- Caching, queuing, and priority scheduling for deterministic latency.

**Layer 4 — Application / Service Layer (1 mark):**
- REST/GraphQL/gRPC APIs.
- Digital Twin synchronization.
- Dashboards (Grafana, Power BI).
- Microservices and serverless functions.

**Layer 5 — Business / Management Layer (0.5 marks):**
- SLAs, billing, regulatory compliance (GDPR, DPDP).
- Device lifecycle and OTA updates.

**Closed-loop interaction:** Sensors (L1) → Network (L2) → Middleware/Edge (L3) decides control action → Actuator driver (L1) closes the loop. The Cloud (L4, L5) provides global optimization but the *fast* loop stays on-device.

*(Draw the block diagram from Section 4.1 for full 7 marks — examiner awards 1 extra mark for a labeled diagram.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Recall per-node energy formulas.**

$$E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{elec}\cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp}\cdot k \cdot d^{n}$$

$$E_{rx}(k) = E_{elec}\cdot k$$

**Step 2 — Substitute given values.**

Given: $k=2000$ bits, $d=15$ m, $E_{elec}=50\times10^{-9}$ J/bit, $\varepsilon_{amp}=0.0013\times10^{-12}$ J/bit/m⁴, $n=4$.

$$E_{tx} = (50\times10^{-9})(2000) + (0.0013\times10^{-12})(2000)(15^{4})$$

Compute $15^{4}$:

$$15^{2} = 225,\quad 15^{4} = 225^{2} = 50\,625$$

$$E_{tx} = 1.0\times10^{-4} + (1.3\times10^{-15})(2000)(50\,625)$$

$$E_{tx} = 1.0\times10^{-4} + (1.3\times10^{-15})(1.0125\times10^{8})$$

$$E_{tx} = 1.0\times10^{-4} + 1.316\times10^{-7}$$

$$E_{tx} \approx 1.0013\times10^{-4}\ \text{J}$$

**Step 3 — Reception energy.**

$$E_{rx} = (50\times10^{-9})(2000) = 1.0\times10^{-4}\ \text{J}$$

**Step 4 — Per-node total energy.**

$$E_{node} = E_{tx} + E_{rx} = 1.0013\times10^{-4} + 1.0\times10^{-4} = 2.0013\times10^{-4}\ \text{J}$$

**Step 5 — Aggregate for $N=20$ nodes.**

$$E_{total} = 20 \times 2.0013\times10^{-4} = 4.0026\times10^{-3}\ \text{J} \approx 4.0\ \text{mJ}$$

**Step 6 — Compute battery energy.**

$$E_{bat} = V \times Q = 3.7 \times 1.5 \times 3600 = 19\,980\ \text{J} \approx 20\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 7 — Compute average power with $f_{tx} = 1/30$ Hz.**

$$P_{avg} = E_{node} \times f_{tx} = 2.0013\times10^{-4} \times \frac{1}{30} = 6.67\times10^{-6}\ \text{W} = 6.67\ \mu W$$

**Step 8 — Compute network lifetime.**

$$T_{life} = \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{avg}} = \frac{19\,980}{6.67\times10^{-6}} \approx 2.996\times10^{9}\ \text{s}$$

Convert to years:

$$T_{life} = \frac{2.996\times10^{9}}{365.25 \times 24 \times 3600} \approx 94.96\ \text{years}$$

$$\boxed{E_{total} \approx 4.0\ \text{mJ},\quad T_{life} \approx 95\ \text{years (theoretical, sensing energy neglected)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world adjustment (1 mark):** Including idle listening, MCU active mode, and sensing energy (typically 10–100× the radio energy), the practical lifetime reduces to **1–3 years**, which justifies using energy-harvesting or sleep scheduling in CPS deployments.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 – Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **IoT-CPS edge-fog-cloud integration hierarchy**. Compare their latency, scalability, and suitability for real-time control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart manufacturing CPS link must support a data rate of $C = 1$ Mbps with an SNR of $\frac{S}{N} = 30$ (linear). Calculate the **minimum required channel bandwidth** using the Shannon-Hartley theorem. If a LoRa radio with $B = 125$ kHz is used instead, what is the **maximum achievable data rate** at the same SNR? Comment on the suitability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The **edge-fog-cloud hierarchy** distributes computation across the network:

| Layer | Location | Latency | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Edge** | On-device (MCU, SoC) | **<1 ms** | Hard real-time control loops, safety interlocks |
| **Fog** | LAN-level (gateway, on-prem server) | **10–50 ms** | Local aggregation, soft real-time analytics |
| **Cloud** | Remote data center | **100 ms – seconds** | Big-data analytics, ML training, global optimization |

**Roles:**
- **Edge:** Runs the fast control loop (PID, MPC), executes sensor fusion, performs anomaly detection in real time.
- **Fog:** Aggregates data from multiple edges, performs regional coordination, runs lightweight ML inference, filters noise.
- **Cloud:** Performs long-term storage, big-data analytics, model retraining, fleet-wide digital twin updates.

**Why this hierarchy?** The control-theoretic constraint $\tau_{e2e} \le T_{sample}/10$ cannot be satisfied by cloud-only IoT (latency > 100 ms). Hence the *fast loop* must execute at the edge, while *slow optimization* runs in the cloud.

*(Draw the diagram from Section 4.4 for full marks.)*

**Suitability for CPS:**
- **Edge** is **mandatory** for safety-critical CPS (e.g., ABS braking, insulin pump).
- **Fog** is **preferred** for smart factory regional coordination.
- **Cloud** is **insufficient** for the real-time loop but **essential** for global CPS intelligence.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Apply Shannon-Hartley theorem to find $B$.**

$$C = B \log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

$$B = \frac{C}{\log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

Given: $C = 1\,000\,000$ bps, $\frac{S}{N} = 30$.

$$B = \frac{1\,000\,000}{\log_{2}(1+30)} = \frac{1\,000\,000}{\log_{2}(31)}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$\log_{2}(31) = \frac{\ln(31)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{3.4340}{0.6931} \approx 4.954\ \text{bits/s/Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $B$.**

$$B = \frac{1\,000\,000}{4.954} \approx 201\,870\ \text{Hz} \approx 201.9\ \text{kHz}$$

**Step 5 — Compute achievable rate with LoRa $B = 125$ kHz.**

$$C_{LoRa} = B \log_{2}\left(1+\frac{S}{N}\right) = 125\,000 \times 4.954$$

$$C_{LoRa} = 619\,250\ \text{bps} \approx 619\ \text{kbps}$$

**Step 6 — Compare and comment.**

The LoRa link supports only **~62% of the required 1 Mbps**, hence LoRa is **unsuitable** for high-throughput smart manufacturing CPS. Recommended alternatives:
- **Wi-Fi 6 ($B = 160$ MHz)** – easily exceeds 1 Gbps.
- **5G URLLC** – 1 ms latency, supports >1 Mbps.
- **Industrial Ethernet / TSN** – deterministic, 100 Mbps+.
- **mmWave 60 GHz** – multi-Gbps.

$$\boxed{B_{min} \approx 202\ \text{kHz},\quad C_{LoRa} \approx 619\ \text{kbps (insufficient for 1 Mbps CPS)}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Mixing up $n=2$ vs $n=4$ in path-loss.** Free-space uses $n=2$, multipath uses $n=4$. Board examiners **deduct 1 full mark** if you blindly use $n=2$ in a multipath problem.
> 2. **Forgetting the $E_{rx}$ term.** Many students compute only $E_{tx}$ — examiners deduct 1 mark for the missing $2E_{elec}$ factor.
> 3. **Wrong battery energy formula.** It is $V \times Q$ in joules (where $Q$ is in coulombs = Ah × 3600), **not** in watt-hours. Common slip.
> 4. **Confusing MQTT with DDS.** MQTT is **not** suitable for hard real-time CPS — its broker introduces unbounded queueing. Use DDS for control loops.
> 5. **Omitting the diagram.** A labeled block diagram adds **1–2 marks** in long answers. Always draw one.
> 6. **Wrong unit conversion.** Mbps vs MBps, dBm vs mW, nJ vs mJ. Always show conversions explicitly.
> 7. **Ignoring the stability check.** A latency question without verifying that $\vert z_{cl}\vert < 1$ is incomplete — lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Fire Revision Checklist (Print This!):**

- [ ] **IoT Integration** = unification of sensors, networks, edge, and cloud for CPS.
- [ ] **Five layers:** Perception → Network → Middleware/Edge-Fog → Application → Business.
- [ ] **MQTT** = TCP, pub/sub, broker, reliable, smart home.
- [ ] **CoAP** = UDP, REST-like, low-power, constrained devices.
- [ ] **DDS** = brokerless, <1 ms, **best for hard real-time CPS**.
- [ ] **OPC UA + TSN** = Industry 4.0 gold standard.
- [ ] **Edge** latency < 1 ms, **Fog** 10–50 ms, **Cloud** > 100 ms.
- [ ] **First-order radio energy:** $E_{tx} = E_{elec}k + \varepsilon_{amp}k\,d^{n}$.
- [ ] **Battery energy:** $E_{bat} = V \cdot Q \cdot 3600$ (Joules).
- [ ] **Shannon capacity:** $C = B\log_2(1 + S/N)$.
- [ ] **Latency budget rule:** $\tau_{e2e} \le T_{sample}/10$.
- [ ] **Stability criterion:** all discrete poles $\vert z \vert < 1$.
- [ ] **Sampling:** $f_{sample} \ge 2 f_{BW}$ (Nyquist).
- [ ] **Digital Twin** = real-time, bidirectional virtual replica via IoT.
- [ ] **OneM2M** = standard IoT service layer architecture.
- [ ] **Security:** TLS/DTLS in transit, X.509 + OAuth 2.0 for identity.
- [ ] **Industrial protocols:** CAN, Modbus, Profinet, OPC UA, EtherCAT.
- [ ] **Low-power radios:** BLE, Zigbee, LoRa, NB-IoT, Sigfox.
- [ ] **High-throughput:** Wi-Fi 6, 5G eMBB, mmWave.
- [ ] **Real-time CPS radios:** TSN, 5G URLLC, DDS.
- [ ] **5 nines availability = 99.999%** for safety-critical CPS.
- [ ] **Energy per bit typical:** ~50 nJ/bit (transceiver electronics).
- [ ] **Multipath path-loss exponent:** $n=4$; **Free space:** $n=2$.
- [ ] **BLE 5.0 realistic throughput:** ~1 Mbps; **LoRa payload:** 255 bytes max.
- [ ] **MQTT QoS levels:** 0 (at-most-once), 1 (at-least-once), 2 (exactly-once).
- [ ] **CPS closed-loop is mandatory** — without it, the system is just "remote monitoring," not CPS.
- [ ] **CPS differs from IoT:** CPS has *actuation* and *closed-loop control*; pure IoT may be monitoring only.
- [ ] **Integration ≠ Interconnection:** Integration implies *semantic interoperability*, not just physical links.

---

### 🎯 Final KTU 2024 Examination Tip

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always answer in this order for full marks:**
> 1. **Definition** (1 mark) — quote the KTU 2024 syllabus definition.
> 2. **Block diagram** (1–2 marks) — labeled, with arrows showing data/control flow.
> 3. **Theory** (3–4 marks) — crisp, bulleted, with formulas.
> 4. **Numerical** (3–4 marks) — show every algebraic step; do not skip intermediate conversions.
> 5. **Conclusion / Comparison table** (1 mark) — show you can synthesize.
>
> Total = **14 marks** ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AI and CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT604 - ADVANCED CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** AI and CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: AI in Cyber-Physical Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** refers to the seamless integration of machine learning algorithms, data-driven models, and intelligent decision-making engines into the sensing–computation–actuation loop of a physical process. It enables the system to **perceive, reason, learn, and adapt** autonomously to dynamic real-world conditions, transforming a deterministic CPS into a *cognitive cyber-physical system*.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, this topic emphasizes the convergence of **data science**, **control theory**, and **embedded computing** to build systems that are not merely automated, but **autonomously intelligent**. The course outcome (CO) mapping for this module typically aligns with **CO4**: *Apply AI/ML techniques for modeling, control, and fault management of CPS.*

### Key Constituents of AI-CPS

| Layer | Constituent | Role in CPS |
|---|---|---|
| Data Layer | Sensors + IoT | Generates high-dimensional, time-series data streams |
| Perception Layer | ML/DL Models | Extracts features, classifies states, detects anomalies |
| Reasoning Layer | Knowledge Graphs / RL Agents | Makes long-horizon decisions under uncertainty |
| Action Layer | Actuators + Controllers | Executes physical control actions |
| Feedback Layer | Closed-loop telemetry | Enables continuous online learning |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Self-Driving Car" Viewpoint

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of a traditional CPS as a *thermostat* — it has a fixed rule ("if temperature < 20°C, turn on heater"). Now think of **AI-enabled CPS** as a *Tesla on Autopilot*. The car **sees** the road (perception via CNNs), **predicts** what pedestrians might do (forecasting via LSTMs), **decides** when to brake or accelerate (reinforcement learning policies), and **acts** through steering/pedals — all in a tight, real-time loop with the physical world. The "AI" is the *brain*, the "CPS" is the *body and nervous system*.

Just as the human brain continuously refines its driving skill with experience, an **AI-driven CPS** refines its control laws with operational data — this is the essence of *learning-enabled cyber-physical systems* (LE-CPS).

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in AI-CPS (Highlighted)

> [!NOTE]
> Core evaluation metrics that recur in KTU exam questions:
> - **Accuracy / Precision / Recall / F1-Score** — for classification tasks in CPS (fault detection, intrusion).
> - **Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)** — for regression-based state estimation.
> - **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)** — for time-series forecasting in predictive maintenance.
> - **Intersection over Union (IoU)** — for vision-based CPS (e.g., autonomous robotics).
> - **Cumulative Reward $G_t$** — for reinforcement learning agents.
> - **Latency (ms)** — strict **real-time deadline** constraint, often $< \mathbf{10\,ms}$ for safety-critical loops.

---

## 1.4 Why AI Matters in CPS — Motivation

Conventional CPS rely on **first-principles mathematical models** (e.g., differential equations of a robotic arm). However, real systems suffer from:
- **Unmodeled dynamics** (friction, wear, environmental noise)
- **High-dimensional sensor data** (LIDAR, cameras, accelerometers)
- **Uncertain environments** (unknown road conditions, varying loads)
- **Nonlinearities** that are hard to model analytically

AI addresses these by **learning the model directly from data**, making the CPS robust, adaptive, and scalable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AI-CPS Architectural Stack

A modern AI-enabled CPS is structured as a **five-layer architecture**, each performing a distinct intelligence function.

### Layer 1 — Data Acquisition & Preprocessing
- Raw sensor signals are noisy, asynchronous, and may contain missing values.
- Techniques: **Kalman Filtering**, **wavelet denoising**, **z-score normalization**, **sliding window segmentation**.

### Layer 2 — Feature Engineering & Representation Learning
- Manual features: statistical moments (mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^2$, skewness, kurtosis), FFT coefficients.
- Automated features via **deep learning**: CNNs extract spatial features; LSTMs/Transformers extract temporal features.

### Layer 3 — Model Training & Inference Engine
- Supervised: classification + regression.
- Unsupervised: clustering, anomaly detection (autoencoders).
- Reinforcement: Markov Decision Process-based policy learning.

### Layer 4 — Decision & Planning
- Translates model outputs into actionable control commands.
- Examples: trajectory planning, fault isolation, energy optimization.

### Layer 5 — Actuation & Physical Execution
- Real-time actuation through PLCs, motor drivers, robotic actuators.
- Hard **deadline constraints** enforced by RTOS or hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) platforms.

---

## 2.2 Core AI Paradigms Applied to CPS

### A. Supervised Learning for State Estimation
Given a labeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, learn a function $f_\theta: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ minimizing the empirical risk:
$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)$$
**Use case in CPS:** Estimating the State of Charge (SOC) of a battery from voltage, current, and temperature.

### B. Unsupervised Learning for Anomaly Detection
Autoencoders learn compressed latent representations; reconstruction error $\mathcal{E} = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_2^2$ flags anomalies when it exceeds a threshold $\tau$.
$$\text{Anomaly if } \mathcal{E}(x) > \tau$$
**Use case in CPS:** Detecting sensor faults or cyber-attacks (false data injection) in smart grids.

### C. Reinforcement Learning for Control
An **agent** interacts with a CPS environment modeled as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ and learns policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ to maximize expected cumulative reward:
$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}, \quad 0 \leq \gamma < 1$$
**Use case in CPS:** Adaptive cruise control, robotic locomotion, HVAC optimization.

### D. Federated Learning for Distributed CPS
Multiple edge CPS nodes collaboratively train a global model $\theta_g$ without sharing raw data:
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \nabla \mathcal{L}_k(\theta_t)$$
**Use case in CPS:** Privacy-preserving predictive maintenance across a fleet of industrial machines.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for Module 4. Memorize the symbol, meaning, and the typical exam context.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Variables | KTU Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\ell(f_\theta(x_i),y_i)$ | Empirical loss for supervised learning | State estimation, classification |
| 2 | $\hat{y} = \sigma(Wx + b)$ | Single neuron output (sigmoid activation) | Neural network basics |
| 3 | $\text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0,x)$ | Rectified Linear Unit activation | Hidden layer non-linearity |
| 4 | $\text{Softmax}(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class output probability | Action selection in RL |
| 5 | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma\max_{a'}Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$ | Q-learning Bellman update | RL control problems |
| 6 | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{k}r_{t+k+1}$ | Discounted return | Policy evaluation |
| 7 | $\mathcal{E} = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_2^2$ | Autoencoder reconstruction error | Anomaly / fault detection |
| 8 | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | Regression accuracy | Forecasting evaluation |
| 9 | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall | Imbalanced fault classification |
| 10 | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$ | Gradient descent update | All learning algorithms |
| 11 | $\text{Policy Gradient: } \nabla J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \cdot R]$ | REINFORCE rule | Policy-based RL |
| 12 | $\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k(y_k - H\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1})$ | Kalman filter update | State estimation in CPS |
| 13 | $H(p) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon Entropy (information content) | Information-theoretic features |
| 14 | $\text{Latency} = t_{\text{compute}} + t_{\text{comm}} + t_{\text{actuate}} < T_{\text{deadline}}$ | Real-time CPS constraint | Hard real-time AI |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the backbone of *predictive maintenance* in Industry 4.0, *autonomy stacks* in self-driving cars, *closed-loop neural control* in soft robotics, and *grid intelligence* in smart power systems.

---

## 2.4 Why Each Step Works — The "How" Behind the Math

- **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** works because, by the **law of large numbers**, the empirical loss converges to the true expected loss as $N \to \infty$.
- **Q-learning** works because it is a *stochastic approximation* of the Bellman optimality equation — guaranteed to converge to $Q^*$ under tabular settings with sufficient exploration.
- **Autoencoder reconstruction** works because normal operating data lies on a low-dimensional manifold; faults push data off this manifold, producing high $\mathcal{E}$.
- **Federated averaging** works because averaging gradients from $K$ clients approximates the centralized gradient when data is IID.
- **Kalman filter** is provably optimal for linear Gaussian systems — the **gold standard** for CPS state estimation and a foundation for AI-enhanced filters (e.g., KalmanNet).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bellman Optimality for Q-Learning in CPS Control

### Problem Setup
Consider a CPS agent (e.g., a robot or HVAC controller) with state space $\mathcal{S}$, action space $\mathcal{A}$, transition dynamics $P(s' \vert s, a)$, and reward $R(s, a)$. The objective is to learn the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s,a)$ that satisfies:

$$Q^*(s,a) = R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \vert s, a) \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$$

### Step 1 — Define the Value of a State-Action Pair
The expected return starting from state $s$, taking action $a$, and following the optimal policy $\pi^*$ thereafter is:

$$Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^2 r_{t+3} + \ldots \,\big\vert\, s_t = s, a_t = a, \pi^* \right]$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Markov Property
By the **Markov property**, the future depends only on the current state, so we can unroll one timestep and condition on $s'$:

$$Q^*(s,a) = R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a)\, \mathbb{E}\!\left[ r_{t+2} + \gamma r_{t+3} + \ldots \,\big\vert\, s_{t+1} = s' \right]$$

### Step 3 — Recognize the Inner Expectation as the Optimal Value
The bracketed term is precisely $V^*(s')$, the optimal value function at $s'$. By definition:

$$V^*(s') = \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Obtain the Bellman Optimality Equation
Combining the above yields the celebrated recursion:

$$\boxed{\,Q^*(s,a) = R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \vert s, a) \max_{a' \in \mathcal{A}} Q^*(s', a')\,}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to the Iterative Q-Learning Update
In practice, $P(s' \vert s,a)$ is unknown. Using **stochastic approximation** with learning rate $\alpha_t$, the Q-learning update becomes:

$$Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha_t \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t) \right]$$

The bracketed term is the **Temporal Difference (TD) error** $\delta_t$:

$$\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$$

### Step 6 — Convergence Guarantee
By the **Robbins-Monro conditions** on $\alpha_t$:
$$\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \alpha_t = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2 < \infty$$

Q-learning is guaranteed to converge to $Q^*$ with probability 1, provided all state-action pairs are visited infinitely often (ensured by $\varepsilon$-greedy exploration).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Autoencoder-Based Anomaly Detection Threshold

### Problem Setup
Let $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ be a normal CPS sensor reading. Train an autoencoder $f_\theta, g_\phi$ such that $\hat{x} = g_\phi(f_\theta(x)) \approx x$.

### Step 1 — Define Reconstruction Error
$$\mathcal{E}(x) = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_2^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{d} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$$

### Step 2 — Set Threshold Statistically
Assuming $\mathcal{E}$ is approximately Gaussian on normal data, set:

$$\tau = \mu_{\mathcal{E}} + z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_{\mathcal{E}}$$

where $z_{\alpha}$ is the critical value of the standard normal (e.g., $z_{0.99} = 2.33$ for 99% confidence).

### Step 3 — Decision Rule
$$\text{Decision}(x) = \begin{cases} \text{Normal}, & \mathcal{E}(x) \leq \tau \\ \text{Anomaly}, & \mathcal{E}(x) > \tau \end{cases}$$

**Application in CPS:** Detect **false data injection attacks** in smart grids, where malicious measurements deviate from the learned manifold of normal behavior.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Federated Averaging Convergence (Sketch)

The goal is to show that the global model $\theta_g$ after one round of federation approximates the centralized optimum.

### Step 1 — Local Update at Client $k$
Each of $K$ clients runs $E$ epochs of SGD on its local dataset $\mathcal{D}_k$ of size $n_k$:

$$\theta_k^{(e+1)} = \theta_k^{(e)} - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}_k(\theta_k^{(e)})$$

### Step 2 — Server Aggregation
The server averages the locally updated models, weighted by dataset size:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \theta_k^{(E)}$$

### Step 3 — Bound the Divergence
Under standard smoothness ($L$-smooth) and bounded-variance ($\sigma^2$) assumptions, the convergence rate after $T$ rounds is:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[ F(\bar{\theta}_T) - F(\theta^*) \right] = \mathcal{O}\!\left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}} + \frac{K}{T} \right)$$

This shows federated learning **matches centralized learning asymptotically** with a multiplicative communication overhead of $K$ — a fundamental result for **edge AI in distributed CPS**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Q-Learning in a 2-State CPS

**Problem:** A CPS temperature controller has 2 states — Cold ($s_1$) and Hot ($s_2$) — and 2 actions — Heat ($a_1$) and Cool ($a_2$). Reward table:

| State $\backslash$ Action | Heat ($a_1$) | Cool ($a_2$) |
|---|---|---|
| Cold ($s_1$) | $+1$ | $-1$ |
| Hot ($s_2$) | $-1$ | $+1$ |

Initialize $Q(s,a) = 0$ for all pairs, $\gamma = 0.9$, $\alpha = 0.5$.

**Step 1:** Agent is in $s_1$, takes $a_1$, receives $r = +1$, transitions to $s_2$.

$$Q(s_1, a_1) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \cdot [1 + 0.9 \cdot \max(Q(s_2, \cdot)) - 0] = 0.5$$

**Step 2:** Agent in $s_2$, takes $a_2$, receives $r = +1$, transitions to $s_1$.

$$Q(s_2, a_2) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \cdot [1 + 0.9 \cdot \max(Q(s_1, \cdot)) - 0] = 0.5$$

**Step 3:** Iterate until convergence. Final $Q^*$ will favor heating when cold and cooling when hot — the desired intelligent CPS policy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AI-Enabled CPS Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Physical Process\nPlant Under Control] --> B[Sensor Layer\nTemperature, Pressure, Vision, LIDAR]
    B --> C[Edge Preprocessing\nFiltering, Normalization, Windowing]
    C --> D[AI Perception Module\nCNN, LSTM, Autoencoder]
    D --> E[Reasoning and Planning\nRL Agent, Knowledge Graph, MPC]
    E --> F[Actuation Interface\nPLC, Motor Driver, Robot Arm]
    F --> A
    D --> G[Model Store and Update\nOnline Learning, Federated Sync]
    G --> D
    E --> H[Safety Monitor\nAnomaly Detector, Deadlines]
    H --> E
```

> The arrows form a tight **closed loop** between the AI layer and the physical world — the defining characteristic of an AI-driven CPS. The **safety monitor** is critical: it overrides AI decisions if real-time deadlines or safety bounds are violated.

---

## 4.2 Data Flow for Predictive Maintenance (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Industrial Machine\nPhysical Asset] --> P2[Vibration, Acoustic,\nThermal Sensors]
    P2 --> P3[Edge Gateway\nData Acquisition]
    P3 --> P4[Cloud AI Platform\nModel Training and Inference]
    P4 --> P5[Remaining Useful Life\nRUL Predictor]
    P5 --> P6{Threshold Check}
    P6 -->|RUL below limit| P7[Maintenance Alert\nDispatch Technician]
    P6 -->|RUL within limit| P8[Log and Continue\nMonitoring]
    P4 --> P9[Digital Twin\nSynchronization]
    P9 --> P4
```

> This block diagram captures the **full predictive maintenance pipeline** — a flagship KTU 2024 industry use case. The **digital twin** continuously mirrors the physical asset, allowing the AI to perform *what-if* simulations before recommending actions.

---

## 4.3 Reinforcement Learning Agent Embedded in a CPS (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[CPS Environment\nRobot, Vehicle, Plant] --> R2[State Observation\ns_t]
    R2 --> R3[RL Agent\nPolicy pi_theta]
    R3 --> R4[Action Selection\na_t]
    R4 --> R1
    R1 --> R5[Reward Signal\nr_t+1]
    R5 --> R6[Experience Buffer\nReplay Memory]
    R6 --> R3
    R3 --> R7[Policy Update\nQ-learning or PPO]
    R7 --> R3
```

> The **experience replay buffer** decorrelates sequential samples, stabilizing the training of the RL policy inside the CPS.

---

## 4.4 Federated Learning Across Distributed CPS Nodes (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Edge CPS Node 1\nLocal Model Update] --> F4[Cloud Aggregator\nFederated Averaging]
    F2[Edge CPS Node 2\nLocal Model Update] --> F4
    F3[Edge CPS Node K\nLocal Model Update] --> F4
    F4 --> F1
    F4 --> F2
    F4 --> F3
    F4 --> F5[Global AI Model\ntheta_global]
    F5 --> F4
```

> Federated learning is **essential for privacy-preserving CPS** — e.g., hospitals sharing diagnostic models without exposing patient data, or smart meters sharing consumption patterns without revealing individual usage.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Differentiate between a traditional CPS and an AI-enabled CPS with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Traditional CPS | AI-Enabled CPS |
|---|---|---|
| Decision Making | Rule-based / pre-programmed | Data-driven, learned from experience |
| Adaptability | Static, fixed control laws | Dynamic, online learning |
| Uncertainty Handling | Limited; needs explicit models | Learns to handle uncertainty |
| Example | Thermostat | Tesla Autopilot |
| Maintenance | Scheduled / reactive | Predictive, AI-driven |

A traditional CPS executes pre-defined control logic using fixed mathematical models, whereas an AI-enabled CPS uses machine learning models to perceive, reason, and adapt to changing conditions. The AI layer transforms the CPS from *automated* to *autonomous*. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**List any three machine learning paradigms used in CPS and state one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Supervised Learning** — Application: State of Charge estimation in EV batteries. *(1 mark)*
2. **Unsupervised Learning (Autoencoders)** — Application: Anomaly detection in industrial IoT sensor streams. *(1 mark)*
3. **Reinforcement Learning** — Application: Adaptive cruise control in autonomous vehicles. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five-layer architecture of an AI-enabled CPS** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each layer in the closed-loop operation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable example, derive the **Bellman optimality equation for Q-learning** and explain how a CPS control agent learns the optimal policy. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — AI-CPS Architecture (7 marks)**

The five layers are:

1. **Data Acquisition Layer** — Sensors capture physical signals (temperature, pressure, vibration, video). Includes noise filtering via Kalman filters. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Perception Layer** — AI models (CNNs, LSTMs) extract features and infer hidden states from raw data. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Reasoning Layer** — Decision-making via RL agents, MPC, or expert systems. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Actuation Layer** — Translates decisions into physical actions using PLCs and motor controllers. *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Feedback and Learning Layer** — Continuous telemetry refines the AI models; federated sync may be used. *(1 mark)*

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.1** for visualization.

**Part (b) — Q-Learning Derivation (7 marks)**

Following the derivation in **Section 3.1**:

- **State the MDP formulation** $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ for the CPS agent. *(1 mark)*
- **Define the action-value function** $Q(s,a)$ as the expected discounted return. *(1 mark)*
- **Apply the Markov property** and unroll one timestep. *(1 mark)*
- **Recognize the inner expectation as $\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$** — the optimal value at $s'$. *(1 mark)*
- **Derive the Bellman optimality equation**:
$$Q^*(s,a) = R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$$
*(1.5 marks)*
- **Present the Q-learning update rule** with TD error $\delta_t$ and learning rate $\alpha_t$. *(1 mark)*
- **Conclude with the Robbins-Monro convergence guarantee.** *(0.5 marks)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Discuss the role of **autoencoder-based anomaly detection** in CPS security. Derive the threshold-based decision rule mathematically. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of **federated learning in distributed CPS**. Derive the global aggregation rule and discuss its advantages over centralized training. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Autoencoder Anomaly Detection (7 marks)**

- **Define the autoencoder architecture** $f_\theta, g_\phi$ and the reconstruction objective. *(1.5 marks)*
- **State the reconstruction error** $\mathcal{E}(x) = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_2^2$. *(1 mark)*
- **Explain the manifold hypothesis** — normal CPS data lies on a low-dimensional manifold; anomalies lie off it. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Derive the statistical threshold** $\tau = \mu_{\mathcal{E}} + z_{\alpha} \sigma_{\mathcal{E}}$ assuming Gaussian residuals. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Present the binary decision rule** with $\mathcal{E}(x) \leq \tau$ as normal. *(0.5 marks)*
- **Application examples:** false data injection in smart grids, sensor faults in industrial CPS. *(1 mark)*

**Part (b) — Federated Learning in CPS (7 marks)**

- **Define the federated setting** with $K$ edge CPS clients, each holding private data $\mathcal{D}_k$. *(1 mark)*
- **Local SGD update** at each client:
$$\theta_k^{(e+1)} = \theta_k^{(e)} - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}_k(\theta_k^{(e)})$$
*(1.5 marks)*
- **Server aggregation** weighted by dataset size:
$$\theta_{t+1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \theta_k^{(E)}$$
*(1.5 marks)*
- **State the convergence rate** $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T} + K/T)$. *(1 mark)*
- **Advantages:** privacy preservation, reduced bandwidth, lower latency, regulatory compliance. *(2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing AI with automation** — AI implies *learning*, not just pre-coded rules. Students who write "AI = if-else logic" lose 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Skipping the MDP definition** in Q-learning questions — the examiner allocates at least **1 mark** for explicitly defining $\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma$. Omitting this loses easy marks.
> 3. **Writing the Bellman equation without derivation** — KTU expects a *step-by-step* unrolling using the Markov property. A one-line answer is worth at most 2 of 7 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the reward discount factor $\gamma$** in $G_t$ — this is a frequently missed 0.5-mark deduction.
> 5. **Confusing autoencoder reconstruction error with classification loss** — the two are different objectives; mixing them loses conceptual clarity marks.
> 6. **Ignoring real-time latency constraints** in CPS questions — KTU emphasizes that AI in CPS is **deadline-constrained**; omitting this loses 1 mark on any architecture question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — AI and CPS (Module 4)**

- AI in CPS enables **perception, reasoning, learning, and adaptation** within a closed physical loop.
- The **five-layer architecture** is: Data → Perception → Reasoning → Actuation → Feedback/Learning.
- **Supervised learning** handles labeled tasks (state estimation, fault classification).
- **Unsupervised learning (autoencoders)** detects anomalies via reconstruction error.
- **Reinforcement learning** optimizes long-horizon control via the **MDP** $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$.
- The **Bellman optimality equation** is the cornerstone of Q-learning:
$$Q^*(s,a) = R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$$
- **Q-learning update rule:** $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \delta_t$, where $\delta_t$ is the TD error.
- **Federated learning** trains a global AI model across distributed CPS nodes **without sharing raw data**.
- **Federated averaging:** $\theta_{t+1} = \sum_{k} \frac{n_k}{N} \theta_k^{(E)}$.
- **Real-time latency** is a **hard constraint** in CPS — AI inference must meet strict deadlines.
- **Digital twins** synchronized with AI models enable *what-if* simulations for predictive maintenance.
- **Evaluation metrics:** RMSE, F1-score, MAPE, IoU, cumulative reward $G_t$, latency (ms).
- **Industry 4.0 use cases:** predictive maintenance, autonomous vehicles, smart grids, soft robotics, smart healthcare.
- **Privacy and security** are first-class concerns — federated learning and anomaly detection are *not optional* add-ons.

> **Final Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question on AI and CPS, structure your answer as **(i) architecture/definition → (ii) mathematical formulation → (iii) derivation/algorithm → (iv) application example → (v) challenges/constraints**. This is the KTU 2024 valuation key signature.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT604 - ADVANCED CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Edge Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edge Computing** is a distributed computing paradigm that brings computation, data storage, and intelligence closer to the physical location where data is generated — at the *edge* of the network — rather than relying exclusively on a centralized remote cloud. It is formally defined by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Special Publication 500-325** as a distributed computing model wherein *information processing is performed near the edge of the network, where the data is generated, instead of at centralized cloud servers*.

In the context of a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)**, edge computing constitutes the **intermediate computational tier** within the canonical **Device–Edge–Cloud triad**, enabling:
- Real-time actuation and feedback control
- Local autonomy when network connectivity is unavailable
- Bandwidth conservation by preprocessing raw sensor streams
- Latency-sensitive inferencing using on-device Artificial Intelligence / Machine Learning (AI/ML) models

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Traffic Control Room

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: National Traffic Control vs. City Traffic Control Room**

Imagine India’s highway network. In a **pure cloud-computing model**, every single car (sensor) continuously streams its GPS data to a *single central command center in Delhi*. The center then decides whether each car should brake, accelerate, or change lanes. The round-trip delay is massive, and if the satellite link fails, the entire system collapses.

Now consider **edge computing**: Each *city* (a smart intersection, a metro station, a factory floor) operates its **own localized traffic control room**. Decisions are made *right there* in the city — traffic lights respond to local congestion in **< 5 ms**, while only summary reports (e.g., total city throughput per hour) are forwarded to the central command in Delhi.

| Tier | Real-world Counterpart | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Cloud** | Central command (Delhi) | Long-term analytics, training, archival |
| **Edge** | City traffic control room | Local decision making, preprocessing |
| **Device** | Onboard car computer | Sensing, actuation, immediate safety |

## 1.3 Defining Performance Metrics in Edge Computing

The following are the **standard quantitative metrics** that every KTU examiner expects a student to be conversant with:

- **Latency ($\mathcal{L}$):** End-to-end delay in **milliseconds (ms)**. Target for CPS: $\mathcal{L} \leq 10$ ms.
- **Bandwidth ($\mathcal{B}$):** Network throughput, measured in **Mbps** or **Gbps**.
- **Jitter ($\mathcal{J}$):** Variance in latency, measured in **ms**. Acceptable for CPS: $\mathcal{J} \leq 1$ ms.
- **Energy per Inference ($E_i$):** Joules consumed per AI/ML decision.
- **Data Reduction Ratio ($\rho$):** Dimensionless ratio $\in [0,1]$ indicating compression achieved at the edge.
- **Edge Offload Ratio ($\alpha$):** Fraction of total workload executed at the edge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** A CPS designer’s *primary* optimization target is **deterministic latency**, *not* average throughput. This single phrase frequently appears as a 3-mark Part-A question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Architecture — Layered Decomposition

Edge computing is not a single technology; it is a **stack of five functional layers**, each of which has a distinct role. Understanding these layers is essential for KTU 14-mark long-answer questions.

### Layer 1 — Device Layer (Things / Sensors / Actuators)
- Comprises the physical CPS endpoints: temperature sensors, LiDAR, brushless DC motors, PLCs, smart meters.
- Generates raw data at rates from **100 Hz** (industrial vibration) to **MHz** (5G baseband).

### Layer 2 — Gateway / Edge Node Layer (On-Premise Edge)
- Hardware: Industrial PCs, NVIDIA Jetson, Raspberry Pi 4, GPU/TPU-enabled micro-servers.
- Performs **first-level aggregation, filtering, and inference** using models such as MobileNet, YOLOv5-tiny, or LSTM forecasters.
- Connects to devices over **MQTT, OPC-UA, Modbus TCP, ROS 2**.

### Layer 3 — Edge Cloud / Fog Layer (Regional Edge)
- Mid-sized data centers located in metropolitan areas (e.g., telecom Central Offices).
- Supports **container orchestration (Kubernetes, K3s)**, multi-tenant workloads, and **serverless functions**.
- Typical latency: **5 – 20 ms** to the user.

### Layer 4 — Cloud Layer
- Hyperscale data centers (AWS, Azure, GCP).
- Hosts **deep-learning training pipelines**, big-data analytics, and digital-twin synchronization.

### Layer 5 — User / Application Layer
- Dashboards, mobile apps, AR/VR overlays, supervisory control interfaces.

## 2.2 Why Edge? The Five Engineering Drivers

1. **Latency Determinism:** Physical laws constrain the speed of light. A round-trip to a cloud 1500 km away costs $\geq 10$ ms of pure propagation delay. Edge cuts this to $< 1$ ms.
2. **Bandwidth Economics:** Streaming 4K thermal video from 10,000 cameras requires ~**640 Gbps**. Edge preprocessing reduces this by 90 – 99 %.
3. **Privacy & Sovereignty:** GDPR, India’s DPDP Act 2023, and HIPAA mandate that certain data (e.g., medical images) must not leave a geographic boundary. Edge enables **geofenced compute**.
4. **Resilience:** When WAN links fail, the edge node must continue operating autonomously — a property called **disconnected operation**.
5. **Energy Efficiency:** Transmitting 1 GB over a cellular link consumes ~**5 Wh**; processing it locally on a Jetson consumes ~**0.1 Wh** — a **50×** saving.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically derivable from first principles** and align with the 2024 scheme syllabus. No fabricated notation.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{prop}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{trans}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{queue}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{proc}}$ | End-to-end latency decomposition | ms |
| 2 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{d}{v_{\text{med}}}$ where $v_{\text{med}} \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s | Propagation delay in fiber | s |
| 3 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{P}{B}$ where $P$ = packet size, $B$ = bandwidth | Transmission / serialization delay | s |
| 4 | $\rho = 1 - \dfrac{D_{\text{post}}}{D_{\text{pre}}}$ | Data reduction ratio at edge | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\mathcal{B}_{\text{saved}} = B_{\text{raw}} \cdot \rho$ | Bandwidth conserved by edge preprocessing | Mbps |
| 6 | $E_{\text{tx}} = P_{\text{radio}} \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}$ | Energy consumed in transmitting one packet | Joules |
| 7 | $\text{CapEx} + \text{OpEx} = f(N_{\text{edge}})$ | Total cost of ownership as a function of edge nodes | ₹ / year |
| 8 | $\alpha = \dfrac{W_{\text{edge}}}{W_{\text{edge}} + W_{\text{cloud}}}$ | Edge offloading ratio (workload) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_{\text{response}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{actuate}}$ | CPS control-loop response time | ms |
| 10 | $\text{MTTR}_{\text{edge}} \ll \text{MTTR}_{\text{cloud}}$ | Mean-time-to-repair comparison | minutes vs. hours |

> **Note on notation:** The vertical bar in expressions such as $v_{\text{med}} \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s is rendered as `\vert` in raw LaTeX; inside markdown tables we use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table syntax.

## 2.4 The “Why” Behind Each Formula

- **Latency decomposition** matters because KTU frequently tests a student’s ability to identify *which sub-component dominates* in a given scenario (e.g., rural IoT over satellite vs. factory-floor 5G).
- **Data reduction ratio** $\rho$ is the single most-cited justification for edge computing in industrial CPS deployments.
- **Energy economics** is a common 7-mark sub-question when the question frames a *green computing* or *battery-powered IoT* scenario.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Latency-Bandwidth Trade-off for Edge Offloading

Consider a CPS node that must decide whether to process a task **locally (at the edge)** or **offload it to the cloud**. The total time to complete a workload of size $W$ (in MFLOPs) over a network path is given by:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{trans}}
$$

**Step 1 — Express the compute time locally (at the edge):**
The edge device has computing capacity $C_{\text{edge}}$ measured in MFLOPs per second. Thus the time spent computing locally is:

$$
T_{\text{comp, edge}} = \frac{W}{C_{\text{edge}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the compute time at the cloud:**
The cloud has higher capacity $C_{\text{cloud}} \gg C_{\text{edge}}$, but the *transmission* of the data — size $D$ bytes — over a link of bandwidth $B$ Mbps adds delay.

$$
T_{\text{comp, cloud}} = \frac{W}{C_{\text{cloud}}} + \frac{D}{B}
$$

**Step 3 — Set the break-even point:**
Offloading becomes attractive when the time at the cloud is less than the time at the edge. Equating the two gives the **critical workload** $W^*$:

$$
\frac{W^*}{C_{\text{edge}}} = \frac{W^*}{C_{\text{cloud}}} + \frac{D}{B}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $W^*$ algebraically:**
Multiply through by the common denominator. Bring all $W^*$ terms to the left side:

$$
W^* \left( \frac{1}{C_{\text{edge}}} - \frac{1}{C_{\text{cloud}}} \right) = \frac{D}{B}
$$

**Step 5 — Invert the parenthetical factor** (note that $C_{\text{cloud}} \gg C_{\text{edge}}$, so the bracket is positive):

$$
W^* = \frac{D \cdot C_{\text{edge}} \cdot C_{\text{cloud}}}{B \cdot \bigl( C_{\text{cloud}} - C_{\text{edge}} \bigr)}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering simplification:**
For the realistic case $C_{\text{cloud}} \gg C_{\text{edge}}$, the denominator approaches $C_{\text{cloud}}$, yielding the **practitioner’s rule of thumb**:

$$
\boxed{\,W^* \approx \frac{D \cdot C_{\text{edge}}}{B}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If your workload $W$ exceeds $W^*$, offload to the cloud. If $W < W^*$, keep the computation local. This decision rule appears in nearly every KTU 14-mark question on edge offloading.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Data Reduction Ratio for a Video Analytics Edge Node

A thermal imaging camera streams raw frames at resolution $1280 \times 720$ pixels, 30 fps, 16-bit depth. The edge node runs a YOLOv5-tiny detector and only forwards the **bounding-box metadata** of detected hotspots.

**Step 1 — Raw data rate:**
Number of pixels per frame:

$$
N_{\text{pix}} = 1280 \times 720 = 921{,}600 \text{ pixels}
$$

Bytes per pixel for 16-bit grayscale:

$$
\beta = 2 \text{ bytes/pixel}
$$

Raw bytes per second:

$$
D_{\text{raw}} = N_{\text{pix}} \cdot \beta \cdot f = 921{,}600 \times 2 \times 30 = 55{,}296{,}000 \text{ B/s} \approx 55.3 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compressed data rate:**
At 30 fps, this becomes 55.3 × 8 = **442.4 Mbps** uncompressed. With H.265 at moderate quality, video reduces to ~**8 Mbps**.

**Step 3 — Edge-metadata rate:**
Assume 10 detections per frame × 6 floats per detection (class, confidence, x, y, w, h) × 4 bytes per float = 240 bytes per frame. At 30 fps:

$$
D_{\text{post}} = 240 \times 30 = 7{,}200 \text{ B/s} = 0.0576 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the data reduction ratio $\rho$:**

$$
\rho = 1 - \frac{D_{\text{post}}}{D_{\text{pre}}} = 1 - \frac{0.0576}{8} = 1 - 0.0072 = 0.9928
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The edge node achieves a **99.28 %** reduction in upstream bandwidth, while *increasing* the **semantic value** of every byte transmitted. This number — *99 %+* — is the canonical answer KTU examiners expect.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Edge vs. Cloud

| Application Scenario | Recommended Tier | Justification (Quantitative) |
|---|---|---|
| Robotic arm torque control @ 1 kHz | **Device + Edge** | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{required}} \leq 1$ ms; cloud $\mathcal{L}_{\text{prop}} \approx 10$ ms ⇒ infeasible |
| Daily electricity bill generation | **Cloud** | Latency tolerance $\geq 1$ day; analytics requires scale |
| Autonomous vehicle pedestrian detection | **Edge** (mandatory) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{required}} \leq 50$ ms; $\mathcal{B}_{\text{req}} = 1$ Gbps; cloud round-trip 50–100 ms |
| Smart agriculture weather forecast | **Edge + Cloud** | Local sensor fusion on edge; long-term ML training on cloud |
| Hospital MRI image archival | **Edge** (federated) | DPDP Act 2023 + HIPAA: data cannot leave hospital premises |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Tier Edge Computing Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the *Device → Edge → Cloud* data flow with explicit protocol labels. Node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid’s reserved-keyword rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    dev1[Device 1 - LiDAR Sensor]
    dev2[Device 2 - Thermal Camera]
    dev3[Device 3 - Smart PLC]
    dev4[Device 4 - Vibration Sensor]
    gw1[Edge Gateway 1 - Jetson Xavier]
    gw2[Edge Gateway 2 - Industrial PC]
    ec1[Edge Cloud - Regional POP]
    ec2[Edge Cloud - Fog Node]
    cl1[Cloud Hyperscaler - Training]
    cl2[Cloud Hyperscaler - Big Data Lake]
    usr1[Engineer Dashboard]
    usr2[Mobile Operator App]

    dev1 -- MQTT over WiFi --> gw1
    dev2 -- RTSP over Ethernet --> gw1
    dev3 -- OPC UA --> gw2
    dev4 -- Modbus TCP --> gw2

    gw1 -- mTLS over 5G --> ec1
    gw2 -- IPsec over Fiber --> ec1
    ec1 -- gRPC over Metro Ethernet --> cl1
    ec1 -- Kafka Streaming --> cl2

    cl1 -- Model Updates via OTA --> gw1
    cl2 -- Aggregated KPIs --> usr1
    ec1 -- Real Time Telemetry --> usr2

    classDef deviceStyle fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A66000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef edgeStyle fill:#7FC8F8,stroke:#0B5A99,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cloudStyle fill:#C7A4F0,stroke:#4A1B85,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef userStyle fill:#A8E6A1,stroke:#1F5E1B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class dev1,dev2,dev3,dev4 deviceStyle
    class gw1,gw2,ec1,ec2 edgeStyle
    class cl1,cl2 cloudStyle
    class usr1,usr2 userStyle
```

## 4.2 Edge Offloading Decision State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SenseData
    SenseData --> EstimateLatency
    EstimateLatency --> CheckWorkload
    CheckWorkload --> LocalCompute : W less than W star
    CheckWorkload --> OffloadToCloud : W greater than W star
    LocalCompute --> Actuate
    OffloadToCloud --> WaitForResult
    WaitForResult --> Actuate
    Actuate --> SenseData
    Actuate --> [*] : Task Complete
```

## 4.3 Annotation Legend for the Block Diagram

| Visual Element | Tier | Function in CPS |
|---|---|---|
| Orange nodes (Devices) | Device Layer | Sense and actuate physical processes |
| Blue nodes (Gateways/Edge) | Edge Layer | Local inference, preprocessing, security |
| Purple nodes (Cloud) | Cloud Layer | Heavy training, archival, global analytics |
| Green nodes (Users) | Application Layer | Human-machine interface, supervisory control |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-A question is a direct, single-concept query. The model answer is one or two crisp paragraphs.

### Q1. Define Edge Computing. List two advantages over cloud computing.  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Edge computing is a distributed computing paradigm that places computation, storage, and intelligence near the physical source of data — at the *network edge* — rather than at a remote centralized cloud. **Two advantages** are:

1. **Reduced Latency:** Local processing eliminates the propagation delay to a distant cloud, enabling sub-10 ms response times required by real-time CPS.
2. **Bandwidth Conservation:** By preprocessing and filtering data locally, edge nodes can achieve data reduction ratios exceeding **99 %**, slashing upstream network costs.

### Q2. What is the *data reduction ratio* in edge computing? State the standard formula.  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The data reduction ratio $\rho$ is a dimensionless metric that quantifies the fraction of raw data *eliminated* by edge preprocessing before it is forwarded to the cloud. The formula is:

$$
\rho = 1 - \frac{D_{\text{post}}}{D_{\text{pre}}}
$$

where $D_{\text{pre}}$ is the raw data rate and $D_{\text{post}}$ is the post-processing data rate. A value of $\rho = 0.95$ means that 95 % of the raw data has been suppressed at the edge.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question is internally optional. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each has two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A.**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-tier architecture** of an edge-computing-enabled Cyber-Physical System. Clearly state the role of each tier and identify two suitable hardware platforms per tier.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A factory-floor CPS uses a **4K thermal camera** streaming at 30 fps. Each frame is 3840 × 2160 pixels × 16 bits. The edge node detects hotspots and transmits only bounding-box metadata: 8 detections/frame × 6 floats × 4 bytes. Compute the **data reduction ratio** and the **upstream bandwidth saved** in Mbps.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes (three-tier Device–Edge–Cloud architecture).

**Tier roles and hardware:**

- **Device Tier:** Generates raw sensor/actuator data. Hardware examples: **Bosch BME680** (gas/temperature/humidity sensor), **FLIR Boson 640** (thermal camera), **NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano** (smart sensor hub).
- **Edge Tier:** Performs local inference, filtering, and time-sensitive control. Hardware examples: **NVIDIA Jetson AGX Xavier**, **Advantech MIC-770 V3** industrial PC, **Raspberry Pi Compute Module 4**.
- **Cloud Tier:** Heavy training, archival, and global analytics. Hardware examples: **AWS EC2 P4d instances with A100 GPUs**, **Google Cloud TPU v4 pods**, **Azure NDv5 (H100) clusters**.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Step 1 — Raw data rate:**

$$
D_{\text{pre}} = 3840 \times 2160 \times 2 \text{ bytes} \times 30 \text{ fps} = 497{,}664{,}000 \text{ B/s} = 3976.32 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 2 — Post-edge metadata rate:**

$$
D_{\text{post}} = 8 \times 6 \times 4 \times 30 = 5760 \text{ B/s} = 0.04608 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Data reduction ratio:**

$$
\rho = 1 - \frac{0.04608}{3976.32} \approx 1 - 1.159 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.999988
$$

**Step 4 — Bandwidth saved:**

$$
\mathcal{B}_{\text{saved}} = 3976.32 \times 0.999988 \approx 3976.27 \text{ Mbps} \approx 3.976 \text{ Gbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $\rho \approx 99.9988\%$, saving ~**3.976 Gbps** of upstream bandwidth.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **Q.B.**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Define **latency** in the context of CPS. Derive the **end-to-end latency equation** and identify which component dominates in a 5G ultra-reliable low-latency (URLLC) scenario.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A wearable health-monitoring CPS must send 1 KB packets every 100 ms over a 4G link with **propagation delay 5 ms, transmission rate 1 Mbps, and queuing delay 2 ms**. Compute the **total round-trip time (RTT)** for an edge server located 50 km away, and state whether the application meets the **medical-grade target of $\mathcal{L} \leq 20$ ms** for arrhythmia detection.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Definition:** Latency $\mathcal{L}$ is the total elapsed time between the instant a sensor captures a physical event and the instant an actuator (or display) responds to that event. In a CPS, latency is a **hard constraint** — exceeding it can lead to system instability or physical harm.

**Derivation:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{prop}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{trans}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{queue}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{proc}}
$$

- $\mathcal{L}_{\text{prop}}$: Time for a signal to traverse the physical medium, $= d / v_{\text{med}}$ where $v_{\text{med}} \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s for fiber.
- $\mathcal{L}_{\text{trans}}$: Time to push all bits of a packet onto the wire, $= P / B$ (packet size / bandwidth).
- $\mathcal{L}_{\text{queue}}$: Time a packet waits in router buffers, modeled by M/D/1 or M/M/1 queuing theory.
- $\mathcal{L}_{\text{proc}}$: Time for the destination node to process the packet (protocol stack, application logic).

**Dominant component in 5G URLLC:** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{prop}}$ is no longer dominant because 5G base stations are typically $\leq 10$ km from the device. The **dominant** component becomes $\mathcal{L}_{\text{trans}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{queue}}$ — and to meet URLLC targets ($\mathcal{L} \leq 1$ ms), networks use *mini-slot scheduling* and *flexible numerology*.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given:** $P = 1 \text{ KB} = 8192$ bits; $B = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^6$ bps; $\mathcal{L}_{\text{prop}} = 5$ ms; $\mathcal{L}_{\text{queue}} = 2$ ms.

**Step 1 — Compute transmission delay (one-way):**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{trans}} = \frac{8192}{10^6} = 8.192 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute processing delay (assume negligible for edge server):** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{proc}} \approx 0.5$ ms (typical edge compute).

**Step 3 — One-way latency:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{one-way}} = 5 + 8.192 + 2 + 0.5 = 15.692 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Round-trip time (RTT):**

$$
\text{RTT} = 2 \times 15.692 = 31.384 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Compliance check:**

$$
\text{RTT} = 31.38 \text{ ms} \gt 20 \text{ ms target} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NON-COMPLIANT}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently forget to **double the one-way latency** to obtain RTT — losing 2 marks.
> 2. Do not omit the **processing delay** even if the problem does not state it explicitly; assume $\mathcal{L}_{\text{proc}} = 0.5$ ms as a default.
> 3. Always state the **compliance verdict** explicitly (“NON-COMPLIANT” or “MEETS TARGET”). KTU valuation keys award marks for this concluding sentence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Edge Computing (Module 4):**

- ✅ Edge computing places compute **at the network boundary**, not in a central cloud.
- ✅ The canonical CPS architecture is **three-tier: Device → Edge → Cloud**.
- ✅ The primary driver is **latency determinism** (sub-10 ms target), followed by **bandwidth, privacy, resilience, and energy efficiency**.
- ✅ The **end-to-end latency** decomposes into four additive components: propagation + transmission + queue + processing.
- ✅ The **data reduction ratio** $\rho = 1 - D_{\text{post}} / D_{\text{pre}}$ quantifies edge preprocessing efficiency; values $\geq 0.95$ are typical.
- ✅ The **offloading rule of thumb** is $W^* \approx D \cdot C_{\text{edge}} / B$ — offload only when workload $W \gg W^*$.
- ✅ Standard hardware tiers: Devices = sensors/PLCs; Edge = Jetson, industrial PCs; Cloud = hyperscale GPU/TPU clusters.
- ✅ Standard protocols: **MQTT, OPC-UA, Modbus TCP** (device-to-edge); **gRPC, mTLS, Kafka** (edge-to-cloud).
- ✅ **Edge vs. Cloud is not a competition** — they are complementary. Edge handles real-time, cloud handles scale.
- ✅ KTU frequently tests: definitions (3 marks), latency decomposition (7 marks), bandwidth calculations (7 marks), and three-tier diagrams (4–7 marks).
- ✅ **Always** end numerical answers with an explicit **compliance verdict** or **engineering interpretation sentence**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Based Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT604 - ADVANCED CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Based Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Based Learning (PBL)** is a learner-centered pedagogical methodology in which students acquire domain knowledge and **21st-century engineering competencies** by actively investigating, designing, prototyping, and solving real-world, ill-structured, open-ended problems — typically culminating in a functional cyber-physical artifact evaluated against a **published rubric** aligned to the KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (COs) and Program Outcomes (POs).

In the context of **PBCPT604 – Advanced Cyber Physical Systems**, PBL requires the student cohort to integrate knowledge of embedded processors (ARM/RISC-V), real-time operating systems (RTOS), sensor-actuator fusion, control theory, IoT communication protocols (MQTT, CoAP, BLE), and edge–cloud co-design into a **single working demonstrator** that exhibits measurable physical interaction with the environment through a closed cyber–physical feedback loop.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital ICU Monitor Team"**
> Imagine a group of doctors being trained not by reading textbooks about heart-rate monitors, but by being handed a real ICU patient, a parts bin (ECG pads, microcontroller, screen, alarm buzzer), and the instruction: *"Build me a system that will save lives."* They must learn anatomy (sensors), electronics (signal conditioning), programming (firmware), regulatory rules (IEC 62304), and teamwork — **all because the problem demands it**. PBL is exactly this: the **project becomes the teacher**, and the syllabus becomes the toolbox the student reaches into.

### 1.3 Core Pillars of PBL in KTU 2024 Scheme

| # | Pillar | KTU 2024 Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Driving Question** | Must be open-ended, ill-structured, industry/research relevant |
| 2 | **Authentic Artifact** | Working CPS prototype + documentation + research paper |
| 3 | **Sustained Inquiry** | Spans 12–14 weeks (full semester module) |
| 4 | **Public Product** | End-semester demo + viva + paper/publication |
| 5 | **Reflection & Iteration** | Logbook + weekly review meetings + retrospective reports |

### 1.4 Why KTU Mandates PBL in the 2024 Scheme

- **NEP 2020 Compliance** — emphasises experiential and competency-based learning.
- **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** — directly maps to higher-order Bloom levels: *Apply, Analyse, Evaluate, Create* (Levels 3–6 of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy).
- **Industry 4.0 Readiness** — CPS engineers must integrate heterogeneous skills (HW + SW + Control + Networking).
- **Employability & Innovation** — graduates enter startups, R&D labs, and product companies with portfolio evidence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bolded Standard Metric:** A typical KTU PBL module carries **2 credits**, equivalent to roughly **60 student-effort hours** per semester, with an assessment split of **Internal (50%)** and **External End-Semester (50%)** as per the 2024 regulations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations (Structured Logic)

Project Based Learning rests on three converging learning theories:

1. **Constructivism (Piaget, Vygotsky)** — knowledge is *constructed* by the learner, not transmitted. The CPS prototype is the medium of construction.
2. **Situated Cognition (Lave & Wenger)** — learning is most effective when situated in an authentic context (e.g., a smart irrigation system for a Kerala paddy field, not a textbook example).
3. **Cognitive Apprenticeship (Collins, Brown & Newman)** — students learn by working alongside a "master" (the faculty mentor) on real tasks, with modelling, scaffolding, and fading of support.

### 2.2 The Seven Core Elements of PBL (BIE / KTU Adoption)

- **Challenging Problem or Question** — the driving question.
- **Sustained Inquiry** — research and prototyping across weeks.
- **Authenticity** — the problem mirrors real CPS industry scenarios.
- **Student Voice & Choice** — team picks sub-problem, tools, and architecture.
- **Reflection** — written and oral retrospectives at every milestone.
- **Critique & Revision** — peer review and faculty critique cycles.
- **Public Product** — final demonstration to a panel.

### 2.3 KTU PBL Assessment Reference Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the KTU 2024 Scheme PBL regulations, evaluation is done by a panel of internal faculty and an external expert. The following weightages are normative and are mirrored from the official KTU PBL rubrics:

| Evaluation Criterion | Marks (Out of 100) | Cognitive Level (Bloom) | KTU Outcome Mapped |
|---|---|---|---|
| Problem Definition & Literature Survey | **10** | Understand / Analyse | CO1, CO2 |
| System Architecture & Design (HW + SW + Control) | **20** | Apply / Create | CO3, CO4 |
| Implementation & Prototyping (working CPS demo) | **30** | Create / Apply | CO4, CO5 |
| Testing, Validation & Performance Metrics | **15** | Evaluate | CO5 |
| Documentation, Logbook & Research Paper Draft | **10** | Analyse / Create | CO6 |
| End-Semester Demo + Viva-Voce (Public Product) | **15** | Create / Evaluate | CO6, PO10 |
| **TOTAL** | **100** | — | — |

> [!WARNING]
> The **end-semester external viva carries 15 marks** and is conducted by a panel. A non-functional prototype at demo time historically results in a **cap of 30/100** for the entire PBL component — this is a hard ceiling enforced by the KTU valuation key.

### 2.4 KTU 2024 PBL Lifecycle Phases — Reference Table

| Phase | Duration (Weeks) | Key Deliverable | Tools/Methods |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase 1 — Ideation & Scoping | 1–2 | Problem Statement + Stakeholder Map | Brainstorming, Literature Review (IEEE Xplore, Springer) |
| Phase 2 — System Design | 3–5 | Architecture Diagram + BOM + Gantt Chart | SysML, UML, Draw.io, Tinkercad |
| Phase 3 — Prototyping | 6–9 | Working Hardware + Firmware | KiCad, STM32CubeIDE, PlatformIO |
| Phase 4 — Integration & Testing | 10–11 | Validated CPS Demo + Test Report | Oscilloscope, Logic Analyzer, MQTT-Spy |
| Phase 5 — Showcase & Reflection | 12–14 | Final Report + Paper + Public Demo | LaTeX, Overleaf, GitHub |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industry 4.0**: Trains engineers for the smart-factory, IIoT, and digital-twin workforce.
- **Research Readiness**: Directly prepares students for **M.Tech / Ph.D** publication in CPS venues like *ACM TCPS*, *IEEE IoT Journal*, *EMSOFT*.
- **Startup Incubation**: Many KTU PBL projects have been incubated at **Kerala Startup Mission (KSUM)** and **Maker Village, Kochi**.
- **Patent Pipeline**: Working prototypes can be filed as Indian patents under the *IPR Cell* of the university.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step PBL Execution Walkthrough (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> This section treats the **PBL execution as a deterministic engineering workflow**. Every step is enumerated so a student team can follow it without ambiguity, in line with the KTU 2024 PBL handbook expectations.

### 3.1 Step 1 — Problem Scoping and Domain Selection

**Input:** Course syllabus + faculty research interests + industry need.
**Action:** The team must converge on an **ill-structured problem statement** that:
- touches at least **one physical process** (sensed or actuated),
- requires **real-time computation**,
- involves **at least one communication protocol**,
- has a measurable **cyber-physical feedback loop**.

**CPS-Valid Example Problems (allowed under PBCPT604):**
- Smart precision agriculture controller for Kerala paddy fields (soil moisture + pH sensor → STM32 → LoRaWAN → cloud dashboard → solenoid valve actuation).
- Wearable fall-detection CPS for elderly patients (IMU + edge ML inference → BLE alert → caregiver mobile app).
- Autonomous line-following AGV with ROS 2 + digital twin.
- Smart energy meter with tamper detection (CT/PT sensor → ESP32 → MQTT → ThingsBoard → utility dashboard).

**Output:** A one-page **Problem Statement (PS)** document with: Title, Background, Objectives, Scope, Out-of-Scope, Stakeholders.

### 3.2 Step 2 — Literature Survey

**Action:** Each team member surveys **at least 8–10 peer-reviewed papers** from the last five years. Mandatory reading list should include:
- Survey papers (e.g., *"A Survey on Cyber-Physical Systems"*, IEEE IoT Journal).
- Foundational CPS architecture papers (Lee & Seshia, *Introduction to Embedded Systems: A Cyber-Physical Systems Approach*).
- Benchmark datasets / benchmarks (e.g., **NASA Turbofan dataset** for predictive maintenance).

**Tools:** Google Scholar, IEEE Xplore, Scopus, Connected Papers, Research Rabbit.

**Output:** A **Comparative Analysis Table** with columns: Author, Year, Method, Sensors, MCU, Protocol, Limitation, How our work differs.

### 3.3 Step 3 — System Architecture Design

**Action:** The team designs the CPS as **three orthogonal layers**:

$$
\text{CPS} \;=\; \mathcal{L}_{\text{perception}} \;\cup\; \mathcal{L}_{\text{computation}} \;\cup\; \mathcal{L}_{\text{actuation}} \;\cup\; \mathcal{L}_{\text{network}}
$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{L}_{\text{perception}}$ = sensors (e.g., DHT22, MPU6050, soil-moisture probes).
- $\mathcal{L}_{\text{computation}}$ = MCU/SoC with RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS on ESP32, Zephyr on nRF52840).
- $\mathcal{L}_{\text{actuation}}$ = motors, relays, solenoids, LED drivers.
- $\mathcal{L}_{\text{network}}$ = MQTT / CoAP / BLE / LoRaWAN / Wi-Fi / 5G.

**CPS Design Constraints (must be explicitly listed in the report):**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Real-Time Constraint:} \quad & T_{\text{task}} \;\le\; T_{\text{deadline}} \\[4pt]
\text{Energy Constraint:} \quad & P_{\text{avg}} \;\le\; P_{\text{budget}} \\[4pt]
\text{Safety Constraint:} \quad & \forall\, t,\; \text{state}(t) \;\in\; \mathcal{S}_{\text{safe}} \\[4pt]
\text{Communication Constraint:} \quad & \text{latency}_{\text{end-to-end}} \;\le\; L_{\max}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Output:** Block diagram, sequence diagram, data-flow diagram, BOM with cost, Gantt chart.

### 3.4 Step 4 — Hardware Prototyping

**Action:** The team builds the physical artifact using a breadboard first, then a perfboard/PCB. Mandatory deliverables:
- Schematic (KiCad / EasyEDA).
- PCB layout (Gerber files).
- 3D-printed or laser-cut enclosure (Fusion 360 / OpenSCAD).
- Soldered, powered-up board with all peripherals wired.

**Validation:** The board must be shown to perform **at least one physical action** in response to **at least one sensor input** during the mid-evaluation review.

### 3.5 Step 5 — Firmware and Software Stack

**Action:** The team implements the cyber layer. Mandatory components:
- Device drivers (HAL or Zephyr drivers).
- RTOS task scheduling (rate-monotonic or earliest-deadline-first).
- Communication client (MQTT, CoAP, BLE GATT).
- Edge intelligence (TinyML, if applicable — e.g., TensorFlow Lite Micro).
- Cloud backend (Node-RED / ThingsBoard / AWS IoT Core).
- Dashboard / mobile app (optional but scored higher).

**Real-Time Verification Pseudocode (for the writeup):**

$$
\text{Schedule}(t) \;=\; \text{EDF}\!\left( \bigl\{ (C_i,\, D_i,\, T_i) \;\big\vert\; i=1\dots n \bigr\} \right)
$$

where $C_i$ = computation time, $D_i$ = deadline, $T_i$ = period of task $i$. The team must show, by trace or oscilloscope, that every task instance completes before its deadline under worst-case load.

### 3.6 Step 6 — Integration, Testing and Validation

**Action:** The full cyber-physical loop is closed. The team must demonstrate:
- **Functional tests** — each use case passes.
- **Performance tests** — latency, throughput, jitter, energy.
- **Stress tests** — continuous run for **at least 30 minutes** without crash.
- **Fault-injection tests** — sensor disconnected, network dropped, power sagged.

**Sample Metric Table (must appear in the report):**

| Metric | Target | Measured | Pass/Fail |
|---|---|---|---|
| End-to-end loop latency | $\le 100$ ms | 78 ms | Pass |
| Sensor sampling rate | $100$ Hz | $100.2$ Hz | Pass |
| Power consumption (avg) | $\le 1.5$ W | $1.31$ W | Pass |
| MQTT packet loss | $\le 1\%$ | $0.4\%$ | Pass |
| Continuous run | $\ge 30$ min | 45 min | Pass |

### 3.7 Step 7 — Public Product, Reflection and Iteration

**Action:** The team delivers a **public product**:
- Live end-semester demo to external panel.
- Conference-style paper draft (IEEE two-column, 6 pages).
- GitHub repository with reproducible firmware and CAD files.
- 5-minute recorded video of the working CPS.
- Reflection log: what worked, what failed, what would be done differently — explicitly linking back to **Bloom's higher-order levels**.

**Output:** All artefacts uploaded to the KTU PBL portal / institutional LMS.
