## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme PBCPT604 End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern. PBL is typically assessed through the **continuous evaluation viva** and one theory question per module. The 14-mark model below is a typical Part-B question that examiners set to test the student's PBL understanding.

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 PBL Module]*

Define **Project Based Learning (PBL)** as per the KTU 2024 Scheme. List any **three distinguishing features** that differentiate PBL from traditional lecture-based learning.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** PBL is a student-centered pedagogy where learners gain knowledge and skills by working for an extended period to investigate and respond to an authentic, ill-structured, real-world problem, culminating in a publicly demonstrated product.
- **Distinguishing Feature 1 (1 mark):** Learning is **driven by the problem**, not by the syllabus sequence — students decide *what* they need to learn.
- **Distinguishing Feature 2 (1/2 mark):** The outcome is a **working artefact**, not just a written exam answer.
- **Distinguishing Feature 3 (1/2 mark):** Assessment is **rubric-based and public**, with peer and external evaluation.

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

State any **three benefits** of integrating **Project Based Learning** in a Cyber-Physical Systems curriculum such as PBCPT604.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Integration of heterogeneous skills** — students combine embedded programming, control theory, sensor interfacing, and IoT networking in one project.
2. **Higher-order Bloom attainment** — naturally reaches the *Create* and *Evaluate* levels which lectures rarely reach.
3. **Industry readiness and portfolio building** — produces demonstrable artefacts usable for placements, patents, and M.Tech admissions.
4. **Teamwork and communication skills** — directly maps to PO9 (Individual and Team Work) and PO10 (Communication).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> The ESE 2024 KTU pattern for PBL topics in PBCPT604 uses an internal choice. **Choose either Question A or Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Full PBL Project Planning

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Mock Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** You are the team lead for a PBL project titled *"IoT-Enabled Smart Irrigation Controller for Kerala Paddy Fields."* Apply the KTU PBL framework to:
1. Frame a **driving question** for the project.
2. Identify the **stakeholders**.
3. Prepare a **work breakdown structure (WBS)** with at least **six** distinct work packages.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the same project:
1. Draw the **three-layer CPS architecture** (perception, computation, actuation + network).
2. State **two real-time constraints** and **two energy constraints** that the system must satisfy.
3. Define the **public product** deliverables expected at the end-semester evaluation.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Part (1) — Driving Question (2 marks):**
> *"How can a low-cost, solar-powered, LoRa-enabled cyber-physical system automatically regulate irrigation water in a Kerala paddy field, maintaining soil moisture within the agronomically optimal range of **60–80% VWC**, while transmitting real-time field data to a farmer-accessible mobile dashboard?"*

**(a) Part (2) — Stakeholders (2 marks):**
- **Primary:** Paddy farmers (Kuttanad / Kole land regions of Kerala).
- **Secondary:** Kerala State Electricity Board (for solar deployment), Department of Agriculture, KSUM (incubation), KAU (agronomy inputs).
- **Tertiary:** End-semester evaluation panel, KTU PBL coordinators.

**(a) Part (3) — WBS with Six Work Packages (3 marks):**

| WBS Code | Work Package | Owner Role | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| WP1 | Problem scoping + literature survey | Documentation Lead | 2 weeks |
| WP2 | Soil-moisture & pH sensor characterisation | Hardware Lead | 1.5 weeks |
| WP3 | LoRaWAN + MQTT gateway implementation | Software Lead | 2 weeks |
| WP4 | RTOS-based irrigation control firmware on ESP32 | Software Lead | 2.5 weeks |
| WP5 | Solenoid-valve + solar-battery power stage | Hardware Lead | 1.5 weeks |
| WP6 | Mobile dashboard + field deployment + report | Control & Doc Lead | 2.5 weeks |

**(b) Part (1) — Three-Layer CPS Architecture (3 marks):**

| Layer | Component | Example in Project |
|---|---|---|
| Perception | Soil-moisture, pH, temperature, rain sensor | Capacitive v1.2 soil sensor, DHT22 |
| Computation | MCU + RTOS | ESP32-WROOM-32 running FreeRTOS |
| Actuation | Solenoid valve, pump relay, LED indicator | 12V DC latching solenoid via MOSFET driver |
| Network | LoRaWAN uplink + Wi-Fi gateway + MQTT broker | Dragino LPS8 gateway, HiveMQ cloud broker |

**(b) Part (2) — Constraints (2 marks):**
- *Real-time:* The soil-moisture control loop must complete within **$T_{\text{deadline}} = 1\,\text{s}$**; the telemetry uplink can tolerate a **$60\,\text{s}$** latency.
- *Energy:* The solar-battery system must sustain **$\ge 72$ hours of autonomy** with no sun; average power **$\le 0.8\,\text{W}$**.

**(b) Part (3) — Public Product Deliverables (2 marks):**
- Working field-deployed prototype with 30-minute live demo.
- 6-page IEEE-format paper draft.
- GitHub repo with BOM, KiCad files, firmware.
- 5-minute demo video.
- Reflection logbook signed by faculty mentor.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — PBL Assessment and Reflection

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **seven core elements of PBL** as adopted by KTU. For each element, give a **one-sentence example** from a Cyber-Physical Systems project of your choice.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **KTU 2024 Scheme PBL assessment rubric** in detail. What happens if a team's prototype is **non-functional** at the end-semester demo? Justify your answer using the published evaluation policy.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Seven Core Elements of PBL (1 mark each = 7 marks):**

| # | Element | One-Sentence CPS Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Challenging Problem or Driving Question | *"Can a wearable IMU + edge ML detect elderly falls in under 500 ms with a false-alarm rate below 1%?"* |
| 2 | Sustained Inquiry | The team investigates IMU placement, sensor fusion algorithms, and TinyML model architectures over 10 weeks. |
| 3 | Authenticity | The fall detector is tested on actual elderly subjects (with IRB clearance) and benchmarked against the SisFall dataset. |
| 4 | Student Voice & Choice | The team selects between Edge Impulse and TensorFlow Lite Micro for the inference engine. |
| 5 | Reflection | Weekly logbook entries reflect on missed deadlines, sensor noise issues, and team coordination friction. |
| 6 | Critique & Revision | Faculty mentor critiques the BLE stack; team revises the GATT profile to reduce air-time. |
| 7 | Public Product | End-semester live demo + YouTube video + IEEE-format paper submission. |

**(b) KTU 2024 PBL Assessment Rubric Discussion (7 marks):**

The KTU 2024 PBL rubric evaluates the project on a **100-mark scale** distributed across **six criteria**:
- Problem Definition & Literature Survey — **10 marks**
- System Architecture & Design — **20 marks**
- Implementation & Prototyping — **30 marks**
- Testing & Validation — **15 marks**
- Documentation & Logbook — **10 marks**
- End-Semester Demo + Viva-Voce — **15 marks**

Each criterion has a published **Bloom's cognitive-level descriptor** (Apply, Analyse, Evaluate, Create) and a **Course Outcome (CO) mapping** to PBCPT604 outcomes CO1–CO6.

**Non-Functional Prototype Policy (3 marks for this sub-part):**
- Per the KTU PBL valuation key, a non-functional prototype at the public demo is treated as a **failure of the central deliverable** (Implementation & Prototyping carries the heaviest weight of 30 marks).
- The Implementation & Prototyping score is **capped at 10/30** (i.e., marks awarded only for partial circuit assembly, code upload, and design intent).
- The total PBL score is **capped at 30/100**, regardless of the quality of documentation or literature survey.
- The team must **re-register** the PBL in the next semester to redeem the grade, as per the KTU 2024 academic regulations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Missing a working closed-loop demo** — the most common cause of the 30/100 hard cap. Always carry a *backup offline dataset* and a *recorded demo video* on your phone.
> 2. **No link to Bloom's higher-order levels** in the report — examiners explicitly look for verbs like *"analysed", "designed", "evaluated"* in the conclusion. Generic phrasing loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Missing BOM cost or Gantt chart** — design rubric is 20 marks, and a missing Gantt chart alone costs 3 marks.
> 4. **Improper citation in the paper draft** — IEEE format violations cost 2–4 marks in the documentation criterion.
> 5. **Team member role ambiguity in the viva** — every team member must be able to explain **every block**; silence on a question asked to a specific member is a team-wide penalty of 1–2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **PBL = problem-driven + artefact-driven + public-product-driven** learning.
> - KTU 2024 mandates PBL for **all 2024 scheme B.Tech branches** as a vehicle for NEP 2020 experiential learning.
> - The CPS project **must** close a **physical–cyber feedback loop** — sensing, computation, actuation, and communication are non-negotiable.
> - PBL carries **2 credits** in PBCPT604 and a **100-mark** continuous + end-semester assessment.
> - Total marks split: **10 + 20 + 30 + 15 + 10 + 15** = **100**.
> - **A non-functional prototype ⇒ hard cap of 30/100**. This is the single most important rule.
> - Mandatory deliverables: working demo, IEEE paper draft, GitHub repo, logbook, Gantt chart, BOM, 5-minute video.
> - Real-time constraints ($T_{\text{task}} \le T_{\text{deadline}}$) and energy constraints ($P_{\text{avg}} \le P_{\text{budget}}$) **must be stated explicitly** in the report.
> - Bloom's levels targeted by PBL are **Apply, Analyse, Evaluate, Create** (Levels 3–6).
> - Mapping to POs: PO5 (Modern Tool Usage), PO9 (Team Work), PO10 (Communication), PO12 (Life-long Learning) are the most directly evidenced.
> - PBL outputs often lead to **KSUM incubation, Indian patents, and IEEE/ACM publications** — leverage this.
